description: Capitalizing a significant repair that increases the output or life of a machine.
explanation: Minor repairs are expensed. However, a major rebuild that makes a machine 'better than new' is capitalized and depreciated over the new remaining life.

title: Impairment of Specialized Tooling
description: Writing off the value of molds or tools because the specific product they make has been discontinued.
explanation: If a tool can only make 'Product X' and you stop selling 'Product X,' that tool is worthless regardless of its physical condition. This is a permanent loss.

title: Tolling Arrangement Revenue (Service Only)
description: Recording revenue when the factory processes raw materials owned by a customer.
explanation: In a 'Tolling' deal, the factory doesn't buy raw materials. It just sells its labor and machine time. No COGS (Inventory) is recorded, only the operating costs of the factory.

title: Purchase of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
description: Recording the purchase of safety gear (helmets, gloves, goggles) for factory workers.
explanation: Safety gear is an indirect production cost (Overhead). It is required for the factory to operate safely.

title: Product Prototype Development (Expensing Phase)
description: Recording materials and labor used to create an initial prototype before mass production.
explanation: Unless specific capitalization criteria are met, the cost of 'testing and trying' new designs is expensed immediately as R&D.

title: Recognizing Byproduct Inventory at Harvest
description: Recording the value of a byproduct when it is produced, reducing the cost of the main product.
explanation: Instead of waiting to sell the byproduct, some factories recognize its value immediately to lower the 'net cost' of the main items they are making.

title: Outsourced Manufacturing (Contract Manufacturer)
description: Recording the cost when an external factory (CMO) produces the product for you.
explanation: Even if you don't make it, you own the materials. When the external factory finishes, you convert your raw materials plus their service fee into Finished Goods.

title: Year-End Variance Proration
description: Allocating variances across WIP, Finished Goods, and COGS rather than just dumping them in COGS.
explanation: If variances are 'material,' auditors require you to spread the extra cost across all inventory that was touched by those costs, not just the items already sold.

title: Inter-Plant WIP Transfer
description: Moving semi-finished goods from one company factory to another for final processing.
explanation: This tracks the physical location of value within the company's internal supply chain.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) Accrual
description: Recording the future cost of safely dismantling heavy machinery at the end of its life.
explanation: If a specialized machine requires a $2,000 clean-up cost in 10 years, you must capitalize that cost today and depreciate it over the machine's life.

title: Vendor Managed Inventory (Consignment Buy)
description: Recording the purchase of raw materials that were already sitting in your warehouse but owned by the vendor.
explanation: In VMI, you only 'buy' the material and record the liability the moment you pull the item from the shelf to use it in production.

title: Environmental Penalty (Non-Deductible)
description: Recording a government fine for a factory emission violation.
explanation: While this is a factory expense, it is usually disallowed for tax purposes. It is a 'Period Cost' and never part of the product cost.

title: Provision for Product Recall
description: Estimating and recording the cost of a future product recall based on a known defect.
explanation: Following the Prudence principle, if you know a batch is bad, you must record the estimated cost of retrieving and fixing it immediately.

title: Government Energy Efficiency Grant
description: Recording a grant received for installing solar panels or high-efficiency motors in the factory.
explanation: Grants for assets are recognized as revenue over the same period the asset is depreciated, to match the benefit to the cost.

title: Unrealized Intercompany Profit Elimination
description: Eliminating the 'markup' added when the manufacturing branch sells inventory to the retail branch of the same company.
explanation: If the factory sells a unit to its own store for $10 profit, that profit isn't 'real' until the store sells it to a real customer. This entry 'cleans' the inventory value for consolidation.

title: Royalty for Patented Production Process
description: Recording a fee paid to a 3rd party for every unit produced using their technology.
explanation: Licensing fees for a production method are part of the 'Indirect Product Cost' and flow through overhead into the unit cost.

title: Physical Count Adjustment (Shortage)
description: Adjusting the digital system to match the physical count in the warehouse.
explanation: The 'Moment of Truth' where errors, theft, or evaporation are recorded as a loss to ensure the Balance Sheet is accurate.

title: Machine Installation & Testing Costs
description: Capitalizing the technical labor and materials used to set up a new production line.
explanation: Under IAS 16, all costs to bring an asset to 'working condition for its intended use' must be added to the asset value.

title: New Machine Training (Expensing)
description: Recording the cost of training staff to use a new robotic arm.
explanation: Crucial distinction: You capitalize the machine setup, but you generally MUST expense the staff training. Knowledge cannot be 'owned' as a tangible asset.

title: Excise Duty on Production
description: Recording the tax owed to the government for producing specific goods (e.g., tobacco, fuel, luxury items).
explanation: In many countries, tax is triggered at the 'Moment of Manufacture,' not the moment of sale. This tax becomes a part of the product's cost.

title: Sale of Excess Carbon Credits
description: Recording revenue when a clean factory sells its unused pollution permits.
explanation: If a factory operates below its emission limit, it can monetize its efficiency. This is a non-core revenue stream.

title: Capitalizing Development Costs (Success)
description: Moving costs from the P&L to the Balance Sheet once a new product becomes commercially viable.
explanation: Under IFRS (IAS 38), once a product reaches 'Technical Feasibility,' you stop expensing and start capitalizing.

title: Night Shift Premium (Labor Cost)
description: Recording the extra wages paid to workers on the 2nd or 3rd shift.
explanation: Overtime and shift premiums paid to direct laborers are part of the 'Direct Labor' component of product cost.

title: Closing Over-applied Overhead
description: Adjusting COGS when the factory was more efficient than the standard estimate.
explanation: The final closing entry to zero out the temporary overhead accounts and reduce COGS for the 'savings' achieved.

title: Dine-In Cash Sale
description: Recording a standard table sale paid in cash at the end of the meal.
explanation: The full amount received includes 16% VAT (Jordan). Revenue is only the pre-tax amount. The tax collected belongs to the ISTD and is a liability until remitted.

title: Dine-In Credit/Debit Card Sale
description: Recording a table sale paid by card, with a POS processing fee deducted by the bank.
explanation: Always record the full sale price as revenue. The bank processing fee (typically 2.5%) is a separate selling expense, not a reduction of revenue.

title: Takeaway / To-Go Order (Cash)
description: Recording a customer who orders and pays at the counter for food to take away.
explanation: Takeaway revenue is recorded at the point of handover to the customer. VAT applies the same way as dine-in sales.

title: Split Bill (Part Cash, Part Card)
description: Recording a table where two guests split the bill — one pays cash, the other pays by card.
explanation: Split payments require you to record each payment method separately while keeping the total revenue and VAT intact as one transaction.

title: POS Void / Cancelled Order
description: Reversing a sale that was incorrectly entered into the POS system before payment was collected.
explanation: A void cancels a transaction as if it never happened. It is a direct reversal of the original entry. This is different from a refund, which happens after payment has been collected.

title: Service Charge Collected from Customer
description: Recording the 10% service charge added to the bill that belongs to the staff.
explanation: Service charges are NOT restaurant revenue. They are collected on behalf of the staff and held as a liability until distributed via payroll.

title: Service Charge Distribution to Staff
description: Paying out the accumulated monthly service charge pool to waitstaff and kitchen team.
explanation: This clears the liability created every time a service charge was collected. The distribution can be cash or added to the payroll run.

title: Cash Tips Left on Table
description: Recording cash tips left by customers directly on the table for the waiting staff.
explanation: Tips belong to the staff, not the restaurant. The cash is held briefly as a liability until handed to the team at the end of the shift.

title: Card Tips Added to POS Bill
description: Recording a tip the customer adds to their card payment at the end of the meal.
explanation: Card tips come in net of the bank's processing fee. The restaurant absorbs the fee but must still pass the full $10 tip to the staff.

title: Customer Refund (Cash)
description: Issuing a cash refund to a dissatisfied customer for a meal that was returned.
explanation: Using Sales Returns (not a direct debit to Revenue) lets you track the volume of refunds as a KPI. VAT must also be reversed since no taxable supply ultimately occurred.

title: Manager Comp / Complimentary Meal
description: A manager approves a free meal for a VIP guest or to resolve a complaint.
explanation: You do NOT record the menu price as a sale. You move the cost of the raw ingredients out of inventory and into a marketing/guest relations expense account.

title: Customer Loyalty Points Earned (Revenue Deferral)
description: Deferring a portion of a sale's value for loyalty points earned by the customer at the time of purchase.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, if points have a standalone value, you cannot recognize the full revenue at time of sale. The points portion is deferred as a liability until redeemed or expired.

title: Customer Loyalty Points Redeemed
description: A customer uses accumulated points to pay for part of their meal.
explanation: When points are redeemed, the deferred liability is converted to earned revenue. The remaining balance is collected in cash.

title: Coupon / Voucher Redemption
description: A customer presents a printed or digital coupon for a discount on their meal.
explanation: Recording the full sale and a separate discount lets you analyze exactly how much revenue was given away in promotions without distorting your gross revenue figure.

title: Staff Meal (50% Discount Policy)
description: An employee eats at the restaurant and pays half price per the staff discount policy.
explanation: The cash portion is standard revenue. The discounted 50% is an employee benefit expense — the restaurant absorbs this cost. No VAT is charged on staff meals in most Jordan interpretations.

title: Catering Order Advance Deposit
description: Receiving a deposit from a corporate client for a large catering event next month.
explanation: The food has not been prepared yet, so this is a liability. Revenue is only recognized when the catering event is completed and the food is delivered.

title: Catering Event Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue when the catering order is delivered and the event takes place.
explanation: The deposit is cleared, the remaining balance is billed as a receivable, and the full revenue (net of VAT) is recognized on the event date.

title: Sale of Restaurant Gift Card
description: A customer purchases a prepaid gift card to give as a present.
explanation: A gift card is not a sale. It is a liability because the restaurant owes the holder food worth JOD 50. Revenue is only recognized when the card is spent at the counter.

title: Gift Card Redemption
description: A customer uses a gift card to pay for their meal.
explanation: When the card is used, the liability converts to earned revenue. VAT is recognized at the point of use, not at the point of sale of the card.

title: Gift Card Breakage (Unredeemed Balance)
description: Recognizing revenue from gift card balances that are statistically unlikely to ever be redeemed.
explanation: Using historical data, if 5% of gift cards are never redeemed, you can recognize that portion as Other Income over time. This is called 'breakage' under IFRS 15.

title: Purchase of Raw Ingredients (Cash)
description: Buying fresh vegetables, meat, and dairy from a market vendor for cash.
explanation: Raw ingredients are assets until used in cooking. They only become an expense (Cost of Sales) when they are consumed to produce a meal that is sold.

title: Purchase of Raw Ingredients (On Credit)
description: Receiving a weekly delivery of ingredients from a supplier with a 30-day payment term.
explanation: Purchasing on credit creates a liability. The inventory is an asset the moment it arrives at the restaurant, regardless of when you pay for it.

title: Purchase of Beverages / Alcohol
description: Buying soft drinks, juices, and beverages from a distributor.
explanation: Beverage inventory is tracked separately from food to help calculate the Beverage Cost % (a key F&B KPI). VAT on the purchase is a recoverable input tax.

title: Inbound Freight on Ingredient Delivery
description: Paying a delivery fee for ingredients to be brought from a wholesaler to the restaurant.
explanation: Under IAS 2, all costs required to bring inventory to its present location must be capitalized into the inventory value — including delivery fees.

title: Return of Spoiled Goods to Supplier
description: Sending back a delivery of tomatoes that arrived damaged, and receiving a credit note.
explanation: The credit note reduces what you owe the supplier and removes the damaged goods from your inventory balance. No expense is recorded.

title: Early Payment Discount from Supplier
description: Paying a supplier invoice early and receiving a 2% cash discount.
explanation: The discount reduces the effective cost of the inventory. In a perpetual system, the saving is credited back to inventory rather than being recorded as income.

title: Daily Food Cost (Inventory to COGS)
description: Recording the cost of ingredients consumed to produce the day's meals sold.
explanation: This is done daily or weekly based on actual kitchen consumption. Food Cost % = COGS / Food Revenue. A typical restaurant targets 28-35%.

title: Daily Beverage Cost (Inventory to COGS)
description: Recording the cost of beverages consumed during the day's service.
explanation: Beverage COGS is tracked separately from food COGS so the Beverage Cost % can be monitored independently — usually targeting 20-25%.

title: Packaging & Disposables Expense
description: Recording the cost of takeaway boxes, bags, napkins, and cups used during the week.
explanation: Packaging costs are a direct cost of delivery and takeaway sales. They are expensed as consumed rather than being tracked as detailed COGS.

title: Physical Inventory Count Adjustment (Shortage)
description: Adjusting the books when the physical stock count is lower than the system balance at month-end.
explanation: Common causes are over-portioning, staff theft, or counting errors. This entry ensures the balance sheet reflects what is physically in the kitchen.

title: Kitchen Gas (LPG) Accrual
description: Recording the estimated cost of cooking gas used in the kitchen before the bill arrives.
explanation: Gas is often billed monthly or when the cylinder is replaced. Accruing it monthly ensures the P&L reflects the true cost of operating the kitchen each period.

title: Abnormal Food Spoilage (Power Cut / Fridge Failure)
description: Writing off a large batch of inventory lost due to an unexpected refrigeration failure.
explanation: Unlike normal spoilage (which is built into food cost), abnormal losses from avoidable events are recorded separately as a loss so management can identify and address the cause.

title: Monthly Payroll (Gross Salaries)
description: Recording the total payroll expense for all restaurant staff at month-end.
explanation: The gross payroll is expensed in the month it is earned. The net amount paid to staff, after deductions for SSC and income tax, is a separate entry when cash leaves the bank.

title: Social Security Contribution (Jordan) — Restaurant
description: Recording the employer's SSC share and the employee's deduction for restaurant staff.
explanation: In Jordan, the employer pays 14.25% and the employee contributes 7.5% of gross salary. Both are remitted to the Social Security Corporation (SSC) monthly.

title: Net Salary Payment to Staff
description: Paying the take-home salary to employees after all statutory deductions.
explanation: This clears the liability for net pay. The SSC and income tax deductions are remitted to the authorities in separate entries.

title: Employee Income Tax Withholding (Jordan)
description: Deducting income tax from a senior restaurant manager's monthly salary.
explanation: For staff whose income exceeds the tax-exempt threshold, the employer is legally required to withhold and remit income tax monthly to the ISTD.

title: Overtime Accrual (Weekend Shifts)
description: Recording extra hours worked by kitchen and floor staff during a busy weekend.
explanation: Overtime must be matched to the period in which it was worked. If a peak weekend falls at month-end, the expense belongs in that month even if paid next week.

title: Monthly EOSB Provision — Restaurant Staff
description: Accruing the end-of-service gratuity liability for restaurant employees as required by Jordanian labor law.
explanation: Jordanian labor law requires a gratuity payment when an employee leaves. Accruing monthly prevents a large unexpected expense when a long-serving chef or manager resigns.

title: EOSB Payout — Departing Employee
description: Paying the gratuity to a waiter who has resigned after 3 years of service.
explanation: This clears the provision built up over the employee's tenure. No new expense is recorded because the cost was already recognized monthly.

title: Salary Advance to Employee
description: A waiter requests an advance of JOD 200 against next month's salary.
explanation: An advance is a company asset (receivable), not an expense. It will be deducted from the employee's next payroll run.

title: Salary Advance Deduction from Payroll
description: Recovering a previously issued salary advance by deducting it from the monthly payroll.
explanation: This clears the advance from the balance sheet. The employee receives a lower net pay this month since the advance has already been spent.

title: Ramadan Season Bonus Accrual
description: Accruing performance bonuses for kitchen and floor staff at the end of Ramadan season.
explanation: Bonuses must be matched to the period of performance. If the bonus is for Ramadan's peak, it belongs in that month's P&L even if paid after Eid.

title: Annual Rent Payment (Prepaid)
description: Paying one year of rent upfront to the landlord at the start of the lease.
explanation: Common in Jordan where landlords often demand 6–12 months upfront. The full payment is an asset; JOD 1,000 moves to Rent Expense each month.

title: Monthly Rent Expense Recognition
description: Moving one month of rent from the prepaid asset to the expense account.
explanation: Each month this entry is made to ensure the P&L shows the correct occupancy cost regardless of when the cash was paid.

title: Rent Payment with Withholding Tax (Jordan)
description: Paying monthly rent to an individual landlord and withholding 5% tax as required by Jordanian law.
explanation: When a company rents from an individual (not a company), Jordanian law requires withholding 5% of the gross rent and paying it to the ISTD on behalf of the landlord.

title: Monthly Electricity & Water Accrual
description: Estimating and recording the utility costs for the month before the bills arrive.
explanation: Restaurants are heavy utility users. Accruing these costs monthly ensures the P&L shows the real cost of running the kitchen in each period.

title: Internet & POS Software Monthly Fee
description: Recording the monthly subscription for internet connection and the cloud-based POS system.
explanation: POS software and internet are essential operating costs for a restaurant. They are expensed in the month they are used.

title: Security Deposit Paid to Landlord
description: Paying a refundable deposit at the start of the restaurant lease.
explanation: Deposits are not expenses. They are assets because the money will be returned at the end of the lease, assuming the premises are in good condition.

title: Leasehold Improvements (Fit-Out)
description: Capitalizing the cost of tiling, kitchen ventilation, and interior fit-out of a rented restaurant space.
explanation: Permanent improvements to a rented space are capitalized as an asset and depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or the improvement's useful life.

title: Monthly Depreciation — Leasehold Improvements
description: Expensing one month's depreciation on the restaurant fit-out.
explanation: A JOD 25,000 fit-out over a 5-year lease is depreciated at JOD 417/month. This reflects the true monthly cost of the physical restaurant setup.

title: Kitchen Equipment Purchase (Oven / Grill)
description: Buying a commercial convection oven for the kitchen.
explanation: Equipment with a useful life over one year is capitalized as a fixed asset, not expensed immediately. It will be depreciated over its expected life (typically 5–10 years).

title: Monthly Kitchen Equipment Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation charge on ovens, fridges, and other kitchen assets.
explanation: A JOD 5,000 oven depreciated over 5 years (straight-line) produces JOD 250/month of depreciation expense on the P&L.

title: Equipment Maintenance & Repair
description: Paying a technician to service the commercial refrigerator.
explanation: Routine maintenance and minor repairs are expensed immediately. Only a major overhaul that extends the asset's useful life would be capitalized.

title: Prepaid Social Media Ad Campaign
description: Paying JOD 900 upfront for a 3-month Instagram and Facebook campaign.
explanation: Advertising covering multiple future months is a prepaid asset. JOD 300 moves to Advertising Expense each month as the campaign runs.

title: Monthly Advertising Expense Recognition
description: Moving one month of the prepaid ad campaign from asset to expense.
explanation: This ensures each month's P&L reflects the actual marketing spend for that period, regardless of when the cash was paid.

title: Food Blogger / Influencer Meal (Barter)
description: Hosting an influencer for a free meal in exchange for a review post on their social channels.
explanation: Record the cost of the raw ingredients (not the menu price) as a marketing expense. No revenue is recorded since no cash was exchanged.

title: VAT Output Tax Filing Preparation
description: Summarizing the total VAT collected from food sales during the month for ISTD filing.
explanation: At the end of the VAT period, Output VAT (collected from customers) is netted against Input VAT (paid on purchases). Only the difference is remitted to the ISTD.

title: VAT Remittance to ISTD (Jordan)
description: Paying the net VAT liability to the Income & Sales Tax Department.
explanation: This is the final step in the VAT cycle — transferring the net tax collected on behalf of the government to the ISTD by the filing deadline.

title: VAT Input Tax on Ingredient Purchase
description: Recording the recoverable 16% VAT paid on a food supply invoice from a registered vendor.
explanation: The VAT paid to a VAT-registered supplier is a recoverable asset. It is deducted from the VAT you collected from customers when filing the monthly return.

title: VAT Reverse Charge on Google/Meta Ads
description: Self-assessing VAT on a digital advertising invoice from a foreign provider.
explanation: When a Jordanian restaurant buys services from abroad (Google, Meta), it must self-assess the VAT. The Input and Output entries usually net to zero but both must appear on the tax return.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the estimated income tax on the restaurant's annual profit.
explanation: Income tax is accrued at year-end based on taxable profit. The actual payment to the ISTD may happen months later but the expense belongs to the current fiscal year.

title: Annual Municipality & Health License Fee
description: Paying the annual operating license and food safety permit fees.
explanation: Annual licenses cover the full year and are recorded as prepaid assets, then amortized at JOD 50/month into License & Permit Expense.

title: Health Inspection Fine (Non-Deductible)
description: Recording a penalty issued by the municipality for a food safety violation.
explanation: Government fines are recorded as an expense for accounting purposes but are typically non-deductible for income tax. This means the cost cannot be used to reduce taxable profit.

title: Petty Cash Float — Kitchen & Floor
description: Setting up a small cash float for daily minor purchases like spices and small supplies.
explanation: A petty cash float allows kitchen staff to make small urgent purchases without going through the bank. The balance stays fixed at JOD 150 unless deliberately changed.

title: Petty Cash Replenishment
description: Replenishing the kitchen petty cash float after it has been spent on small daily items.
explanation: You do NOT credit Petty Cash when replenishing. You debit the specific expenses and credit the main bank account to restore the float to its full balance.

title: End-of-Day Cash Drawer Shortage
description: Recording a discrepancy when the physical cash in the till is less than the POS daily sales report.
explanation: Small shortages are common in cash-heavy businesses and are expensed immediately. Large or recurring shortages should trigger an internal investigation.

title: End-of-Day Cash Drawer Overage
description: Recording extra cash found in the till at the end of the day.
explanation: An overage means a customer was under-changed or a payment was double-collected. The extra amount is recorded as Other Income.

title: Bank Reconciliation — Interest Earned
description: Adjusting the general ledger for interest income shown on the monthly bank statement.
explanation: Interest earned is typically discovered during the monthly bank reconciliation. This 'book-side' adjustment ensures the GL matches the actual bank balance.

title: Bank Service Charges
description: Recording monthly bank maintenance fees and wire transfer charges deducted by the bank.
explanation: Usually discovered during the monthly bank reconciliation. This entry ensures the company's ledger matches the bank statement.

title: Own Delivery Fleet Fuel Cost
description: Recording fuel expenses for the restaurant's in-house delivery motorbikes.
explanation: Fuel for delivery vehicles is a direct cost of the delivery revenue stream. Tracking it separately enables calculation of the profitability of own-fleet delivery.

title: Delivery Driver Wages
description: Recording the wages for in-house delivery staff separate from kitchen and floor payroll.
explanation: Separating delivery labor from kitchen labor allows the restaurant to accurately calculate the cost and profitability of its own-fleet delivery channel.

title: Cleaning & Hygiene Supplies
description: Recording the weekly purchase of cleaning products, sanitizers, and hygiene supplies.
explanation: Hygiene supplies are a direct operating cost for any food establishment. They are expensed as used and are often subject to health inspection scrutiny.

title: Staff Uniform Purchase
description: Buying branded aprons, shirts, and caps for the kitchen and floor team.
explanation: Uniforms are typically expensed in the period purchased unless they are a significant amount covering multiple years, in which case they may be capitalized.

title: Annual Restaurant Insurance Premium
description: Paying the yearly commercial insurance for the restaurant premises and contents.
explanation: Insurance covering 12 months is a prepaid asset. JOD 200 moves to Insurance Expense each month to match the cost to the period it protects.

title: Monthly Insurance Expense Recognition
description: Moving one month of prepaid insurance to the expense account.
explanation: This standard month-end entry ensures the P&L reflects the cost of insurance coverage for each specific month.

title: Happy Hour Price Reduction
description: Recording discounted beverage sales during the daily happy hour promotion.
explanation: Recording the full price and a discount separately allows management to track the true cost of running the promotion versus the gross revenue potential.

title: Pest Control Service
description: Monthly payment to an extermination company for health compliance.
explanation: Pest control is a mandatory expense for any food business. It is a recurring operating cost and is often checked during health authority inspections.

title: Background Music License Fee
description: Paying an annual copyright license to play background music legally in the restaurant.
explanation: Playing music in a commercial venue requires a license. This is an operating expense that supports the dining atmosphere.

title: Crockery & Glassware Breakage
description: Expensing broken plates, glasses, and cutlery from the floor.
explanation: Crockery is tracked as an operating supply. Regular physical counts reveal the monthly breakage rate, which is then expensed to keep inventory accurate.

title: Monthly Bank Loan Repayment
description: Paying the monthly installment on a bank loan taken to open the restaurant.
explanation: Every loan payment is split between reducing the principal debt and paying the cost of borrowing (interest). Only the interest is an expense.

title: Accruing Monthly Loan Interest
description: Recording interest that has accumulated on the restaurant's bank loan before the payment date.
explanation: Under the accrual basis, interest is an expense in the month it accrues, not when it is paid. This ensures the P&L shows the true monthly cost of debt.

title: Owner Cash Injection (Shareholder Loan)
description: The restaurant owner puts in personal money to cover a slow month's operating costs.
explanation: Common in restaurant startups and SMEs. The owner's personal funds are a liability for the business because they must eventually be repaid. This is NOT equity unless formally structured as a capital contribution.

title: Reversal of Prior Month Accrual
description: Reversing the utility accrual at the start of the new month to avoid double-counting when the actual bill arrives.
explanation: Reversing entries are made on the first day of a new period so that when the actual vendor invoice arrives, the net expense recorded is correct with no double counting.

title: Cash Purchase of Commercial Building
description: Recording the outright purchase of a commercial building paid in full.
explanation: The building is recorded at its full purchase price. Land and building costs should be separated because land is never depreciated, while the building is depreciated over its useful life.

title: Mortgage-Financed Property Purchase
description: Buying a building with a 20% down payment and an 80% bank mortgage.
explanation: The full fair value of the building is recorded as an asset. The down payment reduces cash and the remaining balance becomes a long-term liability to the bank.

title: Title Registration & Legal Fees (Capitalized)
description: Capitalizing legal fees, title registration costs, and notary charges into the cost of the property.
explanation: Under IAS 16, all costs necessary to bring the asset to its intended use must be capitalized. Legal fees, title search costs, and registration charges are part of the acquisition cost of the property.

title: Property Transfer Tax (Land Registration Fee)
description: Recording the government transfer tax paid when registering a property in Jordan.
explanation: In Jordan, the land registration fee paid to the Lands & Survey Department is a capitalized acquisition cost, not an expense. It forms part of the property's total cost on the balance sheet.

title: Pre-Acquisition Due Diligence Costs
description: Paying for property surveys, environmental assessments, and structural inspections before completing a purchase.
explanation: Due diligence costs directly attributable to acquiring a specific property are capitalized as part of the asset cost. If the deal falls through, these costs are expensed immediately.

title: Failed Property Acquisition Costs (Expensed)
description: Writing off legal and survey costs for a property deal that did not complete.
explanation: If an acquisition is abandoned, the costs cannot be capitalized because no asset was acquired. They must be immediately expensed to the income statement.

title: Land and Building Cost Allocation
description: Splitting a lump-sum property purchase price between land and building based on an independent valuation.
explanation: Land and building must be recorded separately. Land is never depreciated under IAS 16, so this split is critical for accurate depreciation calculations. The allocation is typically based on a professional valuer's report.

title: Buyer's Broker Commission on Acquisition
description: Capitalizing the real estate agent's commission paid when purchasing a property.
explanation: Broker commissions paid by the buyer are part of the acquisition cost and must be capitalized into the asset. This increases the depreciable base of the building portion.

title: Contractor Progress Billing (CIP)
description: Recording a milestone payment to the main contractor as construction progresses.
explanation: All construction costs accumulate in the CIP account while the building is being built. CIP is a special fixed asset account — it is not depreciated until construction is complete and the building is placed into service.

title: Architect & Engineering Fees (CIP)
description: Capitalizing design and engineering consultancy fees into the construction project cost.
explanation: Professional fees directly related to a construction project are capitalized as part of the project cost, not expensed. They form part of the total cost of the completed building.

title: Building Permit & Municipality Fees (CIP)
description: Capitalizing the government permit fees required to legally commence construction.
explanation: Building permits and municipality approvals are a prerequisite for construction. These fees are directly attributable to the project and are therefore capitalized into the CIP account.

title: Capitalized Borrowing Costs — Construction Loan (IAS 23)
description: Adding monthly interest on a construction loan to the CIP asset during the build period.
explanation: Under IAS 23, interest on borrowings specifically used to finance a 'qualifying asset' (a building under construction) must be capitalized, not expensed, until the asset is ready for use.

title: Contractor Retention Withheld (CIP)
description: Withholding 10% of each progress payment as retention until project completion and defects period.
explanation: Retention is withheld as a performance guarantee. Even though you have not paid the full amount, the full contract value is still part of the CIP asset.

title: Release of Contractor Retention
description: Paying the retained amount to the contractor after the defects liability period ends.
explanation: Once the defects liability period has passed and the contractor has remedied all snags, the retention is released. This clears the liability with no impact on the asset value.

title: Transfer of CIP to Fixed Asset (Completion)
description: Reclassifying the completed building from Construction in Progress to a depreciable Fixed Asset.
explanation: This is the critical transfer entry made when the building receives its occupancy certificate. Depreciation begins from this date, not from when construction started.

title: Construction Change Order — Scope Increase
description: Recording additional costs approved under a variation order from the developer to the contractor.
explanation: Approved change orders increase the total construction cost. They are capitalized into CIP and increase the eventual cost of the completed building.

title: Contractor Delay Penalty (Liquidated Damages)
description: Deducting a penalty from the contractor's payment for failing to complete on time.
explanation: Liquidated damages recovered from a contractor for late delivery reduce the amount owed to them. They are recorded as other income because they offset the financial loss from delays.

title: Infrastructure Contribution to Municipality
description: Paying a mandatory infrastructure levy to the municipality as a condition of the building permit.
explanation: Mandatory government contributions required to develop a plot (road access, utilities connection) are directly attributable to the project and must be capitalized into CIP.

title: Initial Recognition of Investment Property (at Cost)
description: Recording a building purchased specifically to generate rental income as an investment property under IAS 40.
explanation: Under IAS 40, a property held to earn rental income or for capital appreciation is classified as Investment Property, not PPE. It can be measured at cost or fair value.

title: Investment Property Annual Fair Value Gain
description: Increasing the carrying value of an investment property following an independent valuation.
explanation: Under the IAS 40 Fair Value model, any increase in a property's market value goes directly to the income statement — unlike PPE revaluation which goes to OCI. The property is not depreciated.

title: Investment Property Annual Fair Value Loss
description: Reducing the carrying value of an investment property when market values decline.
explanation: Under IAS 40, decreases in fair value also go directly to the income statement. This can significantly impact reported profits in a falling market.

title: Transfer: Owner-Occupied Property → Investment Property
description: Reclassifying a building the company previously used itself, now leased out to a third party.
explanation: When transferring from PPE to Investment Property at fair value, the gain is initially recognized in OCI (not P&L), consistent with PPE revaluation rules.

title: Transfer: Investment Property → Inventory (for Sale)
description: Moving a building from Investment Property to inventory when the developer decides to sell it.
explanation: When the intention changes from holding to selling, the property transfers to inventory at its current carrying value (fair value). No gain or loss is recorded at the transfer date.

title: Disposal of Investment Property — Gain
description: Recording the sale of an investment property for more than its carrying value.

entries.account: Gain on Disposal of Investment Property

explanation: Gains on disposal of investment property go to the income statement. The asset is removed at its last revalued carrying amount.

title: Disposal of Investment Property — Loss
description: Recording the sale of an investment property for less than its carrying value.

entries.account: Loss on Disposal of Investment Property

explanation: If the property sells below its carrying value, the difference is an immediate loss on the income statement.

title: Off-Plan Unit Sale — Initial Deposit Received
description: Recording a booking deposit from a buyer for an apartment that has not yet been built.
explanation: Cash collected before the building is complete is a liability. Under IFRS 15, revenue is only recognized when control of the unit passes to the buyer — typically at handover.

title: Off-Plan Instalment Collection
description: Collecting a construction-linked instalment from an off-plan buyer as per the payment schedule.
explanation: Each instalment collected adds to the deferred revenue balance. No revenue is recognized until handover. The balance sheet grows on both the asset and liability sides simultaneously.

title: Revenue Recognition at Unit Handover (IFRS 15)
description: Recognizing the full sale price as revenue when a completed apartment is handed over to the buyer.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, control passes to the buyer at handover. The full deferred revenue liability is released to the income statement at that single point in time.

title: Cost of Sales Recognition at Handover
description: Recognizing the construction cost of a completed unit as Cost of Sales when it is handed over.
explanation: Revenue and cost of sales must be matched. The accumulated construction cost of the unit is transferred to Cost of Property Sold at the same time the sale revenue is recognized.

title: Sale of Completed Unit (Cash)
description: Recording the sale of a ready residential unit paid in full by the buyer.
explanation: For a ready unit sold at closing, both revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously at the point of sale.

title: Sale of Completed Unit (Buyer Uses Mortgage)
description: Recording a unit sale where the buyer finances the purchase through a bank mortgage.
explanation: When a buyer uses a bank mortgage, the developer records a receivable from the buyer's bank. Cash arrives after the bank processes the disbursement, typically within a few days of signing.

title: Sales Broker Commission Expense
description: Recording the commission paid to an external real estate agent for closing a unit sale.
explanation: Agent commissions (typically 2-3% of sale price) are a selling expense in the period the sale is completed. They are not capitalized into the property cost.

title: Buyer Cancellation — Deposit Forfeited
description: Retaining a buyer's deposit when they cancel the purchase agreement per the contract terms.
explanation: When a buyer legally cancels and forfeits their deposit, the deferred revenue liability is released to income. The developer has fulfilled no obligation but is contractually entitled to keep the funds.

title: Buyer Cancellation — Deposit Refunded
description: Refunding a buyer's deposit when the developer is at fault for the cancellation.
explanation: If the developer fails to deliver on time or breaches the contract, the deposit must be refunded. This simply clears the liability with no income recorded.

title: Post-Handover Payment Plan (Receivable)
description: Recording a receivable for the balance of a unit price to be paid after handover over 3 years.
explanation: Revenue is recognized at handover regardless of when cash is collected. The outstanding post-handover balance is a long-term receivable. An imputed interest adjustment may be required if the plan is interest-free.

title: Sale of Raw Land Plot
description: Recording the sale of an undeveloped land plot to another developer.
explanation: Land held for sale is removed from inventory at its cost. The difference between sale price and cost is a gain recognized immediately.

title: Monthly Rental Income Recognition
description: Recognizing one month of earned rental income from a commercial tenant.
explanation: Rental income is recognized monthly as the right to use the property is provided. This applies whether rent is collected in advance or arrears.

title: Advance Rent Received (Unearned)
description: Recording 12 months of rent received upfront from a commercial tenant at lease commencement.
explanation: Common in Jordan where landlords require 12 months in advance. The cash is not revenue yet — it is a liability representing the obligation to provide accommodation each month.

title: Monthly Recognition of Advance Rent
description: Recognizing one month of rental income from the previously collected advance.
explanation: Each month, JOD 5,000 is moved from the deferred liability to earned revenue as the tenant occupies and uses the space.

title: Straight-Line Lease Revenue (With Rent Escalation)
description: Recording rental income on a straight-line basis over a lease with annual rent increases.
explanation: If a lease has rent escalations, the total lease revenue is averaged and recognized evenly each month. Early months have a lower cash receipt but higher P&L revenue, with the difference accrued.

title: Tenant Security Deposit Received
description: Recording a refundable security deposit paid by a new tenant at lease commencement.
explanation: Security deposits are not income. They are liabilities because the landlord must return them at the end of the lease if the tenant has not caused damage.

title: Tenant Security Deposit Refund
description: Returning a tenant's security deposit at the end of the lease after inspection.
explanation: This clears the deposit liability once the property has been inspected and no deductions are required.

title: Tenant Deposit Partially Forfeited (Damage)
description: Retaining part of a tenant's deposit to cover the cost of repairs after they vacate.
explanation: If a tenant causes damage, the cost is recovered from the deposit. The retained portion is recognized as Other Income since the obligation to refund is extinguished.

title: Late Rent Payment Penalty
description: Charging a tenant a penalty fee for paying rent after the contractual due date.
explanation: Late payment penalties are recognized as Other Income when charged to the tenant. They compensate the landlord for the time value of money lost from delayed receipts.

title: Service Charge Billed to Tenant
description: Billing a commercial tenant for their share of common area maintenance (CAM) costs.
explanation: Many commercial leases are 'triple net' — tenants pay their proportional share of building operating costs. This is recognized as revenue when billed.

title: Vacancy — Reversal of Rental Accrual
description: Reversing accrued rental income for a unit that becomes vacant mid-month.
explanation: If a tenant vacates earlier than expected and rent cannot be collected for that period, any previously accrued income must be reversed to avoid overstating revenue.

title: Property Management Fee Revenue
description: Recording the monthly management fee earned for managing a property on behalf of an owner.
explanation: Management fees (typically 5-10% of collected rent) are recognized as revenue in the month the management service is provided.

title: Maintenance Cost Recharged to Owner
description: Passing through a plumbing repair cost to the property owner after arranging the fix.
explanation: The manager pays the contractor, records the expense, and immediately bills the owner. The net effect is zero income — the manager is simply a pass-through agent for this cost.

title: Owner Net Rental Distribution
description: Remitting the net rental proceeds to the property owner after deducting management fees and expenses.
explanation: This clears the liability to the owner created when rent was collected. The distribution is net of the management fee and any approved expenses.

title: Vacancy Loss Provision
description: Estimating and recording the expected income loss from a unit that is currently empty.
explanation: For larger managed portfolios, a provision for vacancy is created at year-end to reflect the economic reality that not all units will be occupied 100% of the time.

title: Building Maintenance Expense
description: Recording routine maintenance costs for a managed property (painting, minor repairs).
explanation: Routine maintenance that preserves the building in its current condition is expensed immediately. Only improvements that extend the asset's life are capitalized.

title: Facilities Management Contract (Annual)
description: Paying an FM company annually to manage the building's technical systems and maintenance.
explanation: An annual FM contract is a prepaid asset. JOD 3,000 moves to FM Expense each month to match the cost to the period the service is provided.

title: Elevator / Lift Annual Service Contract
description: Paying the annual maintenance fee for elevator certification and servicing.
explanation: Elevator service contracts are mandatory in most jurisdictions for multi-floor buildings. The annual fee is prepaid and amortized monthly.

title: Common Area Utilities (Electricity / Water)
description: Recording utility costs for shared areas — lobbies, parking, corridors — in a multi-tenant building.
explanation: Common area utilities are an operating cost of the building. In a triple-net lease arrangement, these are typically recharged to tenants through the service charge mechanism.

title: Annual Property & Liability Insurance
description: Paying the yearly insurance premium covering the building structure and public liability.
explanation: Building insurance is a prepaid asset, expensed at JOD 1,000 per month over the 12-month coverage period.

title: Security Guard Service (Monthly)
description: Recording the monthly cost of a contracted security company for the building.
explanation: Security is a recurring operating cost. It is expensed monthly as the service is rendered.

title: Landscaping & Cleaning Services
description: Monthly payment to a company for maintaining gardens and common area cleanliness.
explanation: Ongoing cleaning and landscaping are necessary to maintain the property's appeal and value. They are period expenses, not capital improvements.

title: Owners Association Sinking Fund Contribution
description: Making a monthly contribution to the building's sinking fund for future major repairs.
explanation: Sinking fund contributions are restricted cash set aside for future capital expenditure (roof replacement, façade renovation). They remain an asset until spent.

title: Sinking Fund Release for Major Repair
description: Using accumulated sinking fund cash to pay for a major roof replacement.
explanation: When the repair qualifies as a capital improvement it is debited to the asset; if it is merely maintenance it is debited to expense. Either way, the sinking fund is drawn down.

title: Mortgage Loan Drawdown
description: Receiving the first tranche of a construction mortgage from the bank.
explanation: Construction mortgages are often drawn in tranches as construction milestones are achieved. Each drawdown increases the loan liability.

title: Monthly Mortgage Repayment (Principal + Interest)
description: Recording the monthly installment on a property mortgage.
explanation: Each payment reduces the principal debt and recognizes the monthly borrowing cost. The interest portion is the only expense — the principal repayment is a balance sheet movement.

title: Loan Origination Fee Amortization
description: Monthly amortization of the upfront bank arrangement fee on a property mortgage.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, loan arrangement fees are not expensed upfront. They are deducted from the loan carrying value and amortized over the loan term using the effective interest rate method.

title: Mortgage Refinancing — Gain on Early Settlement
description: Settling an existing mortgage early at a discount and recording the gain.
explanation: If a bank agrees to settle the loan at a discount, the difference between the carrying value and the amount paid is a gain recognized immediately on the income statement.

title: Escrow Release on Project Completion
description: Releasing restricted escrow funds to the developer upon obtaining the building completion certificate.
explanation: Escrow accounts hold buyer payments in trust during construction. Upon legal completion, the regulator releases the funds to the developer.

title: Mezzanine Finance Drawdown
description: Receiving subordinated debt from a mezzanine lender to top up the equity gap in a development.
explanation: Mezzanine debt sits between senior debt and equity. It carries higher interest than a bank mortgage and is common in large real estate developments to bridge the funding gap.

title: Building Depreciation (Straight-Line)
description: Monthly depreciation charge on a commercial building over its useful life.
explanation: A building costing JOD 1,000,000 depreciated over 10 years (straight-line, excluding land) produces JOD 8,333/month. Land value is excluded from the depreciable base.

title: Component Accounting — Roof Replacement
description: Capitalizing a new roof as a separate asset component and removing the old roof's book value.
explanation: Under IAS 16 component accounting, each significant part of a building (roof, HVAC, structure) is depreciated separately. When a component is replaced, the old one is derecognized and the new one is capitalized.

title: Component Depreciation — HVAC System
description: Monthly depreciation on the building's central air conditioning system as a separate component.
explanation: HVAC systems have a shorter useful life (typically 10-15 years) than the building structure (40-50 years). Component accounting ensures each part is depreciated at the correct rate.

title: Impairment of Building (PPE)
description: Writing down a building's carrying value when its recoverable amount falls below book value.
explanation: Under IAS 36, if a building's recoverable amount (higher of fair value less costs to sell, or value in use) falls below its carrying amount, an impairment loss must be recognized.

title: Sale of Owner-Occupied Building — Gain
description: Removing a building and its accumulated depreciation and recording the profit on sale.
explanation: The gain is the difference between the sale proceeds and the net book value (cost minus accumulated depreciation). Both the asset and its depreciation must be 'flushed out' at disposal.

title: Sale of Owner-Occupied Building — Loss
description: Recording a loss when a building is sold for less than its net book value.
explanation: If the sale proceeds are less than the net book value, the shortfall is an immediate loss on the income statement.

title: Demolition of Existing Structure
description: Writing off an old building on purchased land that must be demolished before new construction.
explanation: The cost to demolish is capitalized into CIP because it is a necessary step to prepare the site. The old building's net book value is also transferred to CIP as part of the new project cost.

title: IFRS 16 Lease — Initial Recognition (Lessee)
description: Recording a Right-of-Use asset and lease liability when signing a long-term office or retail lease.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, almost all leases with a term over 12 months must be recognized on the balance sheet. The ROU asset and lease liability are both initially measured at the present value of future lease payments.

title: IFRS 16 Monthly Lease Payment Split
description: Splitting the monthly lease payment between principal reduction and interest expense.
explanation: Each lease payment is split: the interest portion is an income statement expense and the balance reduces the lease liability on the balance sheet.

title: Right-of-Use Asset Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation of the ROU asset over the shorter of the lease term or the asset's useful life.
explanation: A JOD 350,000 ROU asset over a 6-year lease is depreciated at JOD 4,861/month. The ROU asset depreciates while the lease liability is reduced by principal payments — these are two separate calculations.

title: Impairment of Right-of-Use Asset
description: Writing down an ROU asset when the leased space is no longer needed (e.g., vacated early).
explanation: If a leased premises is vacated but the lease obligation continues, the ROU asset must be tested for impairment and written down to its recoverable amount.

title: VAT on Property Sale (Jordan)
description: Recording the 16% Sales Tax on the sale of a commercial property in Jordan.
explanation: Commercial property sales in Jordan are subject to 16% Sales Tax. Residential property is generally exempt. VAT collected is a liability to the ISTD.

title: VAT on Commercial Rental Income
description: Adding 16% VAT to a commercial rental invoice in Jordan.
explanation: Commercial property rental in Jordan attracts 16% VAT. The landlord acts as a tax collector — the VAT is not income but a liability to be remitted to the ISTD.

title: Capital Gains Tax Provision on Property Sale
description: Accruing the estimated tax on the profit made from selling a property.
explanation: In Jordan, gains on property sales may be subject to income tax. The estimated liability is accrued in the same period as the gain so the P&L reflects the true net profit from the transaction.

title: Withholding Tax on Rental Income (Tenant Deducts)
description: Recording rental income net of 5% withholding tax deducted by the corporate tenant.
explanation: When a company pays rent to an individual or entity, it withholds 5% and remits it to the ISTD. The landlord records the full rental income and the withholding as a tax prepayment asset.

title: Deferred Tax on Revaluation Surplus
description: Recording the deferred tax liability arising from an upward revaluation of an investment property.
explanation: A property revaluation surplus is a temporary difference between accounting value and tax base. Under IAS 12, a deferred tax liability must be recognized for the tax that will eventually be paid when the property is sold.

title: Zakat on Real Estate Assets (Saudi Arabia)
description: Accruing Zakat on property development inventory and real estate assets held by a KSA company.
explanation: In Saudi Arabia, property development companies must pay Zakat on their Zakatable base, which includes land inventory and work-in-progress. Investment properties held long-term may be exempt.

title: Owners Association Annual Fee
description: Paying the annual strata or homeowners association fee for a unit owned in a managed development.
explanation: Association fees covering a full year are prepaid assets, amortized at JOD 300/month into Association Fee Expense.

title: Municipality Building Levies
description: Recording annual municipality levies charged on commercial and residential buildings.
explanation: Municipality levies are recurring annual charges on property ownership. They are expensed in the period they relate to.

title: Sale-and-Leaseback Transaction — Gain
description: Selling an office building and immediately leasing it back, recording the gain under IFRS 16.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, a sale-leaseback produces a partial gain. The seller-lessee only recognizes the gain relating to the rights transferred to the buyer-lessor, and retains a ROU asset for the rights it keeps.

title: Land Contribution to Joint Venture
description: Contributing land to a real estate JV in exchange for a 50% ownership stake.
explanation: When contributing assets to a JV, the interest received is recorded at fair value. The difference between fair value and book value of the contributed land is a gain — but only for the portion of ownership transferred to the other party.

title: Joint Venture Share of Profit (Equity Method)
description: Recording the company's share of a real estate joint venture's annual net income.
explanation: Under the equity method (IFRS 11), the investor increases its investment value by its share of the JV's profit. This is not a dividend — it is an accrual of economic benefit.

title: VAT Input on Construction Costs
description: Recording recoverable VAT paid on contractor invoices during the construction phase.
explanation: VAT paid to contractors is a recoverable input tax, not part of the building cost. It is claimed back against the VAT charged on unit sales. Separating it from CIP is essential for correct tax filing.

title: Provision for Post-Handover Snagging
description: Accruing the estimated cost of defect repairs expected after handing units over to buyers.
explanation: Prudence requires recognizing the cost of defects in the same period as the related handover revenue. Based on historical data, developers estimate a percentage of revenue as the expected snagging cost.

title: Property Marketing & Show Unit Costs
description: Recording the cost of building and furnishing a show apartment for sales purposes.
explanation: Show unit costs are selling expenses. They are expensed when incurred rather than capitalized into project costs, as they do not form part of the saleable property.

title: Pre-Opening Operating Costs (Expensed)
description: Recording costs incurred before a completed building is ready to generate rental income.
explanation: Pre-opening administrative and marketing costs are expensed as incurred. Unlike construction costs, they cannot be capitalized under IAS 38 as there is no identifiable future economic benefit directly attributable to them.

title: Annual Property Tax Monthly Accrual
description: Accruing one-twelfth of the annual property tax each month.
explanation: Even though the property tax bill arrives once a year, the cost relates to all 12 months. Accruing monthly prevents a profit distortion in the month the bill arrives.

title: Goodwill on Acquisition of Property Portfolio
description: Recording goodwill when acquiring a real estate company for more than the fair value of its property assets.
explanation: When acquiring a property company rather than individual assets, any premium paid over the net fair value of assets acquired is recorded as goodwill under IFRS 3.

title: Reclassification of Current Portion of Mortgage
description: Moving the portion of a mortgage due within the next 12 months from long-term to current liabilities.
explanation: At each year-end, the portion of mortgage principal payable within the next 12 months must be presented as a current liability so that the balance sheet accurately shows short-term obligations.

title: Intercompany Loan for Property Development
description: Recording a loan from the parent company to a subsidiary SPV set up for a development project.
explanation: Real estate developers often use Special Purpose Vehicles (SPVs) for individual projects. Funding from the parent is an intercompany liability at the SPV level.

title: Equity Injection into Development SPV
description: The parent company subscribing to shares in a newly formed project SPV.
explanation: SPVs are typically funded with a small par value share capital and a large share premium. This structure is common in real estate project finance arrangements.

title: Mobilization Advance — Monthly Amortization
description: Reducing the mobilization advance liability as work progresses and the advance is recovered through deductions on progress billings.
explanation: Mobilization advances are typically recovered by deducting a fixed percentage from each progress certificate. This entry reduces the liability as each invoice is issued, until the full advance is recovered.

title: Bid Bond / Tender Guarantee Deposit
description: Recording the cash margin paid to the bank to issue a bid bond when submitting a tender.
explanation: A bid bond is a guarantee that the contractor will sign the contract if awarded the project. The cash margin is restricted — it is still the company's asset but cannot be spent until the bid bond is released.

title: Bid Bond Release (Post-Award or Rejection)
description: Releasing the bid bond margin back to the current account after the tender result.
explanation: Once the tender is awarded to another party or the contractor has signed the contract, the bid bond is released and the cash becomes freely available again.

title: Advance Payment Guarantee — Bank Fee
description: Recording the bank's annual commission for issuing an Advance Payment Guarantee (APG) on behalf of the contractor.
explanation: Clients require an APG before releasing a mobilization advance. The bank issues this guarantee for a fee (typically 1-2% per annum of the guarantee amount), which is an operating finance cost.

title: Letter of Guarantee (LG) Cash Margin
description: Recording cash blocked by the bank as collateral for issuing a performance or advance payment guarantee.
explanation: Banks often require contractors to block 10-30% of the guarantee value as cash collateral. This is still the contractor's asset — it is reclassified from unrestricted cash to restricted cash.

title: Contractor All-Risk (CAR) Insurance Premium
description: Paying the annual Contractor All-Risk insurance premium for a construction project.
explanation: CAR insurance is mandatory on most construction contracts. The premium is prepaid and amortized monthly over the project duration as it is directly attributable to the contract cost.

title: Site Establishment Costs
description: Capitalizing the initial cost of setting up the construction site — fencing, signage, temporary roads, and site offices.
explanation: Site setup costs are directly attributable to the contract and are capitalized into WIP. They will become part of Cost of Sales when the project revenue is recognized.

title: Progress Billing — Invoice Raised to Client
description: Issuing a monthly progress certificate invoice to the client based on work completed.
explanation: Progress billing creates a receivable from the client. The 'Billings on Contracts' account is a contra account used to track the cumulative amount billed versus revenue earned under the percentage of completion method.

title: Contract Revenue Recognition — % of Completion
description: Recognizing revenue based on the percentage of the project physically completed at month-end.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, revenue on long-term contracts is recognized over time as performance obligations are satisfied. The percentage is calculated as (costs incurred to date / total estimated costs) × contract price.

title: Over-Billing (Billings Exceed Revenue Earned)
description: Recording the liability when the amount invoiced to the client exceeds the revenue actually earned based on completion.
explanation: If a contractor bills ahead of actual work done, the excess is a liability (deferred revenue). This is common in front-loaded payment schedules. It represents an obligation to perform future work.

title: Under-Billing (Revenue Earned Exceeds Billings)
description: Recording the asset when revenue earned based on completion exceeds the amount invoiced to date.

entries.account: Costs and Estimated Earnings in Excess of Billings (Asset)

explanation: Under-billing (or 'unbilled revenue') occurs when work is done but invoicing lags. This is a key balance sheet item in construction — it represents earned but not yet billed revenue and is effectively a receivable.

title: Retention Receivable — Release at Practical Completion
description: Collecting the withheld retention amount from the client after achieving practical completion and the defects period ending.
explanation: Retention is typically released in two stages — 50% at practical completion and 50% after the defects liability period. This entry clears the long-held receivable when the client finally pays.

title: Contractual Claim Revenue Accrual
description: Accruing revenue for a claim submitted to the client for additional costs caused by employer-risk events (e.g., design changes, delays).
explanation: Under IFRS 15, claim revenue is recognized only when it is highly probable it will not be reversed. Claims must be formally submitted, supported by cost evidence, and the client must have acknowledged liability or the contract terms must support the claim.

title: Disputed Variation Order — Contingent Revenue
description: Recording a variation order submitted to the client that is under dispute and not yet approved.
explanation: Disputed variations are disclosed but NOT recognized as revenue under IFRS 15 until the constraint on variable consideration is lifted. Premature recognition of disputed amounts is a common audit finding in construction.

title: Price Escalation Clause — Revenue Adjustment
description: Increasing the contract value due to a contractual price escalation clause triggered by rising material or fuel costs.
explanation: Many long-term contracts include price adjustment clauses tied to published indices (steel prices, fuel costs). Once the formula is applied and agreed, the additional revenue is recognized.

title: Milestone Payment Received from Client
description: Collecting a payment linked to achieving a specific project milestone (e.g., structural completion).
explanation: Milestone payments clear the progress billing receivable. The cash receipt does not create revenue — revenue was recognized earlier when the work was performed.

title: Final Account Settlement
description: Recording the agreed final contract value after all variations, claims, and adjustments are settled.
explanation: The final account is negotiated and agreed at project close. Any difference between the final agreed amount and previously recognized revenue is booked as a revenue adjustment in the period of agreement.

title: Material Purchase — Cash (Site)
description: Buying construction materials (steel, cement, blocks) with cash for immediate site use.
explanation: Materials paid in cash are still assets until consumed on site. Small cash purchases are common in construction for urgent material needs.

title: Material Issued from Store to Site (WIP)
description: Moving materials from the central warehouse to a specific project site.
explanation: When materials leave the central store for a specific job, the cost transfers from general inventory to the project's WIP. This is essential for accurate job costing.

title: Surplus Material Returned to Store
description: Returning unused materials from a project site back to the central warehouse.
explanation: Unused materials are returned and credited back to the originating project's WIP. This prevents inflating the project cost and keeps the store balance accurate.

title: Material Wastage / Breakage on Site
description: Writing off materials damaged or wasted beyond acceptable norms on the construction site.
explanation: Normal wastage is usually built into the bill of quantities. Abnormal wastage (theft, negligence, accidents) is expensed separately so project managers can identify efficiency problems.

title: Import Duties on Imported Materials
description: Capitalizing customs duties paid on imported structural steel or specialist equipment into the material cost.
explanation: Import duties are part of the cost to bring materials to the site. They are capitalized into the material cost, not expensed separately, following IAS 2.

title: Fuel & Consumables for Plant and Machinery
description: Recording diesel and lubricants used by excavators, cranes, and generators on site.
explanation: Fuel directly consumed in project operations is a direct cost allocated to WIP, not a general overhead. Tracking per-project fuel consumption is a key cost control measure.

title: Advance Payment to Material Supplier
description: Paying a cement or rebar supplier in advance to secure materials at an agreed price.
explanation: Supplier advances are current assets, not expenses. They are offset against the supplier's invoices as materials are delivered.

title: Supplier Advance Offset Against Delivery
description: Clearing the advance when the supplier delivers materials and invoices the project.
explanation: When materials arrive, the advance is offset against the invoice value. If there is a remaining balance after offsetting, cash or payables are used for the difference.

title: Site Workers Monthly Payroll (Gross)
description: Recording the gross wages for all site-based staff — foremen, masons, carpenters, and laborers.
explanation: Site labor is a direct project cost allocated to WIP. All wages for workers whose time is directly attributable to the project are capitalized, not expensed.

title: Social Security Contribution — Site Workers (Jordan)
description: Recording the employer's SSC obligation for Jordanian site workers.
explanation: The employer's 14.25% SSC contribution is part of the true cost of site labor and is allocated to WIP. The employee's 7.5% share is deducted from wages payable.

title: Overtime — Site Workers (Peak Activity)
description: Accruing overtime wages for site workers during an accelerated construction phase.
explanation: Overtime for site workers is a direct project cost. It is allocated to the specific project's WIP and will flow into Cost of Sales when the related revenue is recognized.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Site Staff
description: Accruing the end-of-service benefit for long-term site employees as required by Jordanian labor law.
explanation: EOSB for site workers is a direct labor cost and is allocated to WIP. For office and admin staff, EOSB goes to overhead expense. This distinction is critical for accurate project cost reporting.

title: Worker Accommodation & Camp Costs
description: Recording the cost of housing, meals, and transportation provided to workers at a remote construction site.
explanation: Worker accommodation and welfare costs are direct project costs, especially for remote sites. They are allocated to the project WIP as part of site overhead.

title: Safety Gear / PPE (Site Workers)
description: Purchasing hard hats, safety boots, harnesses, and hi-vis vests for all site personnel.
explanation: Safety equipment is a mandatory direct project cost. It is allocated to WIP rather than expensed because it is directly attributable to specific contract activity.

title: Foreign Worker Visa & Mobilization Costs
description: Recording the visa fees, medical exams, and flight costs to mobilize foreign laborers to the site.
explanation: Worker mobilization costs are directly attributable to the project and are capitalized to WIP. They are amortized over the expected tenure of those workers on the project.

title: Labour-Only Subcontractor Cost
description: Recording the cost of a gang of workers supplied by a labour-only subcontractor (materials are provided by the main contractor).
explanation: Labour-only subcontractors are common in construction. Their costs are direct labor to WIP, separate from specialist subcontractors who also supply materials.

title: Staff Transport to and from Site
description: Recording the cost of daily bus transport for workers between accommodation and the construction site.
explanation: Site transport is an essential cost of delivering the contract. It is a direct project cost allocated to WIP.

title: Indirect Labor — Site Engineers & Supervisors
description: Recording the salary costs of site managers, quantity surveyors, and engineers who supervise multiple activities.
explanation: Supervisory staff who work exclusively on one project are an indirect project cost allocated to that project's WIP as site overhead.

title: Subcontractor Invoice Accrual
description: Accruing the cost of work completed by a specialist subcontractor before their invoice is received.
explanation: To accurately report project costs at month-end, subcontractor costs must be accrued based on work certified, even if the invoice has not yet been received. This is critical for correct percentage-of-completion calculations.

title: Subcontractor Advance Payment
description: Paying a specialist subcontractor an advance to mobilize and procure materials.
explanation: Subcontractor advances are assets until the work is performed. They are recovered by deducting from future payment certificates — similar to how client advances are recovered.

title: Subcontractor Advance — Recovery Deduction
description: Deducting the subcontractor advance from a progress payment certificate as work is completed.
explanation: The advance is recovered proportionally from each progress payment. This reduces the subcontractor advance asset without recording an expense — it is a balance sheet offset.

title: Subcontractor Retention Release
description: Releasing the retained amount to a subcontractor after their portion of works is complete and the defects period passes.
explanation: This mirrors the retention receivable from the client but from the subcontractor's perspective — the main contractor holds retention from subcontractors as a quality guarantee.

title: Subcontractor Back-Charge
description: Deducting costs from a subcontractor's payment for remedial work or damage they caused.
explanation: When a subcontractor causes damage or forces the main contractor to perform remedial work, the cost is 'back-charged' by deducting from the sub's next payment certificate. It reduces the WIP cost for the project.

title: Subcontractor Termination Costs
description: Recording costs associated with terminating a non-performing subcontractor mid-contract.
explanation: Termination costs — including demobilization, settlement of unpaid work, and costs to complete the works using an alternative subcontractor — are expensed as they represent an inefficiency loss rather than a productive cost.

title: Withholding Tax Deducted from Subcontractor
description: Deducting withholding tax from a domestic subcontractor's payment as required by Jordanian tax law.
explanation: In Jordan, companies must withhold 5% tax from payments to certain domestic contractors and subcontractors and remit it to the ISTD. The subcontractor receives the net amount.

title: Subcontractor Final Account Settlement
description: Recording the agreed final payment to a subcontractor after all work, variations, and disputes are resolved.
explanation: The final account may include agreed variations not previously certified. Any additional cost above what was already accrued is added to WIP in the period the settlement is agreed.

title: Specialist Consultant Fee (Structural / MEP)
description: Paying a structural engineer or MEP consultant for specialist design and supervision services.
explanation: Specialist consultants engaged for a specific project are a direct project cost capitalized to WIP. General design consultants not tied to a specific project would be overhead.

title: Plant & Equipment Purchase (Owned)
description: Buying a construction excavator or concrete mixer for use across multiple projects.
explanation: Owned plant is a fixed asset depreciated over its useful life. The depreciation (not the purchase cost) is allocated to projects as a usage charge.

title: Plant Depreciation Allocated to Project WIP
description: Charging a project with its share of plant depreciation based on machine hours used.
explanation: Depreciation of owned plant is allocated to specific projects based on hours of utilization. This ensures each project bears its fair share of the equipment cost.

title: Plant Hire / Equipment Rental
description: Recording the weekly rental of an excavator or tower crane from a plant hire company.
explanation: Hired plant costs are a direct project cost allocated to WIP. Renting is preferred when equipment is needed for a short period or when capital is constrained.

title: Tower Crane Monthly Hire
description: Recording the monthly hire charge for a tower crane on a multi-storey building project.
