explanation: Each annuity payment reduces the liability recognized when the gift annuity was set up. The liability decreases with each payment until the donor's death, at which point the remaining balance becomes unrestricted.

title: Purchase of Office Equipment
description: Buying computers, printers, and office furniture for the NGO's headquarters.
explanation: Equipment over the capitalization threshold is a fixed asset. The cost is allocated between Management & General and Program based on how the equipment is used.

title: Monthly Depreciation — Office & Program Assets
description: Recording monthly depreciation across all fixed assets used by the NGO.
explanation: Depreciation is allocated across functional categories based on how each asset is used. This ensures the Statement of Functional Expenses reflects the true cost of delivering programs versus running the organization.

title: Donated Asset — Subsequent Depreciation
description: Depreciating a vehicle that was donated and recorded at fair value.
explanation: Donated assets are depreciated the same way as purchased assets — starting from the recorded fair value. A vehicle worth JOD 8,000 with a 5-year life generates JOD 133/month of depreciation.

title: Leasehold Improvement — Program Office
description: Capitalizing fit-out costs for a rented program office.
explanation: Permanent improvements to rented space are capitalized and depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or useful life. For an NGO, these are typically program or management expenses.

title: Capital Campaign Expenditure — Construction Draw
description: Using restricted capital campaign funds to pay a construction contractor.
explanation: As capital campaign funds are spent on the building, the restriction is released. The cash decreases and the CIP asset increases. Restrictions are released in proportion to spending.

title: Asset Write-Off — Fully Depreciated
description: Removing a fully depreciated computer or printer that is no longer in use.
explanation: When an asset is fully depreciated and disposed of, both the cost and accumulated depreciation are removed. No gain or loss arises if the asset has zero book value.

title: Grant-Funded Asset — Deferred Income (IAS 20)
description: Recording a government grant received to buy program equipment as deferred income.
explanation: Under IAS 20, grants for assets are deferred and recognized as income over the same period the asset is depreciated. Each month, a portion of the deferred grant income is released to match the depreciation expense.

title: Month-End Net Asset Reconciliation
description: Verifying that the three net asset classes (Unrestricted, Temporarily Restricted, Permanently Restricted) balance.
explanation: This is a reconciliation check entry, not a transaction. The CFO or Finance Manager verifies that the sum of all three net asset classes equals total assets minus total liabilities at month-end.

title: Time-Based Restriction Release
description: Releasing a time-restricted donation when the specified time period has arrived.
explanation: Some donors specify that funds may only be used after a certain date. When that date arrives, the time-restriction is automatically lifted with this reclassification entry.

title: Correction of Prior Year Misclassification
description: Correcting an error where a restricted grant was incorrectly recorded as unrestricted.
explanation: Net asset misclassifications from prior periods are corrected by adjusting the opening balances. If material, this may require restating prior year financial statements.

title: Foreign Currency Translation — Field Office
description: Translating a field office's monthly activity from USD to JOD for consolidation.
explanation: Field offices report in their local currency. At consolidation, exchange rate differences between the transaction date and reporting date create a cumulative translation adjustment recorded in net assets.

title: Bank Reconciliation Adjustment — Interest Earned
description: Adjusting the general ledger for interest income appearing on the bank statement.
explanation: Bank interest is unrestricted income unless the board has placed restrictions on specific accounts. It is recognized when it appears on the bank statement during reconciliation.

title: Provision for Outstanding Grant Commitments
description: Accruing the estimated future expenditure committed under active grant agreements.
explanation: NGOs with signed grant agreements have future spending obligations. Provisioning at year-end ensures the financial statements reflect the full cost of existing commitments.

title: Accrued Annual Audit Fee
description: Accruing the auditor's fee at year-end before the invoice is received.
explanation: The annual audit relates to the fiscal year just ended. Accruing the fee ensures the expense is matched to the period being audited, not the following year when the invoice arrives.

title: Annual NGO Registration / License Renewal
description: Paying the annual renewal fee to the Ministry of Social Development or relevant authority.
explanation: NGO registration renewals are mandatory operating costs. They are classified under Management & General as they benefit the entire organization rather than a specific program.

title: Statutory Audit Fee Payment
description: Paying the external auditor's invoice for the annual financial statement audit.
explanation: This clears the accrued audit fee liability set up at year-end. The expense was already recognized — this is simply the cash settlement.

title: Legal & Compliance Expense
description: Paying a lawyer for reviewing grant agreements and employment contracts.
explanation: Legal fees for organizational compliance, contract review, and employment matters are Management & General expenses. Legal fees directly related to a specific program would be a program expense.

title: UBIT Tax Actual Payment
description: Paying the Unrelated Business Income Tax accrued in a prior period.
explanation: This clears the UBIT liability recognized when the income was earned. The payment is made to the tax authority — in Jordan, to the ISTD.

title: Withholding Tax on Foreign Grant Receipts
description: Recording withholding tax deducted by a foreign donor before disbursing a grant.
explanation: Some bilateral donors are required to withhold a percentage before disbursing. The NGO recognizes the gross grant as revenue. The withholding is a tax expense unless recoverable under a tax treaty.

title: Purchase — VAT Exempt Status (No Reclaim)
description: Recording a purchase where the NGO cannot reclaim VAT due to its exempt status.
explanation: NGOs with tax-exempt status cannot reclaim input VAT. The full amount including VAT is expensed or capitalized. This is different from commercial entities where VAT is a recoverable asset.

title: Annual Filing Fee — Ministry / Regulator
description: Paying the annual reporting and filing fee to the NGO regulatory authority.
explanation: Annual filing fees are a standard compliance cost for registered NGOs in Jordan. They are classified as Management & General since they benefit the whole organization.

title: Fundraising Gala — Ticket Sales Collected
description: Recording advance ticket sales for an annual fundraising gala.
explanation: Ticket sales before the event are deferred — the performance obligation (providing the event experience) has not yet been met. Revenue is recognized on the night of the event.

title: Fundraising Gala — Event Night Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing ticket revenue and recording event costs on the night of the gala.
explanation: On event night, deferred revenue converts to earned revenue. Direct costs (venue, catering, entertainment) are expensed simultaneously to show the net fundraising proceeds.

title: Online Crowdfunding Campaign Proceeds
description: Recording net proceeds from a GoFundMe or LaunchGood crowdfunding campaign.
explanation: Crowdfunding platforms deduct their fees before disbursing. Gross donations are recorded as revenue and the platform fee is a fundraising expense. Revenue classification depends on whether the campaign specifies a purpose.

title: Peer-to-Peer Fundraising Payout
description: Receiving funds raised by individual volunteer fundraisers on behalf of the NGO.
explanation: P2P fundraisers collect donations on the NGO's behalf. The funds are recognized as contributions when received by the NGO. The fundraiser acts as an agent — their collections are the NGO's revenue.

title: Donor Stewardship Expense
description: Recording costs of donor recognition events, impact reports, and thank-you gifts.
explanation: Donor stewardship costs (annual reports, thank-you events, impact visits) are classified as fundraising expenses. They are essential for donor retention but must be monitored as a percentage of fundraising revenue.

title: Gala Cancellation — Refund to Ticket Holders
description: Refunding ticket holders when an event is cancelled.
explanation: If the event is cancelled, the deferred revenue liability is cleared by refunding ticket holders. No revenue is recognized since the performance obligation will not be fulfilled.

title: Corporate Sponsorship Revenue
description: Recording a corporate sponsor's payment for naming rights or event sponsorship.
explanation: If the sponsor receives something in return (logo placement, speaking slot), it is an exchange transaction — not a contribution. It is recognized as revenue when the benefit is delivered.

title: Fundraising Raffle / Lottery Proceeds
description: Recording ticket sales from a charity raffle event.
explanation: Raffle proceeds are fundraising revenue. The cost of prizes is a fundraising expense. Net proceeds flow to the program or general fund. Local gaming/lottery laws must be observed.

title: Cash Sale at POS (No Card)
description: Recording a straightforward over-the-counter cash sale.
explanation: The most fundamental retail entry. Cash is collected, revenue is recognized, and the VAT element is separated as a liability to be remitted to the ISTD.

title: Debit Card Sale at POS
description: Recording a sale paid by debit card where the bank settles the same day.
explanation: Debit card transactions settle directly to the bank account, net of the POS terminal fee. The full sale price is revenue; the processing fee is a selling expense.

title: Contactless / Apple Pay / Samsung Pay Sale
description: Recording a tap-to-pay transaction at a contactless-enabled POS terminal.
explanation: Contactless payments are processed the same way as card payments. The fee percentage may differ slightly from chip-and-PIN depending on the acquiring bank agreement.

title: Split Payment — Cash and Card
description: A customer pays part of their bill in cash and the rest on a credit card.
explanation: Split payments require recording each payment method separately. Revenue and VAT remain unchanged — only the collection method is split.

title: Layaway Plan — Deposit Received
description: A customer puts an item on layaway by paying a 20% deposit to reserve it.
explanation: A layaway deposit is a liability — the store owes the customer either the goods or their money back. No revenue is recognized until the item is fully paid and collected.

title: Layaway — Final Payment and Revenue Recognition
description: Customer makes the final instalment, collects the item, and revenue is recognized.
explanation: At collection, the deposit liability is cleared, the remaining cash is received, and the full revenue and COGS are recognized together. Control passes at the point of physical handover.

title: Layaway Cancellation — Deposit Forfeited
description: Customer cancels their layaway after the forfeiture period and loses their deposit.
explanation: If the store's policy allows deposit forfeiture after a period of inactivity, the deposit is recognized as Other Income. The item is returned to the sales floor and no COGS is recorded.

title: Trade-In Allowance on Purchase
description: A customer brings in an old item and receives a trade-in discount on a new product.
explanation: The trade-in value is treated as partial payment. The used item enters inventory at its estimated resale value. The full retail price of the new item is the revenue base for VAT.

title: Wholesale / Bulk Sale to Business Customer
description: Selling a large quantity to a business customer at a negotiated wholesale price.
explanation: Wholesale sales are invoiced on credit terms rather than collected at POS. Revenue and COGS are recognized at point of delivery when control transfers to the buyer.

title: Buy Online, Pick Up In Store (BOPIS)
description: Customer orders online and pays upfront; revenue is recognized when they collect in store.
explanation: Payment is collected online but the item has not yet been handed over. Revenue is deferred until the customer collects. If not collected within the policy window, the deferred amount is released to revenue.

title: BOPIS — Customer Collects Item In Store
description: Recognizing revenue when the customer picks up their pre-paid online order.
explanation: Control passes at the point of physical handover. Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously when the customer collects the item.

title: Customer Account / House Account Sale
description: A VIP or corporate customer purchases on a store account settled monthly.
explanation: Store accounts allow trusted customers to purchase and settle at month-end. Revenue is recognized at sale; the receivable represents the amount owed on account.

title: Customer Return — Full Cash Refund
description: A customer returns an item within the return policy window and receives a full cash refund.
explanation: A full return reverses both the revenue and the COGS, and the item is restocked. VAT is also reversed since no taxable supply ultimately occurred.

title: Customer Return — Exchange for Different Item
description: Customer exchanges a returned item for a different product of the same value.

entries.account: Cost of Goods Sold — Item A (Reversal)
Cost of Goods Sold — Item B

explanation: An exchange is two transactions: a return of the original item and a new sale of the replacement. No cash changes hands if the values are equal.

title: Customer Return — Store Credit Issued
description: Customer returns an item but chooses store credit instead of a cash refund.
explanation: Store credit is a liability — the store owes the customer merchandise. Revenue is reversed on the original sale, and the credit balance is tracked until the customer spends it.

title: Customer Return — Damaged Item (Not Restocked)
description: Customer returns a damaged item that cannot be resold.
explanation: Revenue is reversed but the item cannot return to stock. The COGS reversal moves the cost out of COGS and into Inventory Loss — reflecting the economic reality that the goods are gone with no resale value.

title: Price Adjustment / Allowance (No Return)
description: Issuing a partial refund when a customer finds the same item cheaper elsewhere within the price-match window.
explanation: A price adjustment does not involve returning goods. Only the price difference is refunded. COGS is unchanged since the goods remain with the customer.

title: Sales Return Provision — End of Period
description: Estimating and provisioning for returns expected in the next period on current-period sales.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, variable consideration (expected returns) must be estimated and deducted from revenue. For high-return categories (fashion, electronics), this is material and auditor-scrutinized.

title: Warranty Exchange — No Charge to Customer
description: Replacing a faulty product under warranty at no cost to the customer.
explanation: Warranty replacements are charged against the warranty provision set up at the original point of sale. No revenue or COGS entry is made — the cost was already anticipated in the provision.

title: Staff / Employee Discount Sale
description: Recording a sale where a staff member purchases goods at a 30% employee discount.
explanation: Revenue is recorded at the full retail price. The employee discount is a staff benefit expense, not a revenue reduction. This keeps the gross margin analysis clean and tracks the true cost of the benefit.

title: Senior Citizen / Loyalty Member Discount
description: Recording a sale with a permanent 10% discount for a loyalty programme member.
explanation: Loyalty discounts are contra-revenue — they reduce net sales without affecting the VAT base, which is calculated on the actual amount paid by the customer.

title: Bundle / Multi-Buy Promotion (3 for 2)
description: Customer buys three items in a 3-for-2 promotion, effectively getting one free.

entries.account: Sales Revenue (3 Items at Full Price)

explanation: Recording the promotion as a discount rather than a reduced price preserves the full revenue figure for analytics. Management can see both gross revenue and the cost of the promotion separately.

title: Buy-One-Get-One-Free (BOGO) Sale
description: Customer buys one item at full price and receives a second identical item at no charge.

entries.account: Sales Revenue (2 units at full price)

explanation: BOGO promotions are recorded using the full price for both units. The free item's value is recorded as a discount. COGS reflects both units consumed regardless of the promotional structure.

title: Clearance Sale — Markdown Below Cost
description: Selling end-of-season inventory at a price below cost to clear shelf space.
explanation: When selling below cost, COGS exceeds revenue — a deliberate decision to recover partial value. If an NRV write-down was previously recorded, the COGS entry would be at the written-down value instead.

title: Seasonal Markdown — Inventory Write-Down
description: Writing down winter clothing inventory at season-end to its expected clearance sale price.
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be reported at the lower of cost or NRV. At season-end, when a collection can only be sold at a significant discount, an NRV write-down is required before the next reporting date.

title: Promotional Gift With Purchase (GWP)
description: Giving a free promotional item to customers who spend over a threshold amount.
explanation: The promotional gift is removed from inventory at cost and expensed as a marketing cost. No revenue is recorded for the free item. This preserves the integrity of the COGS margin on the main purchase.

title: Flash Sale / Weekend Promotion Discount
description: Recording high-volume sales during a 24-hour flash sale at 40% off.
explanation: Flash sales are tracked as contra-revenue to preserve the visibility of gross revenue in management reports. This enables calculation of the true revenue foregone during promotional periods.

title: Referral Discount — New Customer First Purchase
description: Giving a new customer a 15% discount because they were referred by an existing customer.
explanation: Referral discounts are a customer acquisition tool. Tracking them separately as contra-revenue allows measurement of the cost-per-acquisition through the referral channel.

title: Loyalty Points Earned — Revenue Deferral
description: Deferring a portion of revenue when a customer earns redeemable points on a purchase.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, loyalty points with standalone value are a separate performance obligation. The estimated redemption value is deferred as a liability until points are used or expire.

title: Loyalty Points Redeemed on Purchase
description: Customer uses accumulated points to get a discount on their next purchase.
explanation: Redemption converts the deferred points liability into earned revenue. The customer pays the balance in cash and the transaction is closed.

title: Loyalty Points Breakage (Expired Points)
description: Recognizing revenue from loyalty points that have expired without being redeemed.
explanation: Points that pass their expiry date without redemption are an obligation that no longer exists. The liability is released to Other Income using historical expiry rate assumptions.

title: Gift Card — Partial Redemption with Cash Top-Up
description: Customer partially uses a gift card and pays the remaining balance in cash.
explanation: The gift card value offsets part of the sale. The remainder is paid in cash. Only the portion of the gift card actually used converts to revenue — the remaining balance stays as a liability.

title: Store Credit Balance Issued to Customer
description: Issuing a store credit note to a customer instead of a cash refund.
explanation: A store credit is a liability — the store owes the customer merchandise or services. Revenue is reversed and the credit balance is held until the customer spends it.

title: Store Credit Redeemed on Purchase
description: Customer uses their store credit balance to pay for a new purchase.
explanation: When credit is redeemed, the liability converts to earned revenue and COGS is recognized. No cash changes hands — the transaction is settled entirely through the credit balance.

title: Inventory Purchase — Cash Payment
description: Buying stock directly from a supplier with immediate cash payment.
explanation: Inventory purchased for cash is immediately an asset at cost. It remains on the balance sheet until sold, when it transfers to Cost of Goods Sold.

title: Inventory Purchase — Credit Terms (30 days)
description: Receiving a stock delivery from a supplier on 30-day credit terms.
explanation: Inventory arrives and is immediately an asset regardless of when the supplier is paid. VAT on the purchase is a separate recoverable asset, not part of the inventory cost.

title: Goods Received — Quantity Discrepancy Claim
description: Raising a debit note when fewer units arrive than stated on the supplier's invoice.
explanation: A quantity discrepancy is resolved by reducing both the inventory asset and the payable by the value of the missing goods. A formal debit note is sent to the supplier.

title: Freight-In Capitalized into Inventory Cost
description: Adding inbound freight charges to the cost of delivered inventory.
explanation: Under IAS 2, all costs to bring inventory to its present location must be included in its cost. Freight-in is capitalized into inventory, increasing COGS when the items are eventually sold.

title: Consignment Inventory — Outbound Shipment to Dealer
description: Sending goods to a dealer's showroom on consignment — ownership is retained by the store.
explanation: Consignment outbound is a transfer between two asset accounts — not a sale. The goods are still the store's property until the dealer makes a sale.

title: Inter-Store Inventory Transfer
description: Moving stock from a warehouse or flagship store to a branch with low inventory.
explanation: Inter-store transfers are internal movements with no P&L impact. Total inventory value is unchanged — it is simply reclassified between locations for stock management purposes.

title: Drop-Shipment — Supplier Ships Directly to Customer
description: Recording a sale where the supplier ships directly and the store never holds the goods.
explanation: In dropshipping, no inventory asset is ever created. Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously at the time of the customer order.

title: Advance Payment to Supplier for Future Stock
description: Paying a supplier in advance to secure a large seasonal stock order.
explanation: Supplier advances are current assets — not expenses. They convert to inventory when the goods are delivered and the supplier's invoice is received.

title: Supplier Advance Settled on Delivery
description: Offsetting the advance against the supplier invoice when goods are received.
explanation: When the goods arrive, the advance converts to inventory. If the actual invoice differs from the advance amount, cash or a payable balances the difference.

title: COGS Recognition — Periodic Inventory System
description: Calculating and recording cost of goods sold at the end of a period using the periodic method.
explanation: Under the periodic system, COGS = Opening Inventory + Purchases - Closing Inventory. The closing inventory figure comes from the physical count. COGS is calculated and recorded as one entry at period-end.

title: COGS Recognition — Perpetual Inventory System (Per Sale)
description: Recognizing cost of goods sold at the point of each individual sale in a perpetual system.
explanation: In a perpetual system (used with barcode/POS systems), COGS is recognized on every sale transaction. The inventory balance is updated in real-time.

title: Inventory NRV Write-Down
description: Writing down the value of inventory when net realizable value falls below cost.
explanation: Under IAS 2, inventory must be reported at the lower of cost or NRV. This is especially relevant for fashion, electronics, and perishable goods where market prices can drop quickly.

title: Inventory Damage — Insurance Claim Receivable
description: Recording stock destroyed by flooding and the corresponding insurance claim.
explanation: The inventory loss and the insurance recovery are recorded separately. The loss is the full inventory value; the claim is what the insurer agrees to pay. Any shortfall is a net loss.

title: Cycle Count Overage — Inventory Surplus
description: Adjusting the books when a physical count finds more inventory than the system shows.
explanation: An overage means the system underestimated stock — perhaps due to a missed receiving entry. The inventory is adjusted upward and the gain is recognized as Other Income.

title: Dead Stock Write-Off (Fully Obsolete)
description: Removing completely unsellable inventory from the books after exhausting all markdown options.
explanation: When dead stock is physically disposed of, both the inventory and any previously established allowance are removed. If no provision exists, the full cost is expensed directly.

title: Monthly Store Rent — Prepaid Release
description: Recognizing one month of rent expense from a prepaid annual rent payment.
explanation: Common in Jordan where landlords require 12 months upfront. The prepaid balance is released at JOD 2,500/month to match the occupancy cost to each trading period.

title: Store Utilities — Monthly Accrual
description: Estimating and accruing electricity and water costs before the bill arrives.
explanation: Under the accrual basis, utilities consumed during the month must be recognized even if the bill arrives in the following month.

title: Store Cleaning & Maintenance Service
description: Monthly payment to a cleaning company and routine store maintenance.
explanation: Routine cleaning and maintenance keep the store presentable and functional. These are operating expenses recognized in the month the service is provided.

title: Store Security System — Monthly Fee
description: Monthly monitoring subscription for the alarm system and CCTV.
explanation: Security monitoring is a necessary overhead for any physical retail store. It protects against theft and reduces shrinkage losses.

title: Shopping Bags & Packaging Materials
description: Purchasing branded shopping bags, tissue paper, and boxes for customer purchases.
explanation: Packaging materials are a selling cost expensed when purchased (or when supplies are consumed). Premium packaging for luxury retail is a meaningful cost tracked for margin analysis.

title: Store Signage & Display Materials
description: Printing seasonal promotional signage and window display materials.
explanation: In-store signage and window displays are marketing costs expensed in the period they are used. Permanent signage above a capitalization threshold would be capitalized as a leasehold improvement.

title: In-Store Music Licensing Fee
description: Annual copyright license fee for playing background music in the retail store.
explanation: Playing music commercially requires a license. The annual fee is a prepaid asset amortized monthly at JOD 50.

title: ATM / Cash Handling Service Fee
description: Paying the bank for cash counting, coin rolling, and change provision services.
explanation: High-volume cash retailers pay banks to count and process cash. This is an operating expense discovered on the bank statement during reconciliation.

title: Sales Floor Staff Monthly Payroll (Gross)
description: Recording the gross wage expense for all floor staff — sales associates, cashiers, supervisors.
explanation: Gross payroll is recognized as an expense in the month earned. The net amount is paid after deducting SSC employee share and any income tax withholding.

title: Commission-Based Staff Payout
description: Paying monthly sales commissions to sales staff based on their individual sales targets achieved.
explanation: Commissions are earned when the sale occurs and matched to that period. They motivate sales performance and are a variable labour cost that scales with revenue.

title: Part-Time / Seasonal Staff Wages
description: Recording wages for temporary staff hired for Ramadan and holiday shopping seasons.
explanation: Seasonal hiring is common in retail. These wages are expensed in the period worked. Short-duration staff typically do not accumulate EOSB entitlements under Jordanian law.

title: SSC Employer Share — Retail Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the employer's 14.25% SSC contribution for retail store employees.
explanation: The employer's SSC contribution is a mandatory labour cost in Jordan. It is recognized in the same period as the related wages expense.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Retail Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service gratuity for permanent retail employees.
explanation: Jordanian labour law requires a gratuity payment when an employee resigns or is terminated. Monthly provisioning avoids a large unexpected cost and ensures accurate period reporting.

title: Staff Uniform Purchase
description: Buying branded polo shirts, aprons, and name badges for all store staff.
explanation: Uniforms are expensed in the period purchased. They are a staff welfare and brand presentation cost classified under HR or selling expenses.

title: Staff Training & Development Cost
description: Paying for customer service, sales technique, and product knowledge training for store staff.
explanation: Training is an operating expense. It improves staff capability but cannot be capitalized as an asset since the company does not own the knowledge.

title: Store Fit-Out / Leasehold Improvement
description: Capitalizing the cost of fitting out a new store location — flooring, shelving, lighting.
explanation: Permanent improvements to a rented store are capitalized as a fixed asset. They are depreciated over the shorter of the lease term or useful life — typically 5-10 years.

title: Leasehold Improvement — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation charge on the store fit-out.
explanation: An JOD 80,000 fit-out over a 5-year lease generates JOD 1,333/month of depreciation. This is a significant fixed cost that must be covered before the store is profitable.

title: POS Terminal Purchase & Setup
description: Buying and setting up point-of-sale hardware for a new store.
explanation: POS terminals are fixed assets capitalized at cost and depreciated over their useful life (typically 3-5 years).

title: POS Terminal Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on POS hardware.
explanation: A JOD 3,500 POS system with a 3-year life generates JOD 97/month depreciation. POS hardware depreciates faster than structural fit-out.

title: Shelving & Display Fixtures Capitalization
description: Capitalizing the cost of branded shelving units, display cases, and gondolas.
explanation: Significant fixtures above the capitalization threshold are fixed assets. Smaller items (hangers, price tags) are expensed immediately as store supplies.

title: Furniture & Equipment Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on store furniture, display cases, and shelving.
explanation: Retail fixtures are depreciated over 5 years (straight-line). The monthly charge is part of the fixed overhead that management must monitor relative to store sales volume.

title: Store Renovation — Capital vs. Expense Decision
description: Repainting the store interior — expensed because it restores rather than enhances.
explanation: Routine repainting is maintenance — it restores the asset to its original condition but does not enhance or extend its life. It is expensed immediately. A full store refit that adds new lighting or extends the layout would be capitalized.

title: Fully Depreciated Fixture Write-Off
description: Removing old display shelving from the books when it is replaced after its useful life.
explanation: When a fully depreciated asset is disposed of, both the cost and its accumulated depreciation are removed. No gain or loss arises since the net book value is zero.

title: VAT Output on Retail Sales (Jordan 16%)
description: Summarizing total VAT collected on retail sales during the month for ISTD filing.
explanation: Net VAT payable = Output VAT (collected from customers) - Input VAT (paid on purchases). Only the net difference is remitted to the ISTD monthly.

title: VAT Input on Inventory Purchases
description: Recording the recoverable VAT paid on inventory purchased from a registered supplier.
explanation: VAT on inventory is a recoverable asset, not a cost. It is offset against VAT collected on sales when calculating the monthly net VAT payment to the ISTD.

title: VAT Monthly Remittance to ISTD
description: Paying the net VAT liability to the ISTD by the filing deadline.
explanation: This is the final step in the VAT cycle — transferring the collected net VAT to the government. Late payment attracts penalties and interest.

title: VAT Exempt Goods Sale (Zero-Rated)
description: Recording the sale of a product category exempt from sales tax in Jordan (e.g., basic foods).
explanation: Exempt goods are sold without adding VAT. No Sales Tax Payable entry is made. Retailers must clearly separate exempt and taxable sales for accurate VAT return filing.

title: Customs Duty on Imported Goods
description: Capitalizing customs duties into the cost of imported inventory.
explanation: Import duties are part of the inventory's landed cost and are capitalized under IAS 2. They increase COGS when items are sold and directly affect the gross margin.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the year-end income tax on the store's taxable profit.
explanation: Income tax is accrued based on taxable profit. Differences between accounting profit and taxable income create deferred tax assets or liabilities.

title: Municipality License & Trade Fee
description: Paying the annual commercial license and municipality fees to operate the retail store.
explanation: Annual licenses are prepaid assets amortized monthly. Without a valid trade license, the store cannot legally operate.

title: Withholding Tax on Rent — Individual Landlord (Jordan)
description: Deducting 5% WHT from rent paid to an individual (non-company) landlord.
explanation: Jordanian law requires a company to deduct 5% from rent paid to an individual landlord and remit it to the ISTD. The landlord receives 95% and the company remits the 5% on their behalf.

title: Monthly Bank & POS Terminal Charges
description: Recording monthly bank account maintenance fees and POS terminal rental charges.
explanation: POS terminal rental and bank service fees are recurring operating costs. They are typically discovered during bank reconciliation.

title: End-of-Day Cash Count and Bank Deposit
description: Transferring the day's cash takings from the store till to the bank night safe.
explanation: The daily deposit transfers cash between two asset accounts — from the physical till to the bank account. This is not a revenue or expense event.

title: Bank Overdraft — Short-Term Working Capital
description: Using the store's overdraft facility to pay suppliers during a slow trading period.
explanation: When the bank balance goes negative, the liability shifts from Accounts Payable to Bank Overdraft. The bank charges interest on the overdrawn balance.

title: Trade Finance / Inventory Credit Line Draw-Down
description: Drawing on a revolving inventory finance facility to fund a large seasonal stock purchase.
explanation: Inventory credit lines allow retailers to stock up for peak seasons without tying up their own cash. The facility is repaid as stock is sold, matching the cash flow to the sales cycle.

title: Supplier Invoice — Early Payment Discount Captured
description: Paying a supplier early within the discount window to capture a 2% settlement discount.
explanation: Early payment discounts reduce the effective cost of inventory. The JOD 200 saving is credited back to inventory under the net method — it lowers COGS when those goods are sold.

title: FX Loss on USD-Priced Imported Goods
description: Recording a foreign exchange loss when the JOD weakens between the supplier invoice date and payment date.
explanation: Jordanian retailers importing goods priced in USD are exposed to FX risk. If the JOD weakens between invoice and payment, the store pays more in local currency — an FX loss on the income statement.

title: Tuition Payment Received — Cash / Bank Transfer
description: Recording a student paying their semester tuition directly by bank transfer.
explanation: The cash payment clears the receivable set up when tuition was billed at semester start. Revenue was already recognized as the semester progressed — this is a balance sheet settlement only.

title: Tuition Paid by Employer Sponsor (Third Party)
description: An employer pays a student's tuition directly to the institution under a sponsorship agreement.
explanation: Employer-sponsored tuition is common in professional development programmes. The payment clears the student's receivable balance. Revenue recognition is unchanged — the payer identity does not affect when revenue is earned.

title: Tuition Instalment Plan — First Payment
description: A student enrolls in a payment plan and pays the first instalment at the start of semester.
explanation: Instalment plans split the tuition receivable into monthly payments. The full semester tuition was billed and deferred at enrolment; each payment reduces the receivable as cash is collected.

title: Tuition Instalment — Subsequent Monthly Payment
description: Recording the second and subsequent monthly instalment payments from a student on a payment plan.
explanation: Each instalment reduces the outstanding receivable. If a student misses a payment, the institution may charge a late fee or suspend enrolment per its credit policy.

title: Online / Distance Learning Tuition Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly tuition revenue from students enrolled in an online programme.
explanation: Online tuition is often paid upfront per term and recognized monthly as courses are delivered. It is reported separately from on-campus tuition to track the performance of each delivery mode.

title: Tuition Overcharge Credit Refund
description: Refunding a student who was incorrectly billed a higher tuition rate.
explanation: Billing errors require a revenue reversal equal to the overcharge amount. If the error was in a prior period, the adjustment goes through the current period rather than restating historical figures.

title: Late Registration Fee
description: Charging a student a penalty fee for registering after the deadline.
explanation: Late registration fees are recognized immediately when charged since the obligation (allowing late enrolment) is fulfilled at the point of charge.

title: Transcript & Academic Certificate Fee
description: Recording income from issuing official transcripts or certified copies of qualifications.
explanation: Document issuance fees are recognized immediately upon issue. They represent ancillary administrative income for the institution.

title: Application Fee — Non-Refundable
description: Recording an application processing fee paid by a prospective student.
explanation: Non-refundable application fees are recognized immediately upon receipt — the performance obligation (processing the application) is satisfied when the fee is paid.

title: Re-Sit / Repeat Examination Fee
description: Charging a student for the right to retake a failed examination.
explanation: Re-sit fees compensate the institution for the administrative and faculty cost of re-examining. Revenue is recognized when the student is registered for the re-sit.

title: Need-Based Grant — Institution-Funded
description: Awarding a need-based financial grant from the institution's own funds to a qualifying student.
explanation: Institution-funded grants are an expense — not a scholarship allowance (contra-revenue). The distinction matters: institutional grants use the school's own resources, whereas tuition discounts reduce the gross billing.

title: Merit Scholarship — Full Tuition Award
description: Awarding a 100% tuition scholarship to an academically excellent student.
explanation: A full scholarship zeroes out the student's receivable. Revenue is never recognized for the full amount — only net tuition after all allowances is earned revenue.

title: External Scholarship Applied to Student Account
description: Applying a scholarship received from a government agency or private foundation to a student's tuition balance.
explanation: External scholarship money received on behalf of a student clears their receivable. The funds belong to the student — the school acts as a conduit collecting on their behalf.

title: Student Loan Disbursement — Third-Party Lender
description: Recording proceeds from a bank or government loan disbursed directly to the institution on a student's behalf.
explanation: The loan is the student's liability — not the institution's. The school simply receives the funds on the student's behalf, clearing their account. The school has no obligation to the lender.

title: Scholarship Overpayment Refund to Agency
description: Returning excess scholarship funds to a government agency when a student withdraws mid-term.
explanation: When a sponsored student withdraws, the unearned portion of the scholarship must be returned. This mirrors the Title IV return logic — the institution keeps only what was earned.

title: Sports / Athletic Scholarship (Grant-in-Aid)
description: Recording a full athletic scholarship covering tuition, housing, and meal plan.
explanation: Athletic scholarships cover multiple charges. The full cost is an athletic department expense. Each charged component — tuition, housing, dining — is offset against the respective revenue or receivable account.

title: Faculty Monthly Payroll (Gross)
description: Recording the gross salary expense for all academic teaching staff.
explanation: Faculty salaries are the single largest expense for most educational institutions. They are matched to the academic period in which teaching occurs.

title: Administrative & Support Staff Payroll
description: Recording monthly payroll for registrar, finance, IT, and facilities staff.
explanation: Administrative staff costs are a general overhead expense. Institutions often separate academic payroll from administrative payroll for management reporting.

title: Part-Time / Adjunct Lecturer Fees
description: Paying a visiting or part-time lecturer per course taught on a contract basis.
explanation: Adjunct lecturers are typically paid per course or per hour. They are not permanent employees, so EOSB does not accrue. Withholding tax may apply in Jordan.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — Teaching Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the employer's 14.25% SSC contribution for Jordanian teaching and admin staff.
explanation: SSC is a mandatory employer cost in Jordan. For educational institutions, it is allocated between academic and administrative categories based on the payroll split.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Teaching Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service gratuity for permanent Jordanian faculty and staff.
explanation: Long-serving faculty accumulate significant EOSB entitlements. Monthly provisioning avoids a large unexpected cost when senior academics retire or resign.

title: Staff Pension / Retirement Contribution
description: Recording the institution's contribution to a defined contribution pension scheme.
explanation: Defined contribution plans require the employer to make periodic contributions. The expense is recognized when the contribution is due, not when the employee retires.

title: Faculty Overtime / Extra Teaching Load
description: Paying faculty an additional stipend for teaching beyond their contracted hours.
explanation: Extra teaching load payments are common in understaffed departments. They are expensed in the semester they are earned, not when paid.

title: Annual Performance Bonus Accrual
description: Accruing year-end performance bonuses for academic and administrative staff.
explanation: Bonuses must be matched to the period of performance. If bonuses are for the academic year just ended, they belong in that year's expense regardless of when they are physically paid.

title: Annual Staff Medical Insurance Premium
description: Paying the annual group medical insurance premium for all permanent staff.
explanation: Annual insurance premiums are prepaid assets, amortized at JOD 4,000/month. This matches the insurance benefit to the period it covers.

title: Research Grant — Industry Partner
description: Recording a research grant from a pharmaceutical or technology company.
explanation: Industry-funded research grants are deferred as a liability and recognized as revenue when qualifying research expenditure is incurred or milestones are achieved.

title: Research Grant — Indirect Cost Recovery (Overhead)
description: Recovering a negotiated overhead percentage on a research grant.
explanation: Institutions negotiate Facilities & Administrative (F&A) rates with grant agencies. This overhead recovery offsets the real cost of housing and supporting research activities.

title: Grant Close-Out — Unspent Funds Returned
description: Returning the unused balance of a grant to the funder at project completion.
explanation: Grant agreements typically require unspent funds to be returned unless a no-cost extension is approved. The deferred liability is cleared and cash is remitted to the grantor.

title: Research Equipment Purchased (Grant-Funded)
description: Capitalizing specialist laboratory equipment purchased with research grant funds.
explanation: Grant-funded equipment is capitalized as a fixed asset. Depreciation is typically charged to the grant as an allowable cost over the equipment's useful life.

title: Research Consumables & Materials Expensed
description: Purchasing laboratory chemicals, reagents, and other consumables for ongoing research.
explanation: Research consumables are expensed immediately when purchased since they are used up in the research process. They are a direct grant expenditure.

title: Publication & Open-Access Article Processing Fee
description: Paying an Article Processing Charge (APC) to publish research in an open-access journal.
explanation: Open-access publishing fees are a direct research cost, often charged to the specific grant funding the research. Many funders mandate open-access publication.

title: Conference Travel — Research Grant Funded
description: Recording faculty travel to an international academic conference funded by a research grant.
explanation: Conference travel funded by grants is a direct research expenditure. It must be within the approved grant budget and follow the institution's travel policy.

title: IP Patent Application Cost (Capitalized)
description: Capitalizing legal and filing fees for a patent arising from institutional research.
explanation: Patent application and registration costs are capitalized as intangible assets. They are amortized over the patent's useful life (typically up to 20 years). Tech transfer royalties then generate income from this asset.

title: Classroom Equipment Purchase & Depreciation
description: Buying projectors, smart boards, and AV equipment for classrooms.
explanation: Classroom technology is a fixed asset capitalized at cost and depreciated over its useful life (typically 3-5 years for electronics). Regular replacement is a significant capital commitment for schools.

title: Campus Building Annual Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation charge on the school's owned building.
explanation: A JOD 15,000,000 campus building with a 100-year life depreciates at JOD 12,500/month. Buildings are the largest asset on a school's balance sheet and their depreciation is a significant non-cash expense.

title: Laboratory Equipment Capitalization
description: Purchasing specialist science or engineering laboratory equipment.
explanation: Specialist laboratory equipment has a longer useful life (10-15 years) than general IT equipment. It is depreciated separately to ensure accurate cost attribution by department.

title: IT & Computer Lab Equipment
description: Purchasing computers, servers, and networking equipment for student IT labs.
explanation: Computer lab equipment is capitalized and depreciated over 3-5 years. Schools face significant ongoing capex to keep labs current with industry-standard technology.

title: Campus Vehicle Purchase & Depreciation
description: Buying a minibus for student transport and field trips.
explanation: School vehicles are fixed assets depreciated over their useful life (typically 5-8 years). They are often financed through installment loans.

title: Furniture & Fixtures Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on desks, chairs, and teaching furniture across all classrooms.
explanation: Classroom furniture has a useful life of 7-10 years. Monthly depreciation is recognized to spread the cost of fit-out across the years it benefits.

title: Major Campus Renovation — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing a significant renovation of a science block that extends the building's useful life.
explanation: Major renovations that significantly extend the asset's life or enhance its capability are capitalized. Routine repainting or repairs are expensed. The line between the two requires professional judgment.

title: Asset Write-Off — Fully Depreciated Classroom IT
description: Removing old computers from the asset register once fully depreciated and no longer in use.
explanation: Fully depreciated assets are removed from the register by eliminating both the cost and accumulated depreciation. No gain or loss arises if the net book value is zero.

title: Security Deposit — Leased Campus Space
description: Paying a refundable deposit when leasing additional office or teaching space.
explanation: Lease deposits are assets, not expenses. They are held on the balance sheet until the lease ends and the deposit is returned or applied against damages.

title: Student Activity Fee Collected
description: Collecting a mandatory student activity fee to fund clubs, sports, and cultural events.
explanation: Activity fees are deferred and recognized over the academic term as activities are delivered. The timing must match the period in which student events and services are provided.

title: Sports / Gymnasium Membership Fee
description: Recording the semester fee for student and staff access to campus sports facilities.
explanation: Sports facility fees are auxiliary revenue recognized over the semester. They fund the operating costs of the gym, courts, and sports fields.

title: Student Counselling Service Fee
description: Charging a fee for sessions with the campus psychological counselling centre.
explanation: Counselling fees recover part of the cost of the student support service. Revenue is recognized per session delivered.

title: Student ID / Campus Card Fee
description: Charging a new student for their campus ID card and smart card production.
explanation: ID card fees cover production and administrative costs. They are recognized immediately since the card is produced and handed over at point of payment.

title: Campus Printing & Copying Revenue
description: Recording income from student printing credits used at campus print stations.
explanation: Students pre-load print credits. Revenue is recognized when credits are consumed at print stations. Unused credits are a liability until spent or the term ends.

title: Graduation Ceremony — Income and Direct Costs
description: Recording ticket sales for the graduation ceremony and the associated event costs.
explanation: Graduation ceremonies are significant institutional events that often run at a net cost. Income from ticket sales, gown hire, and photography is offset against venue, catering, and logistics costs.

title: Hostel Laundry / Vending Machine Income
description: Recording miscellaneous income from vending machines and laundry facilities in student residences.
explanation: Vending and laundry income is recognized when cash is collected from machines. It is ancillary revenue tracked under student housing services.

title: Student Transport Fee Collected
description: Collecting a semester fee from students using the school bus service.
explanation: Transport fees covering the full semester are deferred and recognized monthly as the service is provided. The monthly recognition matches the cost of running the bus service.

title: Campus Utilities — Electricity & Water Accrual
description: Accruing the monthly cost of electricity and water for the entire campus.
explanation: Campus utilities are among the largest operating costs for educational institutions. Accruing before bills arrive ensures the P&L reflects the true cost of each academic period.

title: Cleaning & Facilities Management Contract
description: Monthly payment to a facilities management company for campus cleaning and maintenance.
explanation: Outsourced FM contracts cover a wide range of services — cleaning, minor repairs, pest control, and grounds maintenance. The monthly fee is expensed as the service is received.

title: IT Systems & Software Subscriptions (SIS / LMS)
description: Annual subscription for the Student Information System (SIS) and Learning Management System (LMS).
explanation: SIS and LMS platforms are mission-critical for modern education. Annual subscriptions are prepaid assets amortized monthly. These are increasingly the highest technology cost for schools.

title: Library Journal / Academic Database Subscription
description: Paying the annual subscription for access to academic databases like JSTOR, Scopus, or IEEE.
explanation: Database subscriptions are prepaid assets released monthly. For research universities, this is a multi-million dollar annual commitment essential for faculty research and student learning.

title: Buildings & Liability Insurance Premium (Prepaid)
description: Annual insurance premium for campus buildings, contents, and public liability.
explanation: Comprehensive campus insurance is a mandatory operating cost. The annual premium is prepaid and amortized at JOD 2,000/month over the 12-month coverage period.

title: Marketing & Student Recruitment Costs
description: Spending on open days, school visits, digital advertising, and recruitment fairs.
explanation: Student recruitment is an investment in future revenue. These costs are period expenses recognized when incurred. Tracking CAC (cost per enrolled student) is a key institutional KPI.

title: Legal & Professional Advisory Fees
description: Paying legal counsel for employment matters, contract reviews, and regulatory compliance.
explanation: Legal fees are a general administrative expense. Fees directly related to a specific project (construction contract, grant agreement) may be capitalized into that project's cost.

title: Printing & Stationery Expense
description: Monthly purchase of paper, toner, exam papers, and general office stationery.
explanation: Paper and printing costs are a recurring administrative expense. Exam-period printing costs can spike significantly and should be budgeted as a seasonal cost.

title: Telephone & Internet Expense
description: Monthly cost of campus telephone systems, broadband, and Wi-Fi infrastructure.
explanation: High-speed campus-wide internet is now a core educational infrastructure cost. It is an operating expense recognized monthly.

title: VAT on Commercial Activities (Jordan)
description: Recording 16% VAT on commercial revenue streams — venue hire, cafeteria, parking — for a Jordanian school.
explanation: Educational services in Jordan may be exempt, but commercial activities (venue hire, car parks, external catering) attract 16% Sales Tax. The institution must register for VAT on commercial activities above the threshold.

title: Withholding Tax on Building Rent (Jordan)
description: Deducting 5% WHT when paying rent for leased campus space to an individual landlord.
explanation: Schools renting space from individual landlords in Jordan must withhold 5% of rent and remit it to the ISTD. The landlord receives the net amount.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision (For-Profit School)
description: Accruing the annual income tax for a privately owned for-profit educational institution.
explanation: For-profit schools in Jordan pay income tax on their profits. Not-for-profit educational associations registered under the Societies Law may have a different tax treatment.

title: Ministry of Education Annual Registration Fee
description: Paying the annual licensing and registration fee to the Ministry of Education.
explanation: Operating as an educational institution requires annual licensing. The fee is an operating expense recognized in the year it covers.

title: UBIT on Commercial Auxiliary Revenue
description: Accruing Unrelated Business Income Tax on commercial cafeteria sales open to the general public.
explanation: When a not-for-profit school earns income from activities unrelated to its educational mission (commercial catering, venue hire to non-students), that income may be taxable.

title: Zakat Provision — Private School (Saudi Arabia)
description: Accruing Zakat for a privately owned school operating in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
explanation: Private educational companies in Saudi Arabia are subject to Zakat. The base includes equity and certain adjustments. Schools owned by non-Saudis may pay income tax instead.

title: Import Duty on Laboratory Equipment
description: Capitalizing customs duties paid on imported scientific equipment.
explanation: Import duties form part of the equipment's cost and must be capitalized. Some educational institutions may qualify for duty exemptions on educational materials — subject to Ministry approval.

title: Bank Charges & Wire Transfer Fees
description: Monthly bank maintenance fees and charges for international wire transfers to overseas faculty.
explanation: International institutions frequently pay overseas suppliers and staff, incurring wire transfer fees. These are a recurring finance cost recognized monthly.

title: Campus Bond Interest Expense Accrual
description: Accruing monthly interest on bonds issued to finance campus construction.
explanation: Bond interest is a significant fixed cost for institutions with debt-financed facilities. It must be accrued monthly and is often excluded from operating expense ratios in management reporting.

title: Bond Sinking Fund Contribution
description: Making a mandatory monthly contribution to the bond sinking fund for future principal repayment.
explanation: Bond covenants often require institutions to build a sinking fund to ensure they can repay principal at maturity. The fund is restricted cash — it cannot be used for operations.

title: Semi-Annual Bond Coupon Payment
description: Making the semi-annual interest payment to bondholders.
explanation: This clears the accrued interest liability built up over six months. The semi-annual cash outflow is a significant liquidity event that institutions must plan for carefully.

title: Investment Income on Operating Cash Reserves
description: Recording interest earned on short-term deposits of operating cash reserves.
explanation: Institutions with large cash reserves often park them in short-term deposits or money market funds. Interest earned is unrestricted income that supports operations.

title: FX Gain on Foreign Tuition Fee (USD to JOD)
description: Recording an exchange gain when USD tuition from international students is converted at a favorable rate.
explanation: Schools enrolling international students and billing in USD are exposed to FX movement. When the dinar strengthens, USD receivables convert to more JOD than expected — an FX gain.

title: FX Loss on Foreign Vendor Invoice
description: Recording an exchange loss when paying an overseas software vendor in USD.
explanation: Paying international software, database, or equipment vendors in USD creates FX exposure. A weakening dinar between invoice and payment dates results in an FX loss.

title: Stale / Uncashed Student Refund Cheque
description: Reclassifying a student refund cheque that was issued but never cashed after 6 months.
explanation: Uncashed refund cheques reduce the bank balance but the obligation still exists. They are reclassified to a liability account until the student claims the refund or the funds are escheated to the government per unclaimed property laws.

title: Current Account Opened — Customer Deposit
description: Recording cash deposited by a new retail customer opening a current (demand) account.
explanation: Customer deposits are liabilities for a bank — the bank owes the money back on demand. The cash received increases the vault asset and creates a corresponding deposit liability.

title: Savings Account Deposit Received
description: A customer deposits cash into an interest-bearing savings account.
explanation: Savings deposits are slightly less liquid than current accounts. They are still a liability to the bank. The bank will pay interest on this balance, which accrues as an expense over time.

title: Fixed / Term Deposit Placed by Customer
description: A customer locks JOD 50,000 in a 12-month fixed deposit at 4% per annum.
explanation: A fixed deposit transfers funds from the customer's current account to a separate fixed deposit liability. The rate is locked and interest accrues monthly. The bank cannot use the label 'fixed' unless the customer cannot withdraw early without penalty.

title: Monthly Interest Accrual on Customer Deposits
description: Accruing the monthly interest cost on all fixed and savings deposits outstanding.
explanation: On a JOD 50,000 fixed deposit at 4% p.a., the monthly interest is JOD 167. Aggregated across all deposits, the bank records one blended entry. This is the primary driver of Net Interest Margin compression.

title: Fixed Deposit Interest Paid at Maturity
description: Paying the accumulated interest to a customer whose 12-month fixed deposit has matured.
explanation: This clears the accrued interest liability. The principal repayment is a separate entry. Interest payments have already been expensed monthly — this is the cash settlement of that liability.

title: Premature Fixed Deposit Break — Penalty Income
description: A customer breaks their fixed deposit early, incurring a penalty charged by the bank.
explanation: Early termination penalties compensate the bank for the disruption to its liquidity planning. The penalty is Other Income; the deposit principal is returned net of the penalty.

title: Certificate of Deposit (CD) Issued
description: The bank issues a negotiable Certificate of Deposit to a large institutional investor.
explanation: CDs are wholesale deposit instruments issued to institutional investors at a fixed rate for a fixed term. They are classified separately from retail deposits on the balance sheet.

title: Retail Loan Disbursed to Customer
description: Disbursing a personal loan to a retail customer.
explanation: A loan is an asset for the bank — it represents money owed to the bank. The disbursement reduces the bank's cash (or customer deposit balance) and creates a loan receivable.

title: Corporate Term Loan Disbursed
description: Funding a JOD 2 million corporate term loan to a manufacturing company.
explanation: Corporate loans are the largest asset class for commercial banks. The disbursement is credited to the corporate customer's operating account, from which they draw for business purposes.

title: Loan Origination Fee — Deferred (EIR Method)
description: Collecting an upfront loan processing fee that must be deferred under the Effective Interest Rate method.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, loan origination fees are not recognized as immediate income. They are deducted from the loan's carrying amount and amortized over the loan life using the Effective Interest Rate method, increasing the effective yield.

title: Loan Origination Fee — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the deferred origination fee into interest income each month.
explanation: A JOD 500 fee on a 12-month loan adds JOD 41.67/month to interest income. This ensures the income statement reflects the true economic yield of the loan, not just the stated coupon rate.

title: Syndicated Loan — Bank's Participation
description: The bank joins a syndicate, taking a 25% share of a JOD 20 million facility.
explanation: Syndicated loans allow banks to participate in large deals while sharing credit risk. Each bank records only its own participation as an asset. The lead arranger earns additional arrangement fees.

title: Revolving Credit Facility — Customer Drawdown
description: A corporate customer draws JOD 500,000 from their approved JOD 2 million RCF.
explanation: Only the drawn portion of an RCF is recognized as a loan asset. The undrawn commitment is an off-balance-sheet item for which a smaller ECL provision is required (Stage 1 — 12-month ECL on undrawn commitments).

title: Customer Overdraft Utilization
description: A business customer's current account goes overdrawn within their approved limit.
explanation: An overdrawn current account is reclassified from a deposit liability to a loan asset. Interest is charged daily on the overdrawn balance at the agreed overdraft rate.

title: Interest Income Accrual — Performing Loan
description: Accruing monthly interest on a current (Stage 1) loan that is paying on time.
explanation: Interest on performing loans is recognized on the accrual basis. A JOD 1,000,000 loan at 10% p.a. earns JOD 8,333/month of interest income regardless of when the customer actually pays.

title: Interest Income — Non-Performing Loan (Cash Basis)
description: Stopping accrual of interest on a loan classified as non-performing (Stage 3).
explanation: When a loan is classified as non-performing, interest accrual stops on the P&L. Any interest that had been accrued is moved to an 'Interest in Suspense' contra account. Income is only recognized when cash is actually received.

title: Interest Expense on Customer Deposits
description: Accruing the monthly cost of interest payable on all deposit products.
explanation: This is the primary funding cost for a bank. The spread between interest income on loans and interest expense on deposits is the Net Interest Margin (NIM) — the most important profitability metric in banking.

title: Interest Expense on Interbank Borrowing
description: Accruing interest on short-term funds borrowed from another bank in the interbank market.
explanation: Banks borrow from each other overnight or for short terms to manage daily liquidity. The interbank rate (similar to SOFR or LIBOR) determines the cost. This is a key treasury management tool.

title: Interest in Suspense — NPL Transfer
description: Transferring previously accrued interest to suspense when a loan becomes non-performing.
explanation: All previously accrued but unpaid interest on a newly classified NPL is reversed from the receivable and parked in suspense. This prevents overstating income on loans that are unlikely to pay.

title: ECL Stage 1 — 12-Month Expected Credit Loss Provision
description: Provisioning for performing loans (Stage 1) using a 12-month probability of default.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, all performing loans require a 12-month ECL provision from day one. The provision = Loan balance × PD (12-month) × LGD × EAD. This replaced the IAS 39 'incurred loss' model.

title: ECL Stage 2 — Lifetime ECL (Significant Credit Risk Increase)
description: Increasing provision to lifetime ECL when a loan shows significant deterioration in credit quality.
explanation: A loan moves to Stage 2 when there is a 'Significant Increase in Credit Risk' (SICR) — e.g., 30+ days past due, watchlist status, or sector stress. The provision jumps to lifetime expected losses, which can be 5-10x the Stage 1 amount.

title: ECL Stage 3 — Credit-Impaired Loan Provision
description: Provisioning at full lifetime ECL for a loan that is objectively credit-impaired (NPL).
explanation: Stage 3 loans have evidence of default — 90+ days past due, bankruptcy, fraud. The provision covers the entire expected lifetime loss, net of the expected recovery value of collateral.

title: ECL Provision Release — Loan Upgraded to Better Stage
description: Releasing part of the ECL provision when a previously impaired loan improves and moves back to Stage 2 or Stage 1.
explanation: Provision releases improve the income statement. When a customer restructures, repays, or their credit improves, the stage is reassessed and excess provision is released back through the P&L.

title: Loan Write-Off — Fully Impaired
description: Writing off a loan that is 100% provisioned and deemed unrecoverable.
explanation: A write-off removes the loan from both the gross asset and its matching provision. It has NO impact on the P&L (because the provision was already charged). It is purely a balance sheet clean-up entry. Recovery attempts continue after write-off.

title: Partial Write-Off of Impaired Loan
description: Writing off the unsecured portion of a partially collateralized NPL.
explanation: When collateral covers only part of the loan, the secured portion remains on the balance sheet while the unsecured portion is written off against the provision. This more accurately represents the recoverable amount.

title: Recovery of Previously Written-Off Loan
description: Cash received from a borrower whose loan was fully written off in a prior period.
explanation: Recoveries on written-off loans are recognized as income when cash is received. Banks maintain 'shadow records' of written-off loans and continue pursuing recovery through legal channels and debt collectors.

title: Letter of Credit Issued — Contingent Liability
description: The bank issues an import LC on behalf of a customer — commitment recorded off-balance sheet.
explanation: An LC is a contingent liability — the bank only pays if the customer defaults after goods are shipped. It is disclosed off-balance-sheet until the LC is drawn. ECL provisioning for undrawn LCs is required under IFRS 9.

title: LC Cash Margin Collected from Customer
description: Blocking a cash margin from the customer's account as collateral for issuing an LC.
explanation: The margin is a separate restricted deposit. It reduces the bank's credit risk on the LC. The customer's current account is debited and a specific LC margin account is credited.

title: LC Commission Income
description: Recording the issuance fee and confirmation fee charged for opening the LC.
explanation: LC commissions are typically 0.5-1.5% of the LC value per quarter. They are recognized over the validity period of the LC, not necessarily all upfront.

title: LC Settlement — Payment to Beneficiary Bank
description: The bank pays the beneficiary's bank when conforming documents are presented under the LC.
explanation: When the LC is drawn, the contingent liability becomes an on-balance-sheet loan. The bank pays the beneficiary and the customer now owes the bank. The LC margin is applied to reduce the loan.

title: Bank Guarantee (LG) Issued
description: Issuing a performance guarantee for a contractor client participating in a government tender.
explanation: Bank guarantees are contingent liabilities disclosed off-balance-sheet. The bank will only pay if the contractor defaults. ECL provisioning and margin deposits apply similarly to LCs.

title: Bank Guarantee Commission Income
description: Earning the quarterly commission for maintaining an outstanding performance guarantee.
explanation: Guarantee commissions are recognized ratably over the guarantee validity period. For a JOD 500,000 guarantee at 2% p.a., the quarterly income is JOD 2,500.

title: Documentary Collection Fee
description: Earning a fee for handling import documents and collecting payment from the buyer's bank.
explanation: Documentary collections are simpler and cheaper than LCs. The bank handles the transfer of ownership documents between importer and exporter banks for a flat or percentage-based fee.

title: FX Spot Trade — Buy USD (Bank's Trading Book)
description: The bank's treasury desk buys USD 1 million at the spot rate of 0.709.
explanation: FX spot trades settle T+2. The bank's treasury maintains a 'position' in each currency. The profit or loss arises from the difference between the buy rate and eventual sell rate or revaluation rate.

title: FX Spot Trade — Sell USD (Customer Transaction)
description: A customer sells USD 50,000 to the bank at the bank's buying rate.
explanation: The bank earns a spread on every customer FX transaction — the difference between the buy and sell rates. This is a major non-interest income stream for banks with active retail and corporate FX desks.

title: FX Forward Contract — Fair Value Movement
description: Revaluing an outstanding FX forward contract at month-end based on current forward rates.
explanation: Forward contracts in the trading book are marked-to-market daily. Gains and losses go directly to the P&L. For hedging relationships, gains/losses may go to OCI instead.

title: Trading Securities — Fair Value Gain
description: Revaluing the bank's trading bond/equity portfolio upward at month-end.

entries.account: Fair Value Gain on Trading Securities

explanation: Trading securities are measured at Fair Value Through Profit or Loss (FVTPL). Any change in market value goes directly to the income statement. Banks with large proprietary trading books have significant P&L volatility from this.

title: AFS / FVOCI Securities — Fair Value Change (OCI)
description: Revaluing the bank's Available-for-Sale bond portfolio, with the gain going to Other Comprehensive Income.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, debt instruments held at FVOCI have fair value changes in OCI (not P&L) until sold. This reduces P&L volatility for banks holding large bond portfolios. On sale, the cumulative OCI gain/loss recycles to P&L.

title: Held-to-Maturity Bond — Premium / Discount Amortization
description: Monthly amortization of the discount on a government bond held at amortized cost.
explanation: Bonds held at amortized cost (IFRS 9 AC category) use the EIR method. If purchased at a discount, the carrying value increases toward par value each month, boosting interest income beyond the coupon rate.

title: Repo Agreement — Securities Sold Under Repo
description: The bank raises short-term funding by selling government bonds with an agreement to repurchase.
explanation: A repo is economically a secured borrowing — the bonds stay on the bank's balance sheet (not derecognized) and the cash received is a liability. The repo rate is expensed as interest over the term.

title: ATM Interchange Fee Income
description: Earning interchange fees when another bank's customers use the bank's ATM network.
explanation: When another bank's cardholder uses your ATM, your bank charges the cardholder's bank an interchange fee (typically JOD 1-3 per transaction). This is a high-margin, low-cost revenue stream.

title: Credit / Debit Card Annual Fee
description: Charging the annual card fee to a customer's account.
explanation: Card annual fees are either recognized upfront (if the service is fully provided at issuance) or deferred and released monthly (if the fee covers ongoing card services over the year).

title: Wire Transfer / SWIFT Fee
description: Charging a customer for an outgoing international wire transfer.
explanation: Transfer fees are point-in-time revenue — the performance obligation is satisfied when the transfer is processed. SWIFT charges and correspondent bank fees are deducted from this income.

title: Monthly Account Maintenance Fee
description: Charging a monthly service fee on accounts that fall below the minimum balance.
explanation: Monthly fees are recognized ratably over the service period. Minimum balance waivers must be tracked to ensure fees are only charged when contractually permitted.

title: Safe Custody / Custodian Fee
description: Charging an annual fee for safekeeping and administering a customer's securities portfolio.
explanation: Custody fees are recognized over the period the service is provided. They are typically charged quarterly and are a key revenue stream for banks with private wealth or institutional custody businesses.

title: Investment Management / Advisory Fee
description: Earning a quarterly management fee on assets under management (AUM).
explanation: AUM-based fees are recognized over the quarter as investment management services are provided. Performance fees are only recognized when the performance hurdle is definitively achieved.

title: Tier 1 Capital — New Share Issuance
description: The bank issues new common shares to strengthen its regulatory capital position.
explanation: Common equity is the purest form of Tier 1 capital under Basel III. Banks periodically raise equity to maintain capital adequacy ratios above regulatory minimums set by the Central Bank of Jordan.

title: Mandatory Reserve Deposit — Central Bank (CBJ)
description: Transferring the required cash reserve ratio to the Central Bank of Jordan.
explanation: The CBJ requires commercial banks to maintain a mandatory reserve (currently ~7% of deposits). This restricted cash earns no interest and represents a real cost of banking — reducing funds available for lending.

title: Transfer to Statutory Loan Loss Reserve
description: Transferring a percentage of net profit to the statutory loan loss reserve as required by CBJ regulations.
explanation: CBJ regulations require banks to maintain a minimum statutory loan loss reserve. This is an equity reserve (not an expense), appropriated from retained earnings after the annual IFRS 9 ECL charge.

title: Dividend Declaration — Bank Shareholders
description: The board approves a cash dividend subject to CBJ approval.
explanation: Bank dividends require regulatory approval from the CBJ before payment. The liability is created at board declaration but payment may be delayed or reduced pending regulatory review of capital adequacy.

title: Subordinated Debt Issued (Tier 2 Capital)
description: The bank issues subordinated bonds to institutional investors to strengthen Tier 2 capital.
explanation: Subordinated debt qualifies as Tier 2 capital under Basel III. It ranks below depositors in a liquidation scenario, justifying a higher interest rate. It must have at least 5 years remaining maturity to count toward capital.

title: Rights Issue — Proceeds from Existing Shareholders
description: Existing shareholders exercise their rights to purchase new shares at a discount.
