explanation: Rights issues are the most common way for Jordanian banks to raise capital. Existing shareholders maintain their proportional ownership. The subscription price is typically set at a discount to market price to incentivize take-up.

title: Written Premium — New Policy Issued
description: Recording the full annual premium when a new motor or property insurance policy is issued.
explanation: Written premium is the total premium contractually due on a policy written during the period. It is the gross amount before reinsurance cession. Earned premium is recognized separately as time passes.

title: Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR) — Monthly
description: Deferring the unearned portion of annual premiums using the 1/365 pro-rata method.
explanation: For short-duration contracts (motor, property), revenue is earned over the policy period. A policy written on October 1 for one year has 9/12 unearned at December 31. The UPR represents future obligations to provide coverage.

title: Premium Instalment Payment Received
description: Collecting a quarterly instalment from a policyholder who pays in four equal parts.
explanation: Instalment payments clear the premium receivable. The full annual premium was written and partially deferred at policy inception — the instalment is simply the cash settlement of the receivable.

title: Premium Refund — Mid-Term Policy Cancellation
description: Refunding the unearned premium to a customer who cancels their policy before expiry.
explanation: When a policy is cancelled, the insurer refunds the pro-rata unearned premium. The UPR liability is reduced and cash is paid. Short-rate cancellations (with a penalty) may result in a smaller refund.

title: Reinsurance Premium Ceded to Reinsurer
description: Ceding a proportional share of risk and premium to a treaty reinsurer.
explanation: Ceded reinsurance transfers risk but is shown gross — the ceded premium is an expense (reducing net premium income) and a matching UPR ceded asset is created. Netting is not permitted under IFRS 4/17.

title: Unearned Premium — Ceded Reinsurance Asset
description: Recording the ceded unearned premium as an asset representing prepaid reinsurance.
explanation: The unearned portion of the ceded premium is an asset — it represents reinsurance cover still to be provided. It mirrors the UPR liability structure but from the cedant's perspective.

title: Co-Insurance — Premium Split Between Insurers
description: Recording the lead insurer's share of a co-insurance arrangement on a large property risk.
explanation: The lead insurer collects the full premium and remits the co-insurer's share. Each insurer only recognizes their own proportional share as revenue and bears only their share of claims.

title: IBNR Provision — Incurred But Not Reported Claims
description: Estimating and recording claims that have occurred but have not yet been reported to the insurer.
explanation: IBNR is among the most critical estimates in insurance accounting. Actuaries use development triangles and loss development factors to estimate claims that have occurred but not yet been filed. It is both a reserve and an expense.

title: Claim Admitted and Paid
description: Settling an approved motor accident claim by payment to the insured.
explanation: When a claim is settled, the reserve (set up when the claim was reported) is cleared against the cash payment. If the payment differs from the reserve, the difference is a claims development gain/loss.

title: Outstanding Claim Reserve — New Claim Reported
description: Setting up a case reserve when a new claim is received and assessed.
explanation: When a claim is reported, the claims handler sets a case reserve for the expected settlement amount. This is an actuarial estimate that will be refined as the claim develops.

title: Reinsurance Recovery on Paid Claim
description: Recovering the reinsurer's share of a large property claim that exceeded the retention limit.
explanation: Reinsurance recoveries reduce the net claims cost. The recovery asset is recognized when the insurer has a contractual right to receive from the reinsurer, typically when the claim is paid or admitted.

title: Salvage & Subrogation Recovery
description: Recovering value from salvaging a written-off vehicle or pursuing a third party who caused the loss.
explanation: Salvage (value recovered from the damaged asset) and subrogation (recovery from the at-fault third party) reduce the net claims cost. They are recognized when recovery is virtually certain.

title: Claims Reserve Release — Claim Closed Below Estimate
description: Releasing the excess reserve when a claim settles for less than originally estimated.
explanation: Favorable claims development — where actual settlements come in below reserves — is recognized as a credit to claims expense. This is a key driver of profitability in years with good claims experience.

title: Catastrophe Reserve Provision
description: Establishing a catastrophe reserve for potential large-scale events (earthquake, flood).
explanation: Catastrophe reserves are built up in good years to cover potential mass events. Under IFRS 4, some jurisdictions require this as a statutory reserve. Under IFRS 17, the classification may differ based on contract terms.

title: Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE)
description: Recording the cost of claims investigation, legal fees, and loss adjusters.
explanation: LAE covers all costs to investigate, settle, and administer claims — independent loss adjusters, lawyers, expert witnesses, and claims staff time. It is separated from claims payments to measure total claims cost.

title: IFRS 17 — Contractual Service Margin (CSM) at Inception
description: Recording the unearned profit (CSM) of a new insurance contract group at initial recognition.
explanation: Under IFRS 17, day-one profit is deferred as the CSM. It represents the unearned profit that will be released to P&L as insurance coverage is provided. This prevents 'day-one gains' that IFRS 4 allowed.

title: IFRS 17 — CSM Release to P&L
description: Releasing a portion of the Contractual Service Margin as insurance services are rendered.
explanation: The CSM is released to insurance revenue as coverage units are provided. A CSM of JOD 50,000 over a 12-month policy releases JOD 4,167/month. This is the core IFRS 17 profit recognition mechanism.

title: IFRS 17 — Risk Adjustment Change
description: Recording the change in the risk adjustment for non-financial risk each reporting period.
explanation: The risk adjustment reflects the compensation an insurer requires for bearing uncertainty. As time passes and uncertainty reduces, the risk adjustment decreases and the reduction is recognized as insurance revenue.

title: IFRS 17 — Premium Allocation Approach (PAA) Revenue
description: Recognizing insurance revenue under the simplified PAA for short-duration contracts.
explanation: The PAA is a simplified IFRS 17 approach for contracts of 12 months or less (most general insurance). Revenue is recognized in line with the passing of coverage period — essentially a straight-line release of the LRC.

title: IFRS 17 — Onerous Contract Loss Component
description: Recognizing an immediate loss when a group of contracts is onerous at inception.
explanation: If the expected costs of fulfilling a contract group exceed the premiums, the contract group is onerous. Under IFRS 17, the expected loss is recognized immediately — there is no 'day-one loss' deferral allowed.

title: IFRS 17 — Reinsurance Contract Held (CSM Asset)
description: Recording the CSM for a reinsurance contract held by the cedant.
explanation: Under IFRS 17, reinsurance contracts held by the cedant are measured separately and can have a CSM asset (when the expected net cost of the reinsurance is less than the premium paid — i.e., the reinsurance is 'good value').

title: IFRS 17 — Discount Rate Change on Best Estimate Liability
description: Recording the effect of a change in discount rates on the present value of future cash flows.
explanation: Under IFRS 17, long-tail claims (like liability or workers' comp) are discounted. When discount rates change, the BEL changes. Insurers can elect to recognize this in OCI to reduce P&L volatility.

title: Reinsurance Premium Paid to Treaty Reinsurer
description: Remitting the quarterly reinsurance premium to the treaty reinsurer.
explanation: This clears the reinsurance payable created when the ceded premium was accrued. Quarterly payments are standard for proportional treaties.

title: Reinsurance Ceding Commission Income
description: Earning a ceding commission from the reinsurer for the quality of the portfolio ceded.
explanation: Reinsurers pay ceding commissions (typically 20-35% of ceded premium) to compensate the cedant for acquisition and admin costs. This is an important income item that partially offsets reinsurance costs.

title: Reinsurance Recoverable on Paid Claims
description: Recording the amount due from the reinsurer after settling a large claim.
explanation: The cedant settles the full claim to the insured and then recovers from the reinsurer. The recovery receivable is recorded net of any deductible or retention. ECL on reinsurance recoverables is required under IFRS 9.

title: Funds Held by Reinsurer
description: Recording the deposit withheld from the reinsurer as security.
explanation: Some cedants withhold a deposit (funds held) from the reinsurer as security against their claims obligations. The cedant pays interest on this amount. It reduces the reinsurance payable but is still owed to the reinsurer.

title: Excess of Loss (XL) Reinsurance Premium
description: Paying the annual premium for a non-proportional XL treaty that covers catastrophic losses.
explanation: XL treaties trigger only when losses exceed a specified retention. The premium is a fixed annual cost regardless of whether the layer is triggered. It is amortized over the treaty year.

title: Quota Share Treaty — Proportional Risk Transfer
description: Setting up entries for a 40% quota share treaty where 40% of all premiums and claims are ceded.
explanation: A quota share treaty simultaneously transfers a fixed percentage of both premiums and claims. Both sides are recognized gross — the ceded premium is an expense and the claims recovery is a contra-expense.

title: Investment Portfolio — Bond Coupon Income
description: Accruing semi-annual coupon interest on the insurer's bond portfolio.
explanation: Insurers invest premium float in bonds, equities, and real estate. Investment income is the second major revenue stream after earned premium. Bond interest is accrued using the EIR method.

title: Investment Portfolio — Equity Dividend Income
description: Receiving dividends from equities held in the insurer's investment portfolio.
explanation: Equity dividends are recognized when the right to receive payment is established (ex-dividend date). They form part of the total investment return that funds claim payments.

title: AFS / FVOCI Portfolio — Unrealized Fair Value Gain
description: Revaluing the insurer's bond portfolio upward at quarter-end.
explanation: Insurers typically hold large bond portfolios at FVOCI. Fair value gains go to OCI, not P&L, reducing reported volatility. When sold, the cumulative OCI gain recycles to the income statement.

title: Investment Property Fair Value Gain
description: Revaluing a commercial building held by the insurer as an investment asset.
explanation: Under IAS 40 fair value model, changes in investment property values go directly to P&L. Many Gulf insurers hold significant real estate — making this a material income item.

title: Investment Impairment — Equity Security
description: Recording an impairment loss on an equity investment that has permanently declined in value.
explanation: Equity investments at FVTPL require fair value changes in P&L — losses flow automatically. For FVOCI equity instruments, impairment is not recycled to P&L but stays in OCI (IFRS 9 change from IAS 39).

title: Policy Loan Issued to Policyholder
description: Lending a policyholder up to 80% of their life insurance policy's cash surrender value.
explanation: Policy loans are unique to life insurance. The policyholder borrows against their own accumulated cash value. If not repaid, the loan (plus interest) is deducted from the death benefit or surrender value.

title: Deferred Acquisition Cost (DAC) — Initial Recognition
description: Deferring upfront agent commissions paid to acquire new insurance policies.
explanation: Under IFRS 17 and IFRS 4, incremental costs of acquiring contracts (primarily agent commissions) are deferred and amortized over the contract period. This matches the acquisition cost to the revenue it generates.

title: Deferred Acquisition Cost (DAC) — Monthly Amortization
description: Releasing one month of the deferred acquisition cost to the income statement.
explanation: DAC amortization spreads the cost of acquiring the policy over its life. JOD 18,000 DAC on a 12-month policy releases at JOD 1,500/month, matching the cost to the earned premium.

title: Agent / Broker Commission Expense
description: Recording commissions paid to insurance agents and brokers for new and renewal business.
explanation: Commissions are typically 10-25% of written premium depending on the line of business. Motor and health commissions are lower; liability and specialty lines are higher. Renewal commissions are typically lower than new business.

title: Underwriting Expense (Salaries & Overhead)
description: Recording the monthly cost of the underwriting and risk assessment department.
explanation: Underwriting expenses include staff salaries, software, risk data subscriptions, and office costs. They are tracked separately from claims and DAC to calculate the Expense Ratio — a key insurance KPI.

title: Policy Administration System Annual Subscription
description: Paying the annual fee for the core insurance policy administration and billing platform.
explanation: Policy admin systems are the core technology of an insurer. The annual fee is prepaid and amortized monthly. These systems are increasingly cloud-based SaaS platforms.

title: Postpaid Monthly Plan Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly subscription revenue for a postpaid voice and data plan.
explanation: Postpaid revenue is recognized as the service is delivered — one month at a time. The receivable is billed at month-end and typically collected within 30 days.

title: Prepaid Top-Up — Revenue Deferral
description: Recording a prepaid airtime top-up as deferred revenue until the credit is consumed.
explanation: Prepaid credit is a liability — the telecom owes the customer airtime, data, or SMS. Revenue is recognized as the customer consumes the credit. Unused credit that expires is recognized as breakage revenue.

title: Prepaid Credit Consumed — Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue as a prepaid customer makes calls or uses data.
explanation: Each minute of call, MB of data, or SMS sent reduces the deferred revenue balance and recognizes earned revenue. Billing systems track consumption in real-time.

title: Bundle Allocation — Handset and Plan (IFRS 15)
description: Splitting a 24-month contract bundle between the handset (hardware) and the ongoing service plan.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, a handset + plan bundle is two distinct performance obligations. The transaction price is allocated based on standalone selling prices. The handset component is recognized immediately at delivery; the service plan is deferred and released monthly.

title: Data Overage Revenue
description: Billing a customer for data usage beyond their monthly plan allowance.
explanation: Overage charges are variable consideration under IFRS 15 — recognized when the usage occurs and the amount is determinable. Telecoms face constraints in recognizing overage estimates before they are confirmed.

title: International Roaming Revenue
description: Billing a home-network customer for calls and data used while roaming on a foreign network.
explanation: Roaming revenue is charged to the home customer at the roaming rates. The home operator then settles the underlying termination cost with the visited network operator in a separate interoperator settlement.

title: Contract Modification — Customer Upgrades Plan
description: A customer mid-contract upgrades from a basic plan to a premium plan.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, an upgrade that adds distinct goods/services is treated as a new contract. The old remaining deferred balance is closed and the new higher-value plan commences.

title: Prepaid Credit Breakage — Expired Unused Credit
description: Recognizing revenue from prepaid balances that have expired without being used.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, breakage is estimated using historical data and recognized as revenue over the expected period of use. Significant breakage is common — many customers top up more than they use.

title: Interconnect / Termination Income
description: Earning termination revenue when another operator's calls are terminated on the network.
explanation: When a caller on Network A calls a subscriber on Network B, Network A pays Network B a termination rate. This wholesale revenue is settled monthly between operators based on CDR (Call Detail Record) data.

title: Interconnect Expense — Origination Cost
description: Paying origination charges to another network for calls originated on their platform.
explanation: Interconnect is both an income and expense. Net interconnect position depends on traffic flows. Mobile operators in small markets often pay more in origination than they earn in termination.

title: National Roaming Income
description: Earning income from a smaller operator who uses the network's infrastructure for domestic coverage.
explanation: National roaming agreements allow smaller operators (like MVNOs) to use an established network. The host charges a per-minute or per-GB rate. This is pure wholesale revenue with very low incremental cost.

title: Wholesale Capacity Lease — IP Transit
description: Leasing internet bandwidth capacity to ISPs and enterprise clients.
explanation: Wholesale capacity leases are recurring monthly revenue. They are recognized over the lease term as bandwidth is provided. This is core to any telecom with excess network capacity.

title: IRU — Indefeasible Right of Use (Fiber Capacity)
description: Selling a 20-year IRU on fiber capacity to another operator.
explanation: An IRU grants a customer the right to use fiber capacity for a long term (15-25 years). The upfront lump sum is deferred and recognized ratably over the contract term under IFRS 15.

title: Mobile Number Portability (MNP) Fee
description: Recording the administrative fee charged when a customer ports their number to another operator.
explanation: MNP fees are recognized immediately since the porting service is a point-in-time obligation. Some jurisdictions do not allow charging for MNP — making this entry applicable only where regulatory permitted.

title: Spectrum License Capitalization
description: Recording the acquisition of a 4G/5G spectrum license from the regulator.
explanation: Spectrum is the most valuable asset of a mobile operator. License costs are capitalized as an intangible asset and amortized over the license period (typically 15-25 years for 4G/5G).

title: Spectrum License — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the spectrum license cost over its licensed period.
explanation: An JOD 80M spectrum license over 20 years amortizes at JOD 333,333/month. This is one of the largest non-cash expenses in telecom, significantly impacting EBITDA-to-EBIT conversion.

title: Tower / BTS Equipment Purchase
description: Purchasing a new base transceiver station (BTS) for network expansion.
explanation: Network equipment (BTS, antennas, routers) is capitalized as PPE. Telecoms have massive ongoing capex requirements to maintain and upgrade their networks — this is the core capital-intensive nature of the industry.

title: Network Equipment — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation on all active network infrastructure.
explanation: Network depreciation is the largest expense line for most telecoms. 4G equipment typically depreciates over 7-10 years. High depreciation drives the classic telecom EBITDA focus rather than net profit.

title: Tower Site Lease — IFRS 16 Initial Recognition
description: Recognizing a Right-of-Use asset and lease liability when signing a tower site lease.
explanation: Mobile operators lease thousands of tower sites. IFRS 16 requires almost all leases to be on-balance-sheet. A large operator with 2,000 tower leases could have hundreds of millions in ROU assets and lease liabilities.

title: Tower Site ROU Asset — Monthly Depreciation
description: Monthly depreciation of the right-of-use asset for a tower site lease.
explanation: An JOD 180,000 ROU asset over a 5-year tower lease depreciates at JOD 3,000/month. Tower depreciation is the largest component of lease-related depreciation for mobile operators.

title: Network Upgrade — 4G to 5G Capitalization
description: Capitalizing the cost of upgrading existing cell sites from 4G to 5G technology.
explanation: Technology upgrades that enhance network capability are capitalized. Component accounting under IAS 16 requires removing the old 4G hardware's book value and adding the 5G equipment.

title: SIM Card Inventory — Purchase Cost
description: Recording the purchase of a batch of SIM cards from a manufacturer.
explanation: SIM cards are inventory items expensed when distributed to subscribers or dealers. The cost is typically very low (JOD 0.50-2 per card) but volume is high for large operators.

title: Handset Subsidy Cost
description: Recording the cost of selling a handset below cost to attract a customer to a 24-month contract.
explanation: The handset loss (JOD 200) is offset by the higher-margin service plan revenue over 24 months. Under IFRS 15 bundle accounting, the handset is allocated its standalone value, reducing the subsidy loss.

title: Dealer Commission — Subscriber Acquisition
description: Paying a retailer or dealer a commission for activating a new postpaid subscriber.
explanation: Dealer commissions are a major component of subscriber acquisition cost (SAC). They may be expensed immediately or capitalized as contract costs under IFRS 15 if incremental and recoverable.

title: CAC Capitalized as Contract Cost (IFRS 15)
description: Capitalizing incremental commission costs to obtain a contract customer.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, incremental costs to obtain a contract are capitalized if recoverable. For a 24-month postpaid contract, the JOD 35 dealer commission is capitalized and amortized over the 24-month contract term.

title: CAC Contract Cost — Monthly Amortization
description: Amortizing the capitalized customer acquisition cost over the contract period.
explanation: JOD 35 capitalized CAC over 24 months amortizes at JOD 1.46/month. This prevents the full subscriber acquisition cost hitting in month 1 of a 2-year contract, improving reported EBITDA margin.

title: Free Handset With Plan — Cost Allocation
description: Allocating the cost of a 'free' handset across the handset and service plan performance obligations.

entries.account: Cost of Goods Sold (Allocated to Handset POB)

explanation: Even a 'free' handset has a cost. IFRS 15 requires allocating the transaction price across all performance obligations. The contract asset represents the revenue already earned (on the handset) but not yet billed — to be recovered through service fees over the contract.

title: Airtime Fraud Write-Off
description: Writing off revenue lost to SIM box fraud or international revenue share fraud (IRSF).
explanation: Telecom fraud (SIM box, IRSF, subscription fraud) is a significant industry problem. When fraudulent traffic is identified, the related receivable is written off as a fraud loss. Prevention through real-time analytics is critical.

title: Billing Dispute Credit Note to Subscriber
description: Issuing a credit note to a subscriber for incorrectly charged roaming or data fees.
explanation: Billing disputes are common in telecoms. Credit notes reverse previously recognized revenue. Recurring billing errors indicate system issues that must be addressed to maintain customer trust.

title: Service Credit to Subscriber (Goodwill Gesture)
description: Crediting a customer's account with free data after a service outage.
explanation: Free credits for service failures are a customer retention cost. The credit is a liability until consumed by the subscriber. For accounting purposes, it is treated like additional airtime top-up.

title: Number Portability — Revenue Impact (Subscriber Loss)
description: Recognizing the revenue impact when a high-value subscriber ports out to a competitor.
explanation: When a subscriber ports out before contract end, the early termination fee (ETF) is charged and recognized. Any remaining deferred revenue for undelivered service is reversed.

title: Late Payment Penalty Income
description: Charging interest on overdue postpaid invoices.
explanation: Late payment charges are recognized when charged and are legally enforceable per the subscriber agreement. They are Other Income rather than core service revenue.

title: Universal Service Obligation (USO) Levy
description: Recording the mandatory contribution to the national telecommunications USO fund.
explanation: Most countries require operators to contribute to a USO fund that subsidizes connectivity in underserved areas. In Jordan, the TRC administers this. The levy is typically a percentage of gross revenues.

title: Annual Spectrum Usage Fee
description: Paying the annual frequency usage fee to the Telecommunications Regulatory Commission (TRC).
explanation: Annual spectrum usage fees are separate from the one-time license cost. They are prepaid assets amortized monthly. They represent the ongoing cost of the right to use the spectrum.

title: Regulatory Fine — TRC Penalty
description: Recording a fine imposed by the Telecommunications Regulatory Commission for quality of service violations.
explanation: Regulatory fines are expensed immediately. They are non-deductible for income tax purposes and are disclosed separately in financial statements as they indicate compliance failures.

title: Withholding Tax on International Settlements
description: Withholding tax on interconnect settlement payments to foreign operators.
explanation: Payments to foreign telecoms for interconnect, roaming settlement, and capacity leasing may be subject to withholding tax. Applicable rate depends on double taxation treaties between Jordan and the counterparty's country.

title: Telecom-Specific Tax Provision
description: Accruing telecom-specific sector taxes such as a telecoms excise tax on revenue.
explanation: Many jurisdictions impose sector-specific taxes on telecom revenue in addition to standard corporate income tax. In Jordan, this may include the general sales tax on services and specific levies on mobile services.

title: Seismic Survey Costs — E&E Asset
description: Capitalizing the cost of a 3D seismic survey conducted during the exploration phase.
explanation: Under IFRS 6, exploration and evaluation expenditures may be capitalized as E&E assets. Seismic surveys generate data about subsurface geology and are a direct exploration cost. The policy choice to capitalize or expense must be consistent and disclosed.

title: Exploratory Drilling — E&E Asset
description: Capitalizing the cost of drilling an exploratory well to test the presence of hydrocarbons.
explanation: Exploratory wells are drilled to test if oil or gas is present. The cost is capitalized pending determination of whether commercially viable reserves exist. If reserves are confirmed, the cost is reclassified to development; if not, it is written off.

title: Dry Hole Write-Off
description: Writing off the cost of an exploratory well that found no commercially viable hydrocarbons.
explanation: A dry hole is an exploration failure. When a well proves non-commercial, all associated E&E costs must be written off immediately. This is one of the highest-risk aspects of oil & gas accounting — explorers may write off many wells before finding a commercial discovery.

title: E&E Asset Reclassified to Development Asset
description: Reclassifying an exploratory well to a development asset after commercial viability is determined.
explanation: Once a discovery is confirmed as technically and commercially viable, the E&E asset is reclassified to PP&E or Intangible assets depending on the nature of the rights. Depletion then commences when production begins.

title: E&E Asset Impairment — Trigger Event
description: Writing down an E&E asset when indicators of impairment exist (e.g., licence expiry, decision to abandon).
explanation: IFRS 6 requires E&E assets to be tested for impairment when facts and circumstances suggest the carrying amount may exceed the recoverable amount — e.g., licence expiry approaching, no plans to develop, or the area is being relinquished.

title: Development Well — Capitalized Cost
description: Capitalizing the drilling and completion costs for a development well in a proven field.
explanation: Development wells are drilled after commercial reserves are confirmed. Their costs are capitalized to the oil and gas property and depleted over the proved developed reserves using the Units of Production (UOP) method.

title: Production Asset Depletion — Units of Production
description: Recording monthly depletion of the oil & gas asset based on barrels produced.
explanation: Unlike straight-line depreciation, oil & gas assets are depleted using UOP: (Cost / Proved Developed Reserves) × Barrels Produced. In a high-production month, depletion is higher; in a low month, it is lower. This matches cost to revenue generation.

title: Lifting Costs — Operating Expense
description: Recording the day-to-day costs of extracting oil from a producing well (lease operating expenses).
explanation: Lifting costs (pumping, maintenance, chemicals, water disposal) are the direct costs of producing oil and are expensed as incurred. They are distinct from development costs which are capitalized. The lifting cost per barrel ($/bbl) is a key operational metric.

title: Production Sharing Contract — Government Take
description: Recording the government's share of production under a Production Sharing Contract (PSC).
explanation: Under a PSC, the company first recovers costs ('cost oil'), then splits the remainder ('profit oil') with the government. The government's share is recorded as an expense, not a tax. The company only recognizes its own share as revenue.

title: Royalty Expense — Government / Land Owner
description: Recording the royalty payable on gross production to the government or mineral rights owner.
explanation: Royalties are typically 10-20% of gross production value, paid to the party that owns the mineral rights. They are expensed as production occurs and represent the cost of accessing the resource.

title: Crude Oil Inventory — Production Recognized
description: Recording crude oil extracted and placed into storage tanks as inventory.
explanation: Produced oil enters inventory at its cost of production (lifting costs). Revenue is NOT recognized until the oil is sold and control passes to the buyer. Inventory is carried at the lower of cost or NRV.

title: Pipeline Tariff Income
description: Earning tariff revenue from third-party producers who use the company's pipeline for transportation.
explanation: Midstream pipeline tariffs are fee-based revenue recognized as volumes flow through the pipeline. This is lower-risk revenue than upstream production as it does not depend on commodity prices.

title: Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) — Initial Recognition
description: Recording the present value of the estimated future cost to decommission an oil platform or well.

entries.account: Oil & Gas Properties (ARO Added to Asset)

explanation: IAS 37 / IFRS requires recognition of the decommissioning obligation at the start of production. The liability is the present value of estimated future decommissioning costs. An equal amount is added to the asset and depleted over the reserve life.

title: ARO Accretion Expense (Unwinding of Discount)
description: Recording the annual unwinding of the discount on the ARO liability.
explanation: The ARO is initially recorded at present value. Each year, as decommissioning approaches, the discount unwinds — increasing the liability. This 'accretion' is a financing cost, similar to interest expense.

title: ARO Revision — Change in Cost Estimate
description: Revising the ARO liability upward when new engineering studies increase decommissioning cost estimates.
explanation: ARO estimates are revised regularly as technology, regulations, and cost indices change. Upward revisions increase both the asset and liability. The additional asset cost is depleted over the remaining reserve life.

title: Actual Decommissioning Expenditure
description: Recording costs incurred to plug a well and restore the site at the end of its productive life.
explanation: When actual decommissioning occurs, the ARO liability is settled. If actual cost differs from the provision, the difference is a gain or loss. Effective ARO estimation can result in significant gains or losses.

title: Crude Oil Sale — Revenue at Market Price
description: Recording the sale of crude oil cargo at the prevailing market price.
explanation: Revenue is recognized when control of the crude cargo passes to the buyer — typically at the point of loading (FOB) or delivery. The significant margin between production cost and market price is the upstream business model.

title: Natural Gas Sales Revenue
description: Recording monthly gas sales under a long-term gas supply agreement.
explanation: Gas is typically sold under long-term take-or-pay contracts at a formula price. Revenue is recognized monthly as gas is delivered. Gas pricing formulas often link to oil prices or market indices.

title: Take-or-Pay Contract — Minimum Payment Revenue
description: Recognizing the minimum payment under a take-or-pay contract when the buyer fails to take contracted volumes.
explanation: Under a take-or-pay contract, the buyer must pay whether they take the gas or not. The payment is deferred as a liability until: (a) the make-up period expires, or (b) the buyer subsequently takes the volumes they paid for.

title: Oil Price Hedge — Swap Contract Fair Value
description: Revaluing an oil price swap used to protect against falling crude prices.
explanation: When a company hedges its oil production revenue using swaps, the derivative is marked to market monthly. For an effective cash flow hedge, the gain/loss goes to OCI and is recycled to the P&L when the hedged production is sold.

title: Oil Price Hedge — Settlement Received
description: Receiving a settlement payment when the hedge is settled and oil prices fell below the hedged price.
explanation: At settlement, the hedge gain is recycled from OCI to the income statement to offset the lower oil revenue received. The net effect is that the company receives approximately the hedged price regardless of market movements.

title: Underlift / Overlift Adjustment (JV Production)
description: Recording an underlift position where the company lifted less crude than its entitlement share.
explanation: In joint venture fields, each partner must lift exactly their share of production or an imbalance arises. An underlift means you have a right to take more oil later. Revenue is deferred until the physical barrels are lifted.

title: JV Cash Call — Paid to Operator
description: Making a cash call payment to the JV operator to fund development drilling.
explanation: In a joint operating agreement (JOA), the operator periodically issues cash calls to all partners. The non-operator records this as an advance that is replaced by actual capital expenditure once the JV accounting statements are received.

title: JV Cash Call — Received by Operator from Partners
description: The JV operator receives cash calls from non-operating partners to fund operations.
explanation: From the operator's perspective, cash calls received are a liability until the costs are incurred and charged to the JV. The operator is a fiduciary — the funds belong to the JV, not to the operating company.

title: JV Partner Billings — Monthly Cost Statement
description: The operator bills non-operating partners for their share of monthly JV operating costs.
explanation: The operator prepares monthly cost statements (JV billing statements) allocating actual costs to each partner per their working interest. The advance (liability) is converted to a billing receivable as costs are allocated.

title: Cost Recovery Entitlement — PSC
description: Recording the company's entitlement to 'cost oil' under a Production Sharing Contract.
explanation: Under a PSC, the company first recovers its capital and operating costs from a portion of gross production ('cost oil'). The cost recovery entitlement is recognized as revenue as the costs are recovered in each period.

title: Profit Oil Entitlement — PSC Share
description: Recording the company's share of 'profit oil' after cost recovery under a PSC.
explanation: After cost recovery, the remaining production ('profit oil') is split between the company and the government per the agreed PSC formula — often on a sliding scale based on cumulative production or R-factor (return on investment).

title: Farm-Out Transaction — Partial Interest Sold
description: The company sells a 30% working interest in a license to a third party in exchange for a carried interest.
explanation: A farm-out partially divests a license interest. The gain is the consideration received less the book value of the portion of asset disposed. Farm-outs are a key risk management and capital recycling tool in exploration.

title: Petroleum Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the special petroleum income tax applicable in GCC and MENA producing countries.
explanation: Oil & gas companies typically pay petroleum income tax at higher rates than standard corporate tax (55-85% in some jurisdictions). The tax is calculated on the 'ring-fenced' petroleum income from each license area.

title: Deferred Tax on E&E Assets
description: Recognizing the deferred tax liability arising from the temporary difference on capitalized E&E costs.
explanation: E&E costs are often immediately deductible for tax purposes but capitalized for accounting. This creates a taxable temporary difference. The deferred tax liability (DTL) is recognized at the applicable petroleum tax rate.

title: Royalty — Tax vs Accounting Treatment
description: Recording the difference between the royalty expense as per accounts and its tax treatment.
explanation: In some PSC structures, royalties are treated differently for tax and accounting purposes — creating timing differences. A deferred tax asset arises when the tax deduction occurs later than the accounting expense.

title: Resource Rent Tax (Petroleum Resource Rent Tax)
description: Accruing a resource rent tax that applies once the project achieves a certain rate of return.
explanation: Some jurisdictions impose a profit-based resource rent tax on top of royalties and income tax. It is triggered once the project achieves a specified threshold rate of return (the 'uplift' rate), capturing super-profits for the government.

title: Ring-Fencing — Separate Tax Calculation Per License
description: Maintaining separate taxable income calculations for each license area to prevent cross-subsidy.
explanation: Ring-fencing prevents companies from using losses from a new exploration area to reduce tax on a profitable producing field. Each license is taxed independently. This is a major feature of most petroleum fiscal regimes.

title: Motor Third-Party Liability (TPL) Premium Written
description: Recording the premium for a compulsory third-party liability motor policy.
explanation: Motor TPL is compulsory in Jordan and most GCC countries. The premium is written at policy inception. The UPR is calculated and deferred separately. TPL covers bodily injury and property damage to third parties.

title: Motor Comprehensive Premium Written
description: Recording the annual premium for a comprehensive motor policy covering own damage and TPL.
explanation: Comprehensive motor covers own damage, theft, fire, and TPL. It is the highest-premium motor product. In Jordan, the comprehensive rate is typically 2-4% of the vehicle value.

title: Motor Fleet Policy — Group Premium
description: Recording a single fleet premium for a company insuring 50 vehicles on one policy.
explanation: Fleet policies cover multiple vehicles under one contract at a negotiated group rate. The premium is written in full at inception; the UPR release follows the policy period.

title: Motor Own Damage — Total Loss Claim Paid
description: Settling a total loss motor claim where the vehicle is written off.
explanation: A total loss is declared when repair costs exceed a threshold (typically 75%) of market value. The insured is paid the insured value less any excess. The salvage vehicle passes to the insurer.

title: Motor Own Damage — Partial Repair Claim
description: Paying a garage directly for repairing a damaged vehicle under a comprehensive policy.
explanation: Partial repairs are settled directly with authorized garages. The reserve set at FNOL is cleared when the repair invoice is received. Any shortfall or excess goes through claims development.

title: Third-Party Bodily Injury Reserve Setup
description: Setting a large reserve for a serious TPL bodily injury claim with potential long-term liability.
explanation: Serious bodily injury claims are long-tail liabilities. The initial reserve is a conservative estimate that may take years to settle. Actuaries review these reserves regularly as medical reports, court judgments, and negotiation progress.

title: Third-Party Property Damage — Claim Paid
description: Paying a third party directly for vehicle or property damage caused by the insured.
explanation: TPL property damage claims are typically settled faster than bodily injury. Payment goes directly to the damaged third party, not to the insured policyholder.

title: Motor Policy Excess Collected from Insured
description: Collecting the policy excess (deductible) from the insured before releasing the claim payment.
explanation: The excess is the first amount of any claim borne by the insured. Collecting it reduces the net claim payment. The claims reserve should reflect the gross loss; the excess collection offsets part of the settlement.

title: No-Claim Bonus (NCB) Provision
description: Accruing the liability for premium discounts that will be applied at renewal for claim-free policyholders.
explanation: NCB is a loyalty pricing mechanism — policyholders earn up to 40-50% discount for consecutive claim-free years. The projected NCB cost is provisioned at year-end to match the benefit to the period it was earned.

title: Motor Salvage Vehicle Sold
description: Recording proceeds from selling a total-loss vehicle that became the insurer's property.
explanation: After a total loss settlement, the salvage vehicle is taken over and sold at auction. The proceeds reduce the net claims cost. Salvage management is an important cost recovery process for motor insurers.

title: Motor Subrogation Recovery — Third-Party at Fault
description: Recovering a paid claim from a third party who was legally responsible for the accident.
explanation: After paying the insured, the insurer can pursue the at-fault party for recovery (subrogation rights). Recoveries are recognized when virtually certain and reduce the net claims cost for the period.

title: Green Card / International Motor Extension Premium
description: Charging additional premium for extending motor cover internationally.
explanation: International extensions (Green Card) are add-on premiums for short-term cover abroad. They are written at full value and recognized over the extension period.

title: Commercial Property Premium Written
description: Underwriting an annual fire and perils policy on a commercial warehouse.
explanation: Commercial property policies cover buildings and contents against fire, explosion, natural perils, and accidental damage. Premium rates are set by the underwriter based on construction, occupancy, protection, and exposure.

title: Residential Homeowners Policy Premium
description: Writing a homeowners policy for a residential property including building and contents.
explanation: Homeowners policies are personal lines products covering building, contents, and liability. They are written in high volumes at low individual premiums — making data analytics critical for pricing.

title: Property Fire Claim — Building Damage Reserve
description: Setting up a reserve for a major fire loss at a commercial property.
explanation: Large property losses are immediately notified to reinsurers and a substantial case reserve is set. The loss adjuster's preliminary estimate forms the basis of the initial reserve.

title: Business Interruption Claim — Loss of Profit
description: Paying a business interruption claim for lost profits following a fire at the insured's premises.
explanation: BI claims compensate the insured for lost gross profit and increased cost of working during the indemnity period. They are complex to settle as they require detailed financial analysis of the insured's accounts.

title: Property Risk Survey Fee
description: Paying an independent surveyor to inspect and assess a high-value property risk.
explanation: Risk surveys are essential for large commercial properties. The cost is an underwriting expense. Some insurers charge the survey fee to the insured as a condition of cover.

title: Property Co-Insurance Penalty — Underinsurance
description: Applying the average clause when the insured's sum insured is below the actual property value.

entries.account: Cash — Net Claim Paid to Insured

explanation: If a building insured for JOD 500,000 is worth JOD 800,000, the insured receives only 500/800 of any claim (the 'average' principle). The insurer effectively only pays 62.5% of the loss. The difference reduces the net claims cost.

title: Property Reinstatement Value — Inflation Uplift
description: Adjusting the sum insured and premium upward mid-term for construction cost inflation.
explanation: Rising construction costs mean buildings become underinsured if sums insured are not indexed. Automatic indexing clauses generate additional mid-term premium that is recognized over the remaining policy period.

title: Group Medical Policy Premium Written
description: Underwriting an annual group medical scheme for a company covering 200 employees.
explanation: Group medical is the fastest-growing insurance line in Jordan and the GCC. Premiums are set annually based on the group's prior claims experience, demographics, and market trends. High claims ratios in medical make reserving and pricing critical.

title: Individual Health Insurance Premium Written
description: Writing an individual health policy for a self-employed customer.
explanation: Individual health policies are more expensive per head than group schemes due to adverse selection risk. Medical underwriting (health declarations) is applied at inception.

title: Medical Claim — Hospital Direct Billing
description: Recording a hospital invoice under a direct billing arrangement when an insured is admitted.
explanation: Direct billing networks allow insured patients to receive treatment without upfront payment. The hospital bills the insurer directly. Claims are expensed when the service is rendered, based on pre-authorization approvals.

title: Medical Claim — Reimbursement to Insured
description: Reimbursing an insured who paid out-of-pocket for treatment outside the network.
explanation: Out-of-network reimbursements typically require the insured to submit original invoices. The claim is validated against the policy benefits and paid subject to co-payment, deductibles, and benefit limits.

title: Medical Pre-Authorization Liability Accrual
description: Accruing a liability for approved hospital admissions not yet invoiced.
explanation: When a hospital admission is pre-authorized, the insurer has a present obligation even before the invoice arrives. Accruing approved but unbilled claims improves IBNR accuracy and ensures liabilities are not understated.

title: Medical Claims Inflation Reserve Top-Up
description: Increasing reserves to account for rising medical treatment costs mid-year.
explanation: Medical inflation consistently exceeds general inflation. Actuaries increase IBNR and outstanding reserves during the year when observed inflation threatens to make existing reserves inadequate.

title: Medical Co-Pay and Deductible Collected
description: Collecting the insured's co-payment share directly from the hospital network.
explanation: Co-payments and deductibles are the insured's share of each claim. In direct billing networks, the hospital collects the co-pay and remits confirmation to the insurer. This reduces the net claims cost.

title: Third-Party Administrator (TPA) Fee Expense
description: Paying a TPA for managing the medical network, pre-authorizations, and claims processing.
explanation: TPAs manage medical claims operationally for insurers who do not have their own networks. The fee is typically a percentage of premium or a per-member-per-month (PMPM) rate. It is a direct expense of the medical line.

title: Network Hospital Settlement Payment
description: Settling the monthly batch invoice from a network hospital for all direct billing claims.
explanation: Network hospitals are paid in batches — typically monthly or bi-monthly. The payment clears the accounts payable that accumulated from individual pre-authorized admissions throughout the period.

title: Life Insurance — Term Policy Premium Written
description: Writing an annual term life policy providing a death benefit with no cash value.
explanation: Term life is the simplest life product — pure protection with no savings component. The UPR is calculated and deferred. If the insured survives the term, the premium is fully earned with no benefit paid.

title: Life Insurance — Whole Life / Endowment Premium
description: Recording the annual premium for a 20-year endowment policy with savings and protection components.
explanation: Long-term life products require complex reserve calculations. Under IFRS 17, the premium is not simply written — it feeds into the cash flow projections for the CSM and BEL liability measurement.

title: Takaful Contribution Received — Participants Fund
description: Recording a takaful contribution paid by a participant into the Participants' Risk Fund.
explanation: In takaful, contributions are not 'premiums' — they are donations to a shared risk pool (the Participants' Fund). The fund belongs to participants, not the operator. The operator is a custodian/manager of the fund.

title: Takaful Wakala Fee Income to Operator
description: Recording the operator's management fee (wakala fee) charged to the Participants' Fund.
explanation: The takaful operator charges a wakala (agency) fee of typically 20-35% of contributions for managing the risk pool. This is the operator's revenue — similar in concept to an insurance company's expense loading.

title: Takaful Mudarabah — Operator's Profit Share
description: Recording the operator's share of investment profit earned on the Participants' Fund assets.
explanation: In the mudarabah arrangement, the operator invests the participants' funds and shares the investment profit — typically 30% to the operator and 70% to participants. This is a second revenue stream for the takaful operator.

title: Takaful Actuarial Reserve — Life Policy Liability
description: Calculating and recording the actuarial reserve for long-term family takaful policies.
explanation: Long-term takaful policies (family takaful) require actuarial reserves for the accumulated savings component and mortality liabilities. The reserve increases as the savings element grows.

title: Life Policy Surrender Value Paid
description: Paying a policyholder who surrenders their whole life or endowment policy before maturity.
explanation: Surrender values are less than the accumulated policy reserve — the difference (surrender penalty) is the insurer's gain for early termination. Surrenders are a major source of profitability or loss in life insurance.

title: Death Benefit Claim Paid
description: Settling a life insurance claim following the death of the insured.
explanation: Death claims are the core liability of a life insurer. They are paid to the nominated beneficiary upon production of the death certificate and completion of the claims form. Reinsurance recovery applies for large sum-insured cases.

title: Life Policy Maturity Benefit Paid
description: Paying the maturity benefit on a 20-year endowment policy at its expiry date.
explanation: At maturity, the policy reserve is fully released and paid to the policyholder. If the actual payment equals the reserve, there is no P&L impact — it is a pure balance sheet settlement.

title: Policyholder Dividend — Participating Policy
description: Distributing a share of the insurer's surplus to participating policyholders.
explanation: Participating policies entitle holders to share in the insurer's investment and mortality surplus. The dividend is declared annually and can be paid in cash, used to buy additional cover, or left to accumulate with interest.

title: Takaful Surplus Distribution to Participants
description: Distributing the annual underwriting surplus of the Participants' Fund back to eligible participants.
explanation: If the takaful risk pool has a surplus after paying claims and expenses, it is distributed to participants who did not claim — reinforcing the mutual aid principle. This is a key differentiator from conventional insurance.

title: General Liability Premium Written
description: Underwriting a commercial general liability (CGL) policy for a manufacturing company.
explanation: CGL policies cover third-party bodily injury and property damage arising from business operations. Claims are often long-tail — bodily injury suits may take years to settle.

title: Professional Indemnity (PI) Premium Written
description: Underwriting a professional indemnity policy for an accounting or law firm.
explanation: PI covers professionals against claims for negligence in their professional services. Claims are made-and-reported basis — the policy year when the claim is reported controls which policy responds.

title: Directors & Officers (D&O) Premium Written
description: Underwriting a D&O policy for a publicly listed company's board members.
explanation: D&O insures directors and officers against personal liability for corporate decisions. GCC demand has grown significantly following regulatory increases in directors' accountability.

title: Marine Cargo Premium Written
description: Underwriting a marine cargo open cover policy for a trader's annual import shipments.
explanation: Marine cargo covers goods in transit by sea, air, or land. Open covers automatically insure each shipment declared. Premium is typically based on cargo value × rate per mille.

title: Marine Hull Premium Written
description: Underwriting a marine hull policy on a commercial vessel.
explanation: Marine hull covers physical damage to the vessel. It is a specialist line requiring knowledge of international maritime law, Institute Clauses, and Lloyd's market practices.

title: Construction All Risk (CAR) Premium Written
description: Underwriting a CAR policy for a major construction project in Jordan.
explanation: CAR policies cover construction works in progress, plant, equipment, and third-party liability on construction sites. The premium is typically earned proportional to the construction schedule.

title: Workers Compensation Premium Written
description: Underwriting an employer's liability / workers compensation policy.
explanation: Workers comp covers employees injured at work. Premium is based on payroll and industry hazard class. Claims can be long-tail due to permanent disability and periodic payment orders.

title: Cyber Insurance Premium Written
description: Underwriting a cyber liability policy covering data breach and ransomware for a fintech company.
explanation: Cyber insurance is the fastest-growing specialty line globally. It covers first-party losses (ransomware, data recovery) and third-party liability (customer data breach notification, regulatory fines coverage).

title: Broker Commission Payable — Outward
description: Accruing the commission owed to the placing broker for new business written through the broker channel.
explanation: Brokers earn commission (typically 15-25% of premium) for placing business with insurers. The commission is an acquisition cost and should be treated as DAC if IFRS 17 capitalization criteria are met.

title: Premium Written — Broker Channel (Gross)
description: Recording a policy placed through an intermediary broker, with premium collected via the broker.
explanation: Broker-channel business often involves a credit risk on the broker (not the insured). The insurer recognizes the full gross premium as a receivable from the broker, who in turn collects from clients.

title: Mid-Term Endorsement — Additional Premium
description: Collecting additional premium when a policyholder increases their sum insured or adds a risk mid-term.
explanation: Mid-term endorsements adjust the policy. Additional premiums are written pro-rata for the remaining policy period and deferred using the UPR mechanism.

title: Mid-Term Endorsement — Return Premium
description: Refunding a premium reduction when a policyholder reduces their coverage mid-term.
explanation: Return premiums are negative written premiums. The UPR is adjusted and the refund is processed. Short-rate penalties may apply on voluntary reductions.

title: Renewal Notice — Premium Accrual
description: Accruing the renewal premium for a policy that has reached its anniversary date but payment is not yet received.
explanation: If the renewal terms are agreed and the risk continues, the premium can be accrued at the renewal date even if cash has not been received. This requires a credit risk assessment on the policyholder.

title: Lapsed Policy — Unearned Premium Released
description: Releasing the remaining UPR when a policy lapses due to non-payment of premium.
explanation: When a policy lapses, the insurer's obligation to provide future cover ceases. The remaining UPR is released to earned premium. The insurer retains the earned portion regardless.

title: Policy Reinstatement — Back Premium Collected
description: Reinstating a lapsed policy after the insured pays arrears of premium.
explanation: Reinstatement restores lapsed cover from the reinstatement date (not retroactively). The back premium covers the gap period if the insurer agrees to backdate the reinstatement.

title: First Notification of Loss (FNOL) — Initial Reserve
description: Setting an initial reserve immediately when a new claim is reported by telephone or online.
explanation: At FNOL, limited information is available. A 'system default reserve' or initial estimate is set based on the coverage and minimal facts. This is refined as the loss adjuster investigates. Speed of FNOL capture is critical for reserve adequacy.

title: Reserve Increase — Adverse Claims Development
description: Increasing a case reserve when new information indicates the claim will cost more than initially estimated.
explanation: Claims development is the process by which initial reserves are refined over time. Adverse development (reserve strengthening) is common for bodily injury and liability claims where final costs are uncertain.

title: Reserve Decrease — Favorable Claims Development
description: Reducing a case reserve when the claim settles for less than the reserve or evidence improves.
explanation: Favorable development is recognized as a credit to claims expense — reducing the expense charge for the period. Strong reserving discipline leads to more favorable development over time.

title: Ex-Gratia Payment — No Legal Liability
description: Making a goodwill payment to a claimant when there is no legal obligation under the policy.
explanation: Ex-gratia payments are made for commercial, reputational, or humanitarian reasons without admitting liability. They are disclosed separately as they represent payments outside the policy contract.

title: Claim Reopened After Closure
description: Reopening and re-reserving a claim that was closed but the insured has submitted additional costs.
explanation: Claims are sometimes closed prematurely and must be reopened. Reopened claims are a quality metric — high reopen rates indicate inadequate initial investigation or premature closure.

title: Litigation Reserve — Disputed Claim
description: Setting a reserve for a claim where the insured has initiated legal proceedings against the insurer.
explanation: When a claim is disputed and goes to court, a litigation reserve is set based on legal opinion. Legal costs (LAE) are reserved separately. The provision follows IAS 37 criteria — probable and estimable.

title: Large Loss Notification to Reinsurer
description: Recording the notified share of a large loss to be recovered from reinsurers.
explanation: Losses above the retention threshold are notified to reinsurers immediately. The recoverable is set up once the reinsurer acknowledges the notification. Treaty terms determine automatic vs. facultative recovery.

title: Claim Fraud Identified — Reserve Released
description: Releasing the full reserve when a claim is confirmed as fraudulent and will not be paid.
explanation: When fraud is proven, the reserve is fully released as no payment will be made. The release is a credit to claims expense. Fraud investigation costs (legal, SIU) are expensed separately.

title: Unexpired Risk Reserve (URR) — Additional Reserve
description: Setting an additional reserve when the UPR is insufficient to cover expected future claims.
explanation: The URR is set when the UPR is expected to be insufficient to cover future claims on current policies. It is required when the expected claims ratio for the remaining period exceeds 100% of the UPR.

title: Discounted Claims Reserve — Long-Tail Lines
description: Discounting long-tail claims reserves (workers comp, bodily injury) to present value.
explanation: Long-tail claims may not be paid for 5-15 years. Discounting to present value reduces the reserve liability. Under IFRS 17, all long-tail reserves are discounted. Under IFRS 4, discounting is optional but must be disclosed.

title: IBNER — Incurred But Not Enough Reported
description: Increasing reserves for claims where reported information suggests the reserve is inadequate.
explanation: IBNER covers the gap between what claimants have reported and what the final settlement is expected to be. It is a component of IBNR for claims already reported but where the full extent of loss is not yet known.

title: Run-Off Portfolio — Reserve Transfer
description: Transferring claims reserves for a discontinued line of business to a run-off reserve account.
explanation: When a line of business is discontinued, its remaining liabilities are placed into run-off. Segregating these reserves helps management track the remaining exposure and cost of the discontinued book.

title: Actuarial Assumption Change — Prior Year Development
description: Adjusting reserves due to a change in the mortality, discount rate, or inflation assumption.
explanation: Actuarial assumptions are reviewed annually. A change in mortality rates, interest rates, or cost inflation can materially change the reserve. The impact is recognized in the period the assumption is revised.

title: Periodic Payment Order (PPO) Liability
description: Setting a liability for a court-ordered lifetime periodic payment to a seriously injured claimant.
explanation: PPOs replace lump-sum settlements with lifetime annuity payments. The liability is the present value of all future payments, discounted at the risk-free rate. PPOs create very long-tail, inflation-sensitive liabilities.

title: Bond Investment Purchased at Premium
description: Buying a government bond at above par value (premium) for the investment portfolio.
explanation: A JOD 1,000,000 par bond purchased at JOD 1,050,000 carries a JOD 50,000 premium that must be amortized over the bond's remaining life using the EIR method, reducing the effective yield below the coupon rate.

title: Bond Premium Amortization — Monthly
description: Amortizing the premium paid on a bond purchase, reducing interest income each month.
explanation: Premium amortization reduces the bond's carrying value toward par over its life. Each month, the carrying value decreases and effective interest income is lower than the coupon received.

title: Equity Investment Purchase
description: Buying listed equities for the insurer's investment portfolio.
explanation: Equity investments are typically classified at FVTPL for insurers unless the insurer elects FVOCI. FVTPL means all fair value changes and dividends go through the income statement.

title: Equity Sold — Realized Gain
description: Selling shares from the investment portfolio at above their carrying value.
explanation: Realized gains are recognized on disposal. For FVTPL equity, this has already been partially reflected in prior fair value adjustments. The realized gain is the actual cash-out less book value.

title: Equity Sold — Realized Loss
description: Selling shares below their carrying value, recognizing a realized loss.
explanation: Realized losses reduce investment income. Significant realized losses can trigger solvency concerns if they erode the insurer's capital base below regulatory minimums.

title: Investment in Associate — Equity Method Pickup
description: Recording the insurer's share of profit from a strategic investment in an associate company.
explanation: Large insurers often have strategic equity stakes in banks, hospitals, or other companies. Under IAS 28, significant influence (20-50% ownership) requires the equity method — the insurer's share of profit increases the investment carrying value.

title: Investment Property — Monthly Rental Income
description: Earning monthly rental income from commercial properties held as investments.
explanation: Rental income from investment properties is recognized over the lease term. It is disclosed separately from underwriting income to show the investment contribution to total earnings.

title: Short-Term Deposit — Interest Accrual
description: Accruing interest earned on short-term bank deposits held for liquidity management.
explanation: Short-term deposits are a core liquidity management tool. Interest is accrued monthly. Insurers must comply with regulatory asset allocation rules — typically a minimum percentage in liquid assets.

title: External Investment Management Fee Paid
description: Paying the quarterly fee to an external fund manager for managing the bond portfolio.
explanation: Investment management fees are an investment-related operating expense. They are netted against investment income in financial reporting to show the net investment return.

title: Investment Portfolio Rebalancing
description: Selling bonds and buying equities to restore the target asset allocation.
explanation: Portfolio rebalancing maintains the insurer's target asset allocation (e.g., 60% bonds, 30% equity, 10% real estate). Rebalancing transactions trigger realized gains/losses on disposed assets.

title: Facultative Reinsurance — Single Risk Certificate
description: Ceding a specific large commercial property risk on a facultative (case-by-case) basis.
explanation: Facultative reinsurance is placed risk-by-risk when the risk exceeds treaty limits or requires specialist reinsurer expertise. Each certificate is a separate contract, more expensive than treaty but provides targeted capacity.

title: Inward Reinsurance Accepted — Premium Income
description: Acting as a reinsurer and accepting cessions from other insurance companies.
explanation: Large insurers also act as reinsurers, accepting risk from smaller companies. This is inward (or assumed) reinsurance. The premium income and associated claims are tracked separately from the direct insurance book.

title: Inward Reinsurance — Claim Paid to Cedant
description: Paying a claim to the original insurer (cedant) under an accepted reinsurance treaty.
explanation: When the reinsurer accepts claims from cedants, the claims are expensed against the inward reinsurance premium income. Combined ratios are calculated separately for the direct and assumed reinsurance books.

title: Retrocession Premium Paid
description: A reinsurer further cedes its own accepted reinsurance risk to a retrocessionnaire.
explanation: Retrocession is 'reinsurance of reinsurance.' Large reinsurers use retrocession to further distribute risk. It is the final layer of risk transfer in the global insurance/reinsurance chain.

title: Reinsurance Bad Debt — Reinsurer Default Provision
description: Provisioning for potential non-recovery from a reinsurer showing financial distress.
explanation: Reinsurance recoverables must be assessed for credit risk under IFRS 9. If a reinsurer is downgraded or shows distress, an ECL provision reduces the net recoverable. Reinsurer counterparty risk is a significant balance sheet concern.

title: Commutation Agreement — Reinsurance Settlement
description: Settling all past and future claims under a reinsurance treaty for a single lump sum.
explanation: A commutation extinguishes the reinsurance relationship for a fixed payment. It provides certainty for both parties but involves accepting a discount. The difference between the recoverable and the settlement is a gain or loss.

title: Underwriter & Actuary Staff Payroll
description: Recording monthly gross salaries for technical insurance staff.
explanation: Technical staff (underwriters, actuaries, risk analysts) are the core human capital of an insurer. Their salaries are typically allocated between policy acquisition and management expenses in IFRS 17 reporting.

title: Claims Handlers & Loss Adjusters Payroll
description: Recording monthly salaries for the claims department.
explanation: Claims staff costs are part of Loss Adjustment Expense (LAE) and should be allocated to the claims expense line. Efficient claims handling directly reduces the combined ratio.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Insurance Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service benefit for Jordanian insurance company staff.
explanation: Jordanian labor law requires EOSB provisioning for all staff. For insurance companies, the EOSB is an operating expense allocated across underwriting and claims functions.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — Insurance Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the 14.25% employer SSC contribution for Jordanian insurance company employees.
explanation: SSC is part of the true employment cost and is allocated between underwriting and claims categories in the same proportions as the underlying salary.

title: Insurance Premium Tax (IPT) Collected
description: Collecting insurance premium tax from policyholders and remitting to the tax authority.
explanation: In some jurisdictions, insurance premiums attract a specific Insurance Premium Tax (distinct from VAT). The insurer collects it on behalf of the government. It is not the insurer's revenue — it is a tax pass-through liability.

title: VAT on Insurance Ancillary Services
description: Charging VAT on non-insurance services provided by the insurer (policy admin fees, surveys).
explanation: Pure insurance premiums are typically VAT-exempt in most GCC and MENA countries. However, ancillary fees (policy issuance fees, survey charges, admin fees) may attract VAT as they are not insurance services per se.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the annual income tax on the insurance company's taxable profit.
explanation: Insurance companies pay corporate income tax on underwriting profit plus investment income. Technical reserves are generally tax-deductible when set, creating deferred tax timing differences.

title: Regulatory Solvency Margin — Capital Top-Up
description: Issuing new shares to maintain the minimum solvency margin required by the insurance regulator.
explanation: Insurers must maintain minimum solvency margins set by the Jordan Insurance Regulatory Commission (IRC). If capital falls below the minimum due to losses, a capital injection is required to continue operating.

title: Insurance Regulator Annual Filing Fee
description: Paying the annual license renewal and filing fee to the Jordan Insurance Commission.
explanation: Annual regulatory fees are a mandatory operating cost for licensed insurers. They are expensed in the period they cover.

title: External Actuarial Consultant Fee
description: Paying the annual actuarial valuation fee to an independent consulting actuary.
explanation: Independent actuarial certification of reserves is required in many jurisdictions. The fee is a management and governance expense. For smaller insurers, external actuaries are essential as full-time actuaries are too costly.

title: Office Rent & General Overhead
description: Monthly office rent, utilities, and general administrative expenses.
explanation: Office and overhead costs must be allocated between underwriting, claims, and investment functions for IFRS 17 management expense reporting. The allocation basis (headcount, time, floor space) must be consistent.

title: Core Insurance IT System Annual Subscription
description: Annual subscription for the policy administration, claims, and billing platform.
explanation: Core insurance systems (policy admin, claims management, reinsurance, actuarial) are the operational backbone of an insurer. Annual subscriptions are prepaid assets amortized monthly.

title: Notice Deposit (Call Money) Placed
description: Recording a large depositor placing funds in a notice account requiring 7-30 days notice for withdrawal.
explanation: Notice deposits earn higher interest than demand accounts but require advance notice for withdrawal. They provide more predictable funding than current accounts for the bank's liquidity management.

title: Interbank Deposit Placed (Surplus Liquidity)
description: Placing excess overnight or short-term funds with another bank in the interbank market.
explanation: Surplus liquidity is deployed in the interbank market rather than sitting idle. Placements earn the interbank rate (close to the policy rate). They are short-term assets managed by the treasury desk.

title: Interbank Deposit Received (Funding)
description: Borrowing from another bank overnight to fund end-of-day settlement requirements.
explanation: Banks borrow in the interbank market when they have short-term funding shortfalls. This is the primary mechanism for daily liquidity management alongside the central bank.

title: Fixed Deposit Rollover at Maturity
description: A maturing fixed deposit is automatically renewed for another 12 months at the current rate.
explanation: Rollover is a common customer instruction. The matured principal plus accrued interest is reinvested. The bank records a closing of the old deposit and opening of the new one — even if the net cash movement is zero.

title: Foreign Currency Deposit Opened (USD Account)
description: A customer opens a USD savings account and deposits $10,000.
explanation: Foreign currency deposits are recorded in the original currency. The bank's functional currency (JOD) financial statements translate these at each reporting date, creating FX translation adjustments.

title: Foreign Currency Deposit — Month-End FX Revaluation
description: Revaluing USD deposit liabilities to JOD at the month-end closing spot rate.
explanation: All monetary items denominated in foreign currencies are retranslated at the closing rate under IAS 21. When the USD strengthens against JOD, the liability in JOD terms increases — creating an FX loss for the bank.

title: Home Mortgage Loan — Disbursement
description: Disbursing a 20-year mortgage to a retail customer purchasing an apartment.
explanation: Mortgages are long-term secured loans. The property serves as collateral. Under IFRS 9, the origination fee is deducted from the initial carrying amount and amortized using the EIR over the 20-year term.

title: Home Mortgage — Monthly Principal + Interest Collection
description: Collecting the monthly mortgage installment from the borrower.
explanation: Each mortgage payment clears the accrued interest first (already on the income statement) and reduces the principal. Early in the mortgage term, most of the payment is interest; later, most is principal — classic amortization behavior.

title: Auto Loan Disbursement
description: Funding a 5-year vehicle loan with payment going directly to the dealership.
explanation: Auto loans are typically disbursed directly to the dealership, not the customer. The bank holds a lien on the vehicle as collateral. The vehicle registration is in the customer's name.

title: Personal Loan — Monthly Installment Collected
description: Collecting a scheduled monthly installment on an unsecured personal loan.
explanation: Personal loan collections reduce the outstanding principal and clear accrued interest. If a payment is missed, the overdue amount flows into the NPL classification and staging process.

title: Credit Card Limit Granted — Off-Balance Sheet
description: Approving and issuing a JOD 5,000 credit card limit to a qualified customer.
explanation: An undrawn credit card limit is an off-balance-sheet commitment. Under IFRS 9, a Stage 1 ECL provision is required on the undrawn commitment, reflecting the 12-month probability that some of it will be drawn and default.

title: Credit Card Purchase — Billed to Cardholder
description: A cardholder's purchases during the billing cycle are posted to their card account.
explanation: When a cardholder makes a purchase, the undrawn limit decreases and an on-balance-sheet receivable is created. The bank has funded the merchant through the card scheme settlement.

title: Credit Card Payment Received
description: A cardholder pays their monthly statement balance in full.
explanation: Full payment clears the credit card receivable and restores the full approved limit. If payment is partial, the revolving balance accrues interest at the card rate until fully repaid.

title: Credit Card Overlimit Fee Charged
description: Charging a fee when a cardholder's balance exceeds their approved credit limit.
explanation: Overlimit fees are recognized immediately. Banks must clearly disclose this fee in card agreements. Some regulators limit or prohibit overlimit fees as a consumer protection measure.

title: Credit Card Late Payment Fee
description: Charging a penalty fee when the minimum payment is not received by the due date.
explanation: Late payment fees are a significant revenue stream for credit card portfolios. They are recognized when charged. Regulatory caps on fee amounts apply in many jurisdictions including Jordan.

title: Credit Card Cashback / Rewards Points Accrual
description: Accruing the liability for cashback and rewards earned by cardholders on their spending.
