explanation: Cashback and points programs are a cost of acquiring and retaining cardholders. The liability is recognized as spending occurs. Under IFRS 15, breakage on unredeemed points is released to income using historical rates.

title: Consumer Loan — Early Repayment Penalty
description: Collecting a penalty when a borrower repays a personal loan before its scheduled maturity.
explanation: Prepayment penalties compensate the bank for lost future interest income. Under IFRS 9, the EIR is recalculated if the modification is non-substantial; otherwise the loan is derecognized and a new one recognized.

title: Loan Restructuring — IFRS 9 Modification Gain/Loss
description: Restructuring a distressed borrower's loan with extended tenor and reduced installments.
explanation: Under IFRS 9, a non-substantial modification requires recalculating the carrying amount as the PV of revised cash flows at the original EIR. If lower than the old carrying amount, the difference is an immediate modification loss.

title: Corporate Term Loan — Quarterly Repayment
description: Collecting the quarterly principal repayment plus interest on a corporate term loan.
explanation: Quarterly repayments on term loans are standard for corporate borrowers. The collection splits between principal (reduces the balance sheet asset) and interest (clears the accrued receivable already in P&L).

title: Corporate Term Loan — Bullet Repayment at Maturity
description: Receiving full repayment of principal when a bullet (non-amortizing) corporate loan matures.
explanation: Bullet loans require full principal repayment on maturity. Refinancing risk is significant for borrowers — and credit risk for the bank. The bank must monitor well in advance whether the borrower can refinance or repay.

title: Project Finance — Construction Drawdown
description: Funding a tranche of a project finance facility against certified construction progress.
explanation: Project finance loans are drawn in tranches against certified progress. The bank typically appoints a monitoring engineer to certify each drawdown request. Interest during construction (IDC) is capitalized to the loan.

title: Working Capital Facility — Annual Renewal Fee
description: Earning a renewal fee when a corporate overdraft facility is renewed for another year.
explanation: Annual renewal fees are recognized immediately on renewal as the bank's performance obligation (credit assessment and facility extension) is completed at that point.

title: Invoice Discounting / Supply Chain Finance
description: Purchasing a corporate supplier's invoices at a discount to provide early payment.
explanation: The bank pays the supplier 98% of the invoice. When the buyer pays at maturity, the bank collects 100%. The 2% discount income is amortized over the invoice tenor using the EIR method.

title: Watch List — Stage 2 SICR Trigger
description: Moving a borrower to the watch list following a Significant Increase in Credit Risk.
explanation: When SICR is identified (30+ days overdue, industry stress, financial ratio deterioration), the loan moves to Stage 2 and the provision jumps from 12-month to lifetime ECL. This is a significant and often sudden P&L impact.

title: Debt-to-Equity Swap — NPL Converted to Shares
description: Converting a large non-performing corporate loan into equity shares of the borrower company.
explanation: Debt-equity swaps are an NPL workout strategy. The loan is extinguished and equity is received at fair value. The difference versus the net book value of the loan (after provisions) is a further gain or loss.

title: Foreclosure — Collateral Property Taken Over
description: Taking possession of mortgaged property after a borrower defaults and legal proceedings complete.
explanation: When foreclosure is completed, the loan is extinguished and the property is recognized at fair value. Any remaining loan balance not covered by the property value (after applying the provision) is an additional loss.

title: OREO (Foreclosed Property) — Monthly Depreciation
description: Recording depreciation on a commercial building held as Other Real Estate Owned.
explanation: OREO is carried at the lower of cost or fair value less costs to sell. Depreciable buildings continue to depreciate until disposal. Regulators typically require banks to dispose of OREO within a defined period (2-3 years in Jordan).

title: OREO Sold — Gain on Disposal
description: Selling a foreclosed commercial property above its carrying (book) value.
explanation: Gains on OREO disposal are recognized when the sale completes. Banks actively manage OREO portfolios to minimize carrying costs and avoid regulatory concentration limits on non-core assets.

title: NPL Debt Restructuring — Principal Haircut
description: Accepting a reduction in principal owed as part of an NPL workout agreement.
explanation: A principal concession extinguishes part of the debt. The haircut is absorbed by the existing ECL provision. If the provision is insufficient, additional impairment is charged immediately.

title: Murabaha — Bank Purchases Asset from Supplier
description: An Islamic bank purchases goods from a supplier on behalf of a customer under Murabaha.
explanation: In a Murabaha, the bank must genuinely own the asset before selling it to the customer. This brief ownership is essential for Shariah compliance — the bank bears title risk, however momentarily.

title: Murabaha — Sale to Customer at Cost Plus Markup
description: Selling the Murabaha asset to the customer at cost plus the agreed profit margin.
explanation: The Murabaha markup (JOD 15,000) is deferred as a liability. Under AAOIFI standards and IFRS, this is recognized over the installment period using the EIR method — not recognized as immediate income at the point of sale.

title: Murabaha Installment Collected
description: Collecting a monthly Murabaha installment from the customer.
explanation: Each installment reduces the receivable and releases deferred income to revenue. The income recognition mirrors the EIR method for conventional loans — ensuring consistent P&L treatment across Islamic and conventional products.

title: Ijara — Bank Purchases Asset for Leasing
description: An Islamic bank purchases a vehicle to lease to a customer under an Ijara arrangement.
explanation: In an Ijara, the bank owns the asset throughout the lease term. Unlike Murabaha, ownership does not transfer at inception. The bank bears ownership risks and must depreciate the asset on its own books.

title: Ijara — Monthly Rental Income
description: Collecting the monthly rental payment from the lessee under an Islamic lease.
explanation: Ijara rental income is recognized over the lease term as the usufruct (right to use) is delivered. This is economically equivalent to interest income in conventional banking but is structured as a rental for Shariah compliance.

title: Ijara Asset — Monthly Depreciation
description: Depreciating the leased asset held on the bank's balance sheet under an Ijara arrangement.
explanation: The bank owns and depreciates the Ijara asset. The net income is the rental minus depreciation. This is a key structural difference from conventional leasing and adds complexity to the bank's fixed asset management.

title: Ijara Muntahia Bittamleek — Title Transfer at End
description: Transferring ownership of the leased asset to the customer at the end of the Ijara term.
explanation: At term end, the fully depreciated asset is gifted or sold for a nominal amount to the customer. The bank removes both the asset and its accumulated depreciation. No gain or loss arises if the asset is fully depreciated.

title: Musharaka — Bank's Capital Contribution
description: An Islamic bank contributes capital to a joint venture with a customer under Musharaka.
explanation: In a Musharaka, the bank and customer are co-owners sharing profits and losses. The bank's contribution is an equity investment, not a loan. Profit-sharing ratios are agreed upfront; loss-sharing follows the capital ratio strictly.

title: Musharaka — Bank's Share of Profit
description: Receiving the bank's agreed share of Musharaka profits at the quarterly distribution.
explanation: Musharaka profit is distributed per the agreed profit-sharing ratio — not the capital ratio. This distinction is fundamental to Shariah compliance. Losses, however, must be shared strictly in proportion to capital contributed.

title: Sukuk Investment — Purchased
description: Purchasing a sovereign or corporate Sukuk for the investment portfolio.
explanation: Sukuk represent ownership in underlying assets. Despite this Shariah distinction, they are accounted for under IFRS 9 based on the business model and cash flow tests — classified as AC, FVOCI, or FVTPL.

title: Sukuk — Periodic Profit Payment Received
description: Receiving the periodic profit distribution on a held Sukuk instrument.
explanation: Sukuk profit payments (economically equivalent to bond coupons) are accrued monthly using the EIR method and collected when distributed. The income recognition is functionally identical to a conventional bond.

title: Diminishing Musharaka — Customer Buys Bank's Unit
description: The customer purchases a unit of the jointly owned property from the bank, reducing the bank's share.
explanation: In Diminishing Musharaka (common for home finance in Jordan/GCC), the customer gradually buys out the bank's share. Each unit purchase reduces the bank's ownership and the rental income decreases proportionally.

title: Export LC — Advising Fee Income
description: Earning a fee for advising (authenticating and transmitting) a foreign LC to the beneficiary.
explanation: The advising bank verifies the LC's authenticity and transmits it to the exporter beneficiary. This earns a flat fee. The advising bank takes no credit risk unless it also confirms the LC.

title: Export LC — Negotiation / Discounting
description: The bank pays the exporter immediately against conforming documents, before receiving payment from the issuing bank.
explanation: LC negotiation provides exporters with immediate liquidity. The bank pays net of a discount and collects the full LC value from the issuing bank at maturity. The discount is income earned over the negotiation period using EIR.

title: Standby Letter of Credit (SBLC) Issued
description: Issuing a standby LC as a payment guarantee for a customer's performance obligation.
explanation: SBLCs are payment guarantees drawn only on default. Unlike commercial LCs which are drawn routinely, SBLCs are a true last-resort instrument. The fee is recognized over the validity period.

title: Banker's Acceptance — Bill of Exchange Accepted
description: The bank accepts (guarantees payment on) a trade bill, creating a banker's acceptance.
explanation: A banker's acceptance is the bank's unconditional promise to pay the bill at maturity. It creates a contingent on-balance-sheet liability when drawn and is highly liquid in secondary markets.

title: Central Bank Bills (CBJ Bills) Purchased
description: Purchasing short-term Central Bank of Jordan bills for liquidity management.
explanation: CBJ bills are risk-free liquid assets that count toward the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR). They are classified as FVOCI or amortized cost. Holding CBJ bills is often part of regulatory compliance and treasury strategy.

title: Government Bond — Coupon Received
description: Collecting the semi-annual coupon on Jordanian government bonds held in the portfolio.
explanation: Government bond coupons clear the accrued interest receivable built up monthly using the EIR method. The income was already recognized in P&L — this is the cash settlement of that receivable.

title: Interest Rate Swap — Fair Value Movement
description: Revaluing an interest rate swap used to convert fixed-rate loan income to floating.
explanation: Banks use IRS to manage interest rate risk on their loan and deposit books. If used as a fair value hedge, the gain/loss goes to P&L along with the offsetting hedged item adjustment, minimizing net volatility.

title: Cross-Currency Swap — Fair Value (Cash Flow Hedge)
description: Revaluing a USD/JOD cross-currency swap used to hedge foreign currency bond issuance.

entries.account: FX Hedge Reserve (OCI — Cash Flow Hedge)

explanation: Cross-currency swaps are used when banks issue foreign currency bonds. As an effective cash flow hedge, the fair value gain goes to OCI and is recycled to P&L when the hedged cash flows (coupon payments) occur.

title: Nostro Account Reconciliation — In-Transit Item
description: Adjusting the books for items in transit between the bank and its correspondent bank.
explanation: Nostro accounts frequently have in-transit items. A suspense account holds unmatched items until they are identified and cleared. Unreconciled nostros are a key audit risk and require daily management.

title: Overnight Interbank Lending (Money Market)
description: Lending JOD 50 million overnight to another bank at the interbank offered rate.
explanation: Overnight lending is the most liquid money market instrument. It earns the daily interbank rate and matures the next business day. Banks with surplus liquidity place funds overnight rather than holding excess reserves.

title: Reverse Repo — Securities Purchased Under Repo
description: The bank lends cash and receives government securities as collateral in a reverse repo.
explanation: In a reverse repo, the bank is the cash lender and receives securities as collateral. The cash is a short-term secured loan. The repo rate is accrued as interest income over the repo term.

title: Card Interchange Income — Issuer Revenue
description: Earning interchange income when a cardholder makes a purchase at a merchant.
explanation: The issuing bank earns interchange (typically 1-2% of transaction value) from the acquiring bank on every card purchase. This is the primary revenue driver for credit card portfolios and is recognized when transactions are processed.

title: Merchant Acquiring Fee Income
description: Earning merchant discount fees from businesses accepting card payments via the bank's POS terminals.
explanation: Acquiring banks charge merchants a merchant discount rate (MDR) of typically 1.5-3% of transaction value. After paying interchange to the issuer, the spread is the acquiring bank's margin.

title: Card Scheme Assessment Fee Paid (Visa / Mastercard)
description: Paying quarterly assessment fees to Visa or Mastercard for participation in their payment network.
explanation: Visa and Mastercard charge member banks assessment fees based on transaction volumes. These are a direct cost of operating a card business and are typically reported net against interchange income.

title: Card Chargeback — Dispute Processed
description: Processing a cardholder dispute that results in reversing the original transaction.
explanation: Chargebacks reverse the original card transaction. The issuing bank reverses the interchange income and refunds the cardholder. High chargeback rates from a merchant trigger card scheme investigation and penalties.

title: Card Fraud Loss — Write-Off
description: Writing off a fraudulent card transaction that cannot be recovered from the merchant.
explanation: Card fraud losses not recoverable through chargeback are expensed immediately. Fraud management through real-time monitoring, 3D Secure, and EMV chip technology is critical to controlling this cost.

title: Cash Vault — Physical Count Overage
description: Adjusting for extra cash found during the end-of-day vault count.
explanation: Cash counting differences are recognized immediately. Overages and shortages are investigated and documented. Patterns indicate teller error, fraud, or system issues. Daily cash counts are a fundamental internal control.

title: Cash Vault — Shortage Write-Off
description: Writing off a cash shortage discovered during the daily vault count.
explanation: Cash shortages are expensed. If attributed to a specific teller, recovery may be pursued per employment contract. Large unexplained shortages trigger immediate investigation and potential regulatory reporting.

title: Cheque Clearing — Presented for Payment
description: Presenting a cheque received from a customer for clearing through the national clearing system.
explanation: At presentation, a provisional credit is given and the cheque enters the clearing cycle. Final credit is confirmed when the drawee bank honors the cheque (typically T+1 or T+2 in Jordan's ACH system).

title: Cheque Returned — Insufficient Funds (NSF)
description: Reversing the clearing entry when a cheque is returned unpaid due to insufficient funds.
explanation: Returned cheques reverse the provisional credit and generate an NSF fee. In Jordan, returned cheques carry potential criminal liability — making this a critical credit risk and compliance matter.

title: Outward Remittance — Fee Income
description: Charging a fee for sending an international money transfer on behalf of a customer.
explanation: Remittances are a major revenue stream for Jordanian banks given the large diaspora population sending money to family. Competition from fintechs has compressed fees but banks retain trust and compliance advantages.

title: Inward Remittance — Corridor Fee Earned
description: Earning a commission on inward remittances received from overseas correspondents.
explanation: Jordan receives significant inward remittances from workers in the GCC. Banks earn a fee on inward receipts either through the exchange rate spread or a flat processing charge.

title: Loan Commitment Fee Income
description: Earning a quarterly fee on the undrawn portion of an approved revolving credit facility.
explanation: Commitment fees compensate the bank for reserving capital against undrawn facilities. Under IFRS 9, if the facility is expected to be drawn, the fee is deferred and incorporated into the EIR of the loan once drawn.

title: Arrangement / Syndication Fee Income
description: Earning an upfront fee for arranging and underwriting a syndicated loan facility.
explanation: The portion of the arrangement fee related to services for other syndicate banks is recognized immediately. The portion attributable to the arranger's own loan participation is deferred into the EIR over the loan life.

title: Agency Fee — Syndicated Loan Administration
description: Earning an annual fee for administering a syndicated loan on behalf of the lender group.
explanation: The facility agent receives an annual fee for coordinating drawdowns, repayments, waivers, and information distribution among syndicate members. Recognized over the year as services are performed.

title: Collective ECL — Portfolio-Level Provision
description: Calculating and recording the collective ECL provision on the entire performing loan portfolio.
explanation: Collective provisioning covers Stage 1 and Stage 2 loans where individual assessment is impractical. Banks apply PD, LGD, and EAD models to pools of loans grouped by similar credit characteristics.

title: ECL Macro-Economic Overlay Provision
description: Adding a management overlay to model-calculated ECL for current macro-economic conditions.
explanation: IFRS 9 requires forward-looking macro-economic scenarios in ECL models. When models cannot fully capture current conditions (recession, geopolitical stress), management adds a quantified overlay with documented rationale.

title: NPL Portfolio Sold to Debt Collector
description: Selling a portfolio of written-off loans to a third-party debt collection company.
explanation: Written-off loans are off-balance-sheet but tracked in shadow records for recovery. Sale proceeds are pure recovery income. Typical prices are 5-20 cents on the dollar depending on loan age, type, and collateral coverage.

title: Collateral Revaluation — Property Value Decline
description: Increasing ECL provision after a property held as collateral declines in market value.
explanation: Collateral value directly affects LGD in ECL models. When property values fall, LGD increases, requiring a higher provision even if the borrower continues to pay. Annual collateral revaluations are required by the CBJ.

title: Government Guaranteed Loan — Zero ECL
description: Releasing ECL provision on a loan fully guaranteed by the Jordan government.
explanation: Loans guaranteed by sovereign entities attract minimal ECL under IFRS 9. When an enforceable government guarantee is received on a provisioned loan, the provision is reversed to the extent of the guarantee coverage.

title: Branch Staff Monthly Payroll (Gross)
description: Recording the monthly gross payroll for tellers, relationship managers, and branch supervisors.
explanation: Staff costs are the largest operating expense for retail banks. Payroll is allocated between front-office and back-office functions for cost-income ratio analysis and branch profitability reporting.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Bank Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service benefit for Jordanian bank employees.
explanation: Banks with large workforces carry significant EOSB liabilities. Monthly provisioning avoids a large charge when long-serving employees depart and is required under Jordanian labor law.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — Bank Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the 14.25% employer SSC contribution for bank employees.
explanation: SSC contributions are a significant payroll cost for large banks with hundreds of employees. They are part of the total employment cost reported in the staff costs line of the income statement.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the annual income tax on the bank's taxable profit.
explanation: Jordanian banks pay income tax at the applicable corporate rate (currently 35% for banks). Stage 3 loan provisions are generally tax-deductible; Stage 1/2 collective provisions are not, creating significant deferred tax items.

title: Deferred Tax Asset — Collective Loan Provisions
description: Recognizing a deferred tax asset for Stage 1/2 ECL provisions not yet tax-deductible.
explanation: Stage 1 and 2 collective provisions are often only tax-deductible when the loan is specifically impaired. This creates a deductible temporary difference and a DTA — recognizable when future taxable profit is probable.

title: Withholding Tax on Customer Deposit Interest
description: Deducting withholding tax from interest paid to individual depositors and remitting to the ISTD.
explanation: In Jordan, banks withhold income tax on interest paid to individual depositors. The withheld amount is remitted to the ISTD monthly. The effective tax rate depends on the depositor's income tax bracket.

title: CBJ Annual Supervision / Inspection Fee
description: Paying the annual supervision fee charged by the Central Bank of Jordan.
explanation: The CBJ charges annual supervision fees based on asset size. This mandatory cost covers on-site examinations and off-site surveillance. It is an operating expense recognized in the period it covers.

title: Core Banking System — Annual License / Subscription
description: Paying the annual fee for the core banking platform (Temenos, Oracle FLEXCUBE, etc.).
explanation: The core banking system is the most critical IT infrastructure. Annual fees are prepaid and amortized monthly. Core banking modernization is among the largest capital and operating investment in the banking sector.

title: Branch Fit-Out — Leasehold Improvement
description: Capitalizing the cost of fitting out a new branch premises (counters, security, ATMs, fit-out).
explanation: Branch fit-outs are significant capital investments amortized over the shorter of the lease term or useful life. Banks with large branch networks carry hundreds of millions in leasehold improvements on the balance sheet.

title: ATM Machine — Purchase and Depreciation Setup
description: Purchasing ATM machines for branch and off-site deployment.
explanation: ATMs are fixed assets with a useful life of 7-10 years. Their monthly depreciation plus cash replenishment and maintenance must be weighed against ATM fee income and teller cost savings — a key branch economics analysis.

title: Fixed-Line / Broadband Monthly Subscription Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue for residential broadband and fixed-line telephone subscriptions.
explanation: Fixed-line broadband revenue is recognized monthly as the service is delivered. It is accounted for separately from mobile revenue as it has different cost drivers, churn rates, and margin profiles.

title: Fibre-to-the-Home (FTTH) Monthly Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly subscription revenue from a residential FTTH connection.
explanation: FTTH is a premium broadband product requiring significant upfront capex for fibre deployment. Monthly revenue is recognized as connectivity is provided. The high capex-to-revenue ratio makes FTTH a long-payback investment.

title: Enterprise Managed Services Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from a corporate customer for a fully managed network and security service.
explanation: Managed services contracts bundle connectivity, hardware, security monitoring, and helpdesk support. Under IFRS 15, each distinct performance obligation is assessed separately. Long-term contracts may have material contract modifications.

title: Cloud & Hosting Service Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from providing cloud infrastructure and colocation services to business customers.
explanation: Cloud and hosting is the fastest-growing B2B revenue stream for telecoms. Revenue is recognized over the service period. Upfront setup fees may be deferred under IFRS 15 if they relate to future performance obligations.

title: IoT / M2M Connectivity Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly SIM-based connectivity revenue from IoT devices (smart meters, vehicles, sensors).
explanation: IoT revenue is high-volume, low-ARPU (Average Revenue Per User). Millions of connected devices generate reliable recurring revenue. The key metric is the cost per SIM rather than per-subscriber revenue.

title: A2P SMS (Bulk Business Messaging) Revenue
description: Earning revenue from banks, retailers, and government agencies sending OTPs and alerts via SMS.
explanation: Application-to-Person (A2P) SMS revenue comes from businesses sending automated messages. As person-to-person SMS declines, A2P has become a growing revenue stream — especially for OTP, bank notifications, and e-commerce alerts.

title: Premium Rate / Shortcode Revenue (Revenue Share)
description: Recording the telecom's share of revenue from premium rate SMS or voice services.
explanation: The telecom acts as billing agent for premium services. Only the operator's revenue share (net of the content provider's share) is recognized as revenue. The content provider's share is a payable.

title: OTT Content Partnership Revenue Share
description: Earning a share of subscription revenue from bundling a streaming service into mobile plans.
explanation: Telecoms bundle OTT content (streaming, music, games) with mobile plans. Revenue share agreements give the telecom a percentage of subscription revenue for subscribers acquired through their platform.

title: Device Insurance Premium — Monthly Recurring
description: Recognizing the telecom's agent commission for selling device insurance on behalf of an insurer.
explanation: When telecoms sell device insurance as an agent of an insurance company, only the agent commission is revenue — not the full premium. Under IFRS 15, acting as an agent requires net presentation.

title: Handset Installment Plan — In-House Financing
description: The telecom offers 12-month installment payments for a handset without a third-party lender.
explanation: When the telecom provides in-house financing, the full handset revenue is recognized at delivery (control has passed). The receivable is split between current and long-term based on when installments are due.

title: SIM-Only Plan Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from a SIM-only postpaid plan with no handset subsidy.
explanation: SIM-only plans are pure service contracts with no handset component — simpler IFRS 15 treatment with a single performance obligation. Higher margins than bundled plans as there is no handset cost.

title: Family Plan — Multi-Line Bundle Revenue
description: Recognizing monthly revenue from a family plan covering 4 lines at a group discount.
explanation: Multi-line family plans are a retention tool — the group discount creates switching cost. Under IFRS 15, each line is a separate performance obligation allocated the same SSP as individual plans.

title: Contract Downgrade — Revenue Reduction
description: A customer mid-contract downgrades from a premium to a basic plan.
explanation: Under IFRS 15, a plan downgrade is a contract modification that reduces the transaction price. The remaining contract revenue is adjusted prospectively. No catch-up adjustment to past periods is required.

title: Early Termination Fee (ETF) Income
description: Charging a fee when a postpaid customer cancels their contract before the minimum term expires.
explanation: ETFs recover the unrecouped subsidy and acquisition cost when a customer leaves early. Under IFRS 15, the ETF is recognized when charged since the constraint on variable consideration is lifted at that point.

title: Reconnection / Reactivation Fee
description: Charging a fee to reconnect a suspended postpaid or prepaid account.
explanation: Reconnection fees are recognized immediately on reactivation since the performance obligation is the restoration of service — a point-in-time event.

title: SIM Replacement Fee
description: Charging a customer for replacing a lost, stolen, or damaged SIM card.
explanation: SIM replacement fees cover the cost of the physical SIM and administrative processing. Revenue is recognized at point of issuance.

title: Roaming Revenue Adjustment — Interoperator Reconciliation
description: Adjusting roaming revenue after the quarterly reconciliation of CDRs with roaming partners.
explanation: Roaming revenue is initially estimated based on processed CDRs. Quarterly reconciliation with partners may reveal discrepancies requiring revenue adjustments. This is a normal part of the interoperator settlement cycle.

title: Postpaid Receivable — Bad Debt Write-Off
description: Writing off a postpaid customer's outstanding balance after exhausting collection attempts.
explanation: Postpaid bad debt write-offs are applied against the allowance. The expense was already recognized when the provision was set up. Write-off is a balance sheet cleanup entry with no additional P&L impact.

title: Allowance for Doubtful Postpaid Accounts
description: Monthly provision for expected bad debts on the outstanding postpaid receivable portfolio.
explanation: Telecoms provision for expected bad debts on postpaid balances using historical collection rates by aging bucket. This is an IFRS 9 ECL-based estimate updated monthly.

title: Revenue Refund — Customer Complaint Resolution
description: Issuing a credit to a customer's account to resolve a billing complaint.
explanation: Customer credits for complaints reduce recognized revenue. The credit balance is a liability until applied against a future invoice or refunded in cash. Tracking refunds by category helps identify systemic billing issues.

title: Tower Lease — IFRS 16 Interest Expense
description: Recording the monthly interest cost on the lease liability for a tower site.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, each lease payment splits between principal repayment and interest expense. The interest is calculated on the outstanding lease liability using the implicit or incremental borrowing rate.

title: Tower Sale-Leaseback — Derecognition and Gain
description: Selling owned towers to a TowerCo and recognizing the portion of gain relating to rights transferred.
explanation: Tower sale-leasebacks are a major capital recycling strategy for GCC telecoms. Under IFRS 16, the gain recognized is only the portion relating to the rights transferred to the buyer. The leaseback portion of the gain is deferred in the ROU asset.

title: Passive Infrastructure Sharing Income
description: Earning rental income from a competitor sharing space on the telecom's tower sites.
explanation: Tower sharing reduces industry capex and generates incremental income for the host operator. Income is recognized monthly as the space is occupied. This is high-margin revenue with minimal incremental cost.

title: Active RAN Sharing — Cost Split with Partner
description: Recording the cost-sharing payment under a Radio Access Network sharing agreement.
explanation: Active RAN sharing allows two operators to share radio equipment, dramatically reducing network opex. The hosting operator charges the guest operator for their share of network costs.

title: Fibre Cable Installation — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the cost of laying fibre optic cable for a new urban coverage area.
explanation: Fibre cable has a useful life of 20-30 years and is capitalized as PPE. The high upfront cost and long life make fibre a strategic infrastructure investment — the depreciation charge is low relative to the revenue potential.

title: Undersea / International Cable Capacity Purchase
description: Purchasing capacity on an international submarine cable for international bandwidth.
explanation: International cable capacity is purchased as an indefeasible right of use (IRU) for a fixed term. It is capitalized as an intangible asset and amortized over the IRU period — typically 15-25 years.

title: Network Decommissioning Cost
description: Recording the cost of removing and disposing of obsolete 2G/3G network equipment.
explanation: Technology migrations require decommissioning legacy networks. If equipment is not fully depreciated at disposal, the net book value is recognized as a loss. Decommissioning costs (removal, disposal) are expensed.

title: Asset Under Construction (AUC) — Transfer to PPE
description: Transferring a completed network site from Assets Under Construction to operational PPE.
explanation: Network capex is accumulated in AUC during construction. Once a site is commissioned and live, it transfers to PPE and depreciation begins. The AUC balance is a key metric for tracking capital deployment.

title: Capitalized Borrowing Cost — Network Build (IAS 23)
description: Capitalizing interest on loans specifically used to finance a qualifying network construction project.
explanation: Under IAS 23, borrowing costs directly attributable to a qualifying asset must be capitalized. For telecoms building large network infrastructure over extended periods, this can be a significant amount reducing reported interest expense.

title: Handset Outright Purchase — Walk-In Sale
description: Recording the sale of a handset to a customer who pays full price with no contract.
explanation: An outright sale has a single performance obligation — transfer of the handset. Revenue and COGS are recognized simultaneously at point of sale. No deferral or bundle allocation is required.

title: Handset Inventory Purchase from Manufacturer
description: Purchasing a batch of smartphones from a manufacturer for retail sale.
explanation: Handset inventory is a significant working capital item for telecoms. Inventory is capitalized at cost including import duties and freight. The mix of handset models and price points directly affects subsidy costs and gross margin.

title: Handset Inventory Write-Down — Obsolescence
description: Writing down slow-moving handset models when newer devices make them unsellable at cost.
explanation: Handset technology cycles are short (12-18 months). Models not sold before a new launch may require write-down to NRV. This is a key risk for telecoms carrying large handset inventory.

title: Trade-In Device Received — Inventory Recognition
description: Recording a used device received as a trade-in from a customer upgrading their handset.
explanation: Trade-in devices are recognized at their estimated resale value (NRV). The trade-in value reduces the effective selling price of the new device. The used device is then refurbished and resold.

title: Certified Pre-Owned Device Sold
description: Selling a refurbished trade-in device through the telecom's certified pre-owned programme.
explanation: Pre-owned devices generate revenue at a higher margin than new devices since the trade-in cost is low. Revenue and COGS are recognized at the point of sale.

title: Device Accessories Sold (Cases, Chargers)
description: Recording the sale of phone accessories at the telecom retail store.
explanation: Accessories are high-margin, low-ticket items sold at the point of handset purchase or separately. Revenue is recognized at point of sale — a single, simple performance obligation.

title: Warranty Provision on Handsets Sold
description: Accruing a provision for expected warranty claims on handsets sold with a 12-month manufacturer warranty pass-through.
explanation: Where the telecom provides its own warranty (not just passing through the manufacturer's), IAS 37 requires a provision for expected claims based on historical failure rates. The provision is matched to the period of sale.

title: Interoperator Debt — Aged Receivable Provision
description: Provisioning for overdue interoperator receivables where settlement is uncertain.
explanation: Interoperator receivables can be disputed and age significantly. IFRS 9 ECL provisioning applies to these commercial receivables. Disputed amounts are assessed for probability-weighted recovery.

title: Interoperator Settlement — Net Monthly Payment
description: Settling the net monthly interoperator position — paying or receiving the balance after netting termination income against origination costs.
explanation: Operators settle on a net basis monthly. If interconnect payable exceeds receivable, a net cash payment is made. The gross income and expense were already recognized when traffic was carried.

title: Transit Traffic Income
description: Earning income for carrying and terminating traffic that originates and terminates on other operators' networks.
explanation: Transit income is earned for routing calls between two other networks. The operator provides the 'middle leg' of an international call. Margins are low but volume can be significant.

title: International Gateway Access Fee
description: Charging operators and ISPs for access to the telecom's international gateway infrastructure.
explanation: Telecoms with international gateway infrastructure charge other operators for accessing it. This infrastructure income is recurring and high-margin as the gateway capex is already sunk.

title: MVNO Wholesale Billing to Hosted Operator
description: Billing a Mobile Virtual Network Operator (MVNO) for network usage at wholesale rates.
explanation: MVNOs lease network capacity from the host operator and resell it under their own brand. The host charges wholesale rates covering voice, data, and SMS usage. This is a low-cost revenue stream as the network cost is already incurred.

title: Technical Staff (Network Engineers) Payroll
description: Recording monthly gross salaries for network engineers, systems administrators, and IT staff.
explanation: Technical staff costs are a significant operating expense capitalized or expensed depending on whether the work relates to network construction (capitalize) or maintenance (expense).

title: Commercial / Sales Staff Payroll
description: Recording monthly salaries for sales, marketing, customer service, and retail channel staff.
explanation: Commercial staff costs are a selling and distribution expense. For retail telecoms, this includes store staff, call centre agents, and field sales teams — typically the second largest cost after network opex.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — Telecom Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing the end-of-service benefit for Jordanian telecom employees.
explanation: Large telecoms with hundreds or thousands of employees carry material EOSB liabilities. Monthly provisioning is required under Jordanian labor law to avoid large charges at departure.

title: SSC Employer Contribution — Telecom Staff (Jordan)
description: Recording the 14.25% employer SSC contribution for Jordanian telecom employees.
explanation: SSC is calculated on gross salaries. For a large telecom with 400K monthly payroll, the employer SSC is JOD 56,475. It is a mandatory operating cost recognized in the period the salary is earned.

title: Annual Staff Bonus Accrual
description: Accruing the year-end performance bonus for all eligible telecom employees.
explanation: Year-end bonuses are accrued in the performance period and paid in Q1 of the following year. Accrual ensures the expense is matched to the year the performance was delivered.

title: Sales Commission — Retail Channel Staff
description: Paying monthly commission to retail store staff based on postpaid activations and handset sales targets.
explanation: Retail commissions are variable selling costs linked to sales performance. They are expensed in the period earned. These are separate from dealer commissions paid to third-party distribution channels.

title: Expat Staff Housing Allowance
description: Paying monthly housing allowances for expatriate executive or technical staff.
explanation: Housing allowances for expat staff are part of the employment cost. They are a cash expense disclosed separately in staff cost notes. For senior executives, housing can be a significant component of total compensation.

title: Staff Share Option Scheme — IFRS 2 Expense
description: Recording the monthly IFRS 2 charge for employee share options granted to senior management.
explanation: Under IFRS 2, share options granted to employees are recognized as an expense over the vesting period. The fair value at grant date is spread across the vesting period regardless of subsequent share price movements.

title: Tower Site Rent — Cash Lease Payment
description: Recording the actual monthly cash payment for a tower site lease under IFRS 16.
explanation: Under IFRS 16, the cash lease payment splits between reducing the lease liability (principal) and settling the interest accrual. The P&L impact comes from depreciation of the ROU asset and interest on the lease liability — not the cash payment.

title: Base Station Energy / Electricity Cost
description: Recording the monthly electricity cost for running base transceiver stations and tower equipment.
explanation: Energy is one of the largest network operating costs for mobile telecoms. Base stations run 24/7 and power consumption is significant. Energy efficiency (solar, battery backup) is a major OPEX reduction initiative in the GCC.

title: Network Operations Centre (NOC) Maintenance
description: Monthly cost of NOC operations including vendor support contracts and monitoring tools.
explanation: NOC operations are critical for network uptime and quality. Vendor support contracts (Ericsson, Nokia, Huawei) are a major component of network opex. These are expensed as incurred.

title: BSS / OSS IT Systems — Annual Subscription
description: Annual subscription for Business Support Systems (billing, CRM) and Operations Support Systems.
explanation: BSS/OSS platforms are the operational backbone of a telecom — handling billing, provisioning, network management, and customer care. Annual subscriptions are prepaid and amortized monthly at JOD 300,000.

title: Customer Care Centre Outsourcing Cost
description: Monthly fee paid to a BPO company managing inbound customer service calls.
explanation: Many telecoms outsource customer care to BPO companies to reduce fixed costs and enable scalability. The monthly fee is a direct selling expense tracked against customer satisfaction metrics.

title: Marketing & Brand Campaign Cost
description: Recording the cost of a major above-the-line advertising campaign.
explanation: Brand advertising is expensed as incurred. Telecom marketing spend is substantial and tracked by campaign, channel, and objective. TV, digital, and outdoor are the primary media for GCC telecoms.

title: Subscriber Acquisition Cost — Non-Capitalized (Prepaid)
description: Expensing the distribution cost of prepaid SIM activation through dealers — not meeting IFRS 15 capitalization criteria.
explanation: Prepaid SIM distribution costs are expensed immediately since there is no long-term contract from which to recover them. Only incremental costs to obtain contracts with expected recovery are capitalized under IFRS 15.

title: Telecom Retail Shop — Monthly Rent
description: Monthly rental expense for a branded telecom retail store in a shopping mall.
explanation: Retail stores are a key customer acquisition and service channel for telecoms. Under IFRS 16, most retail store leases are on-balance-sheet as ROU assets. This entry shows the monthly cash release from prepaid rent where applicable.

title: Bond / Sukuk Issuance — Proceeds
description: A telecom issuing a 5-year bond or Sukuk to fund network capex.
explanation: Bond proceeds are recognized as a liability at the net proceeds received. Issuance costs are deducted from the carrying amount under IFRS 9 and amortized over the bond's life using the EIR method.

title: Bond Interest Expense — Monthly Accrual
description: Accruing monthly interest on outstanding bonds using the effective interest rate method.
explanation: A JOD 250M bond at 5% p.a. accrues JOD 1,041,667/month. The EIR method means the actual interest expense includes amortization of issuance costs — slightly higher than the nominal coupon.

title: Bond Issuance Cost Amortization (EIR Method)
description: Amortizing the bond issuance costs into interest expense each month.
explanation: JOD 2M issuance costs on a 5-year bond amortize at JOD 33,333/month. This additional interest expense ensures the carrying amount of the bond converges to its nominal value at maturity.

title: Bank Term Loan Drawdown
description: Drawing down a JOD 100 million bank term loan for network infrastructure investment.
explanation: Bank loans are the most common financing instrument for GCC telecoms alongside bonds. The loan proceeds are used for network capex. Interest is accrued monthly using the agreed rate.

title: Bank Loan Interest — Monthly Accrual
description: Accruing monthly interest on the outstanding bank term loan balance.
explanation: A JOD 100M loan at 5% p.a. accrues JOD 416,667/month. The monthly accrual is settled on the quarterly interest payment date.

title: IFRS 16 Lease Liability — Total Monthly Interest
description: Recording the aggregate monthly interest expense on all active tower and retail site lease liabilities.
explanation: Large telecoms with thousands of tower and retail site leases carry substantial IFRS 16 lease liabilities. The aggregate monthly interest is a significant expense line — distinct from network depreciation and network opex.

title: FX Gain on USD Equipment Payable
description: Recording an FX gain when the JOD strengthens between invoice date and payment for USD-priced network equipment.
explanation: Network equipment from Ericsson, Nokia, and Huawei is typically priced in USD or EUR. When the JOD strengthens between invoice and payment, the telecom pays less in local currency — an FX gain.

title: FX Loss on USD Equipment Payable
description: Recording an FX loss when the JOD weakens between invoice date and payment for USD equipment.
explanation: A weakening JOD increases the local currency cost of USD-denominated equipment purchases. This FX loss flows through the income statement and is disclosed in finance costs.

title: Dividend Declared to Shareholders
description: The board approves a cash dividend distribution to shareholders.
explanation: The dividend creates a liability at board declaration date. GCC telecoms are known for high dividend payout ratios given their stable cash generation. WHT on dividends paid to non-resident shareholders applies in many jurisdictions.

title: Share Buyback — Treasury Shares
description: The telecom repurchases its own listed shares in the open market.
explanation: Share buybacks are an alternative to dividends for returning capital to shareholders. Treasury shares are held at cost as a contra-equity item — they reduce total equity. They can be cancelled or reissued later.

title: Corporate Income Tax Provision
description: Accruing the annual income tax on the telecom's taxable profit.
explanation: Telecoms pay corporate income tax on taxable profit. Significant timing differences arise between accounting and taxable profit due to accelerated depreciation on network assets, spectrum amortization, and IFRS 16 lease treatment.

title: Deferred Tax — Spectrum License Timing Difference
description: Recording the deferred tax liability from the difference between accounting amortization and tax depreciation on the spectrum license.
explanation: Tax authorities may allow accelerated deduction of spectrum costs while accounting amortizes them over the license period. The temporary difference creates a deferred tax liability — the tax benefit taken early must be 'given back' later.

title: Deferred Tax — IFRS 16 ROU Asset vs Lease Liability
description: Recording the net deferred tax position from IFRS 16 recognition of ROU assets and lease liabilities.

entries.account: Deferred Tax Asset (Lease Liability > ROU Asset)

explanation: IFRS 16 creates two temporary differences: the ROU asset (taxable) and the lease liability (deductible). If lease liabilities exceed ROU asset values, the net position is a DTA. Tax authorities may treat lease payments as fully deductible rental expense.

title: VAT on B2C Telecom Services (Jordan 16%)
description: Recording 16% General Sales Tax on postpaid and prepaid subscriber revenue in Jordan.
explanation: Telecom services in Jordan attract 16% General Sales Tax. Prices are typically quoted inclusive of tax. Revenue is always recognized net of the tax collected on behalf of the government.

title: VAT Input on Network Equipment Purchases
description: Recovering input VAT paid on locally purchased network equipment and installation services.
explanation: VAT on capital equipment purchases is a recoverable input tax — it reduces the net VAT payable to the ISTD and is not capitalized into the asset cost.

title: WHT on Dividend Paid to Foreign Shareholders
description: Withholding tax deducted from dividend payments to non-resident shareholders.
explanation: Jordan imposes WHT on dividends paid to foreign shareholders. The applicable rate depends on double taxation treaties. The telecom deducts and remits the WHT, paying the net dividend to the foreign investor.

title: Transfer Pricing Adjustment — Intercompany Charges
description: Recording a management fee charged by the parent company to the local operating subsidiary.
explanation: Transfer pricing governs intercompany charges between related entities. Management fees, royalties, and shared services are common. ISTD requires these to be at arm's length with supporting documentation.

title: Zakat Provision — GCC Telecom Operations (KSA)
description: Accruing Zakat for a Saudi telecom subsidiary or operation.
explanation: Telecom companies in Saudi Arabia are subject to Zakat on their Saudi-owned capital base. The Zakat base broadly includes equity and retained earnings adjusted per ZATCA's methodology — typically 2.5% of the adjusted base.

title: Geological & Geophysical (G&G) Studies — Expensed
description: Expensing geological mapping and regional basin studies before an exploration license is secured.
explanation: Pre-license G&G studies that do not relate to a specific exploration area with rights are expensed as incurred. They cannot be capitalized as E&E assets under IFRS 6 because no exploration right yet exists.

title: Exploration License Acquisition Cost Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the signature bonus and legal costs paid to acquire an exploration permit/block.
explanation: The cost of acquiring the legal right to explore is directly attributable to finding oil and gas resources. Under IFRS 6, it is capitalized as an E&E asset from the date the license is granted.

title: Exploration License — Annual Rental Payment
description: Paying the annual license rental to the host government to maintain the exploration permit in good standing.
explanation: Annual license rentals are paid to maintain the exploration right. They are capitalized to the E&E asset as a cost of holding the license. The full E&E balance is tested for impairment at the end of each period.

title: Exploratory Well — Suspended Pending Determination
description: Keeping an exploratory well's costs capitalized while the company assesses whether reserves are commercial.
explanation: Under IFRS 6, exploratory well costs can remain capitalized beyond one year if (a) reserves have been found and (b) management is actively assessing commerciality. A suspended well over 12 months triggers enhanced disclosure requirements.

title: Suspended Well — Written Off After Determination
description: Writing off a previously suspended exploratory well after management decides not to develop.
explanation: Once management decides a suspended well is non-commercial or there is insufficient justification to continue, the E&E asset is written off immediately. Delayed write-offs are an audit risk area in the oil and gas sector.

title: Stratigraphic Test Well — Cost Treatment
description: Recording a well drilled purely to obtain geological information with no production intent.
explanation: Stratigraphic test wells drilled solely for geological data with no drilling intention beyond that purpose are expensed under successful efforts accounting. Under full cost accounting, they are capitalized to the cost pool.

title: Exploration Overhead Allocation to E&E Asset
description: Allocating a portion of exploration department salaries and overhead to the E&E asset.
explanation: Only directly attributable exploration overhead can be capitalized to E&E assets. General administrative overhead cannot. The allocation basis (time records, project codes) must be defensible to auditors.

title: E&E Farm-Out — Partial Interest Sold Pre-Discovery
description: Selling a 40% working interest in an exploration block before any discovery is made.
explanation: Pre-discovery farm-outs monetize acreage value before the drilling risk is resolved. The gain is the consideration less the proportional book value of the E&E asset. Some operators use a full-cost pool method that eliminates the gain.

title: Offshore Platform / Production Facility — Construction
description: Capitalizing construction costs for a fixed offshore production platform.
explanation: Production platforms are the most capital-intensive assets in offshore E&P. Costs accumulate in Assets Under Construction and transfer to PPE when the facility is commissioned. The ARO for eventual decommissioning is recognized simultaneously.

title: Subsea Infrastructure Capitalized
description: Capitalizing subsea pipelines, wellheads, manifolds, and umbilicals connecting wells to the platform.
explanation: Subsea infrastructure connects the reservoir to surface processing. It is a development cost capitalized under the field's development plan. Depletion follows UOP based on the proven developed reserves.

title: Gas Processing Plant Construction — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the cost of building a gas treatment and compression facility.
explanation: Gas processing plants treat raw gas for pipeline or LNG export. They are long-lived assets (30+ years) depreciated on a straight-line or UOP basis. Capacity expansions are capitalized; routine maintenance is expensed.

title: Water / Gas Injection Well — Capitalized
description: Capitalizing the cost of drilling and completing an injection well for pressure maintenance.
explanation: Injection wells are development assets — they maintain reservoir pressure to maximize production from the field. Their cost is capitalized and depleted using UOP over the associated producing reserves.

title: Development Borrowing Cost Capitalized (IAS 23)
description: Capitalizing interest on a project finance facility specifically funding a qualifying development asset.
explanation: Under IAS 23, borrowing costs directly attributable to a qualifying development asset are capitalized. For large offshore projects funded by project finance, this can be a substantial addition to the asset cost.

title: Facilities Turnaround / Shutdown — Major Maintenance
description: Recording the cost of a planned major maintenance turnaround on a production facility.
explanation: Major turnarounds (typically every 4-6 years) involve complete shutdown for inspection and refurbishment. Under component accounting (IAS 16), turnaround costs are capitalized as a separate component and depreciated over the interval to the next turnaround.

title: Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) — Cost Treatment
description: Recording the cost of a chemical EOR program injecting surfactants to increase oil recovery.
explanation: EOR costs are capitalized when the injection program can be demonstrated to increase total recoverable reserves. If the expenditure only maintains current production rates, it is expensed as a lifting cost.

title: DD&A — Facilities and Equipment (Units of Production)
description: Monthly depletion, depreciation, and amortization of all producing assets using the UOP method.
explanation: All capitalized development costs (platforms, pipelines, processing) are depleted using UOP. The rate is: (Net Book Value / Remaining Proved Developed Reserves) × Production Volume. High production months yield higher DD&A.

title: DD&A Rate Revision — Reserve Estimate Change
description: Revising the DD&A rate prospectively after an upward revision to proved developed reserves.
explanation: Reserve estimate changes are treated as changes in accounting estimates (IAS 8 — prospective treatment). An upward revision lowers the future DD&A rate, improving profitability. A downward revision increases the rate — a major earnings impact.

title: Gas Processing Plant — Straight-Line Depreciation
description: Depreciating a gas plant on a straight-line basis over its 30-year useful life.
explanation: When a gas plant serves multiple fields with different reserve lives, straight-line depreciation over its economic life is more appropriate than UOP. An JOD 85M plant over 30 years depreciates at JOD 236,111/month.

title: Impairment Test — Producing Field CGU (IAS 36)
description: Recording an impairment charge when the recoverable amount of a producing field falls below book value.
explanation: Under IAS 36, each producing field is a Cash Generating Unit (CGU). When oil prices fall or reserves are revised down, the recoverable amount (higher of VIU and FVLCTS) may fall below the carrying amount, triggering impairment.

title: Impairment Reversal — Proved Reserves Upgrade
description: Reversing part of a prior impairment charge following a significant reserve upgrade.
explanation: Under IAS 36, impairment reversals are permitted when the recoverable amount subsequently increases. The reversal cannot exceed the original impairment amount. This is triggered by reserve upgrades, higher oil prices, or improved development plans.

title: Gas Flaring Cost and Regulatory Penalty
description: Recording the cost of flaring associated gas and any regulatory fine for exceeding the flaring limit.
explanation: Gas flaring is both an operating expense (lost resource value) and a potential regulatory liability. Many GCC and MENA regulators are tightening flaring limits. Penalties are expensed and disclosed separately.

title: Produced Water Treatment & Disposal
description: Recording the cost of treating and disposing of water produced alongside oil and gas.
explanation: Produced water volumes often exceed oil production volumes in mature fields. Treatment and disposal costs are a significant operating expense (Lease Operating Expense) that increases as a field matures.

title: Crude Oil Quality Adjustment — Price Differential
description: Recording a price discount on heavy, high-sulphur crude relative to the Brent benchmark.
explanation: Crude oil prices are quoted relative to benchmark crudes (Brent, WTI, Dubai). Heavy or sour crudes trade at a discount that is contractually defined. The differential is presented as a reduction to revenue.

title: Oil Stock Gain / Loss — Tank Calibration Adjustment
description: Adjusting for the difference between metered volumes and physical tank dip measurements.
explanation: Tank calibration, thermal expansion, and measurement uncertainty create differences between fiscal meters and physical stock counts. Material variances are investigated; minor differences are recognized as stock gains or losses.

title: LNG Liquefaction Cost
description: Recording the cost of liquefying natural gas at the LNG train for export.
explanation: LNG liquefaction is an energy-intensive process. The cost is a direct production expense. LNG producers with tolling arrangements (where a third party liquefies the gas) record a tolling fee rather than the underlying costs.

title: LNG Cargo Sale — Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue from the sale of an LNG cargo to an Asian buyer.
explanation: LNG cargo sales are recognized when control passes to the buyer — typically at the ship's rail (FOB) or upon delivery (DES). Cargos are often sold under long-term SPAs (Sale and Purchase Agreements) with oil-linked price formulas.

title: Condensate Sale — Revenue Recognition
description: Recognizing revenue from selling condensate (light liquid hydrocarbons) separated from gas.
explanation: Condensate is a high-value by-product that requires separate metering and pricing. It is typically priced at a premium to crude oil and reported as a distinct revenue stream from natural gas.

title: NGL Separation & Sale
description: Recording revenue from natural gas liquids (ethane, propane, butane) extracted from raw gas.
explanation: NGLs are separated at a gas processing plant and sold as individual components. NGL extraction improves the economics of gas production. Revenue is allocated to each NGL product based on its market price.

title: Crude Oil Transportation — Tanker Hire
description: Paying a tanker hire charge to transport crude from the loading terminal to the customer's port.
explanation: Transportation costs are expensed as incurred. Whether classified as LOE or a reduction to revenue (netback pricing) depends on the commercial terms — CIF sales include transport in the revenue; FOB sales show it as a separate expense.

title: Offshore Installation Insurance Premium
description: Annual insurance premium for an offshore production platform covering property damage and well control.
explanation: Offshore insurance (energy package, well control, liability) is a significant operating cost. Annual premiums are prepaid assets amortized monthly. Offshore insurance is a specialized market dominated by Lloyd's of London syndicates.

title: Oil Price Formula — Retroactive Adjustment
description: Adjusting revenue when the final price is calculated retroactively based on a monthly average index.
explanation: Many oil sale agreements use a provisional invoice price with final pricing based on a monthly average. The retroactive adjustment reconciles the provisional and final prices. IFRS 15 constrains recognition of variable consideration until the uncertainty is resolved.

title: Make-Up Gas Delivery — Take-or-Pay Fulfillment
description: Delivering make-up gas to a buyer who previously paid but did not take their contracted volumes.
explanation: When previously deferred take-or-pay revenue is finally earned by delivering the gas, the liability is released to revenue. The make-up period may span several years depending on the gas sales agreement terms.

title: Capacity Reservation Fee Income
description: Earning a fee from a third party reserving pipeline capacity regardless of actual throughput.
explanation: Capacity reservation fees provide stable, predictable income for pipeline and processing owners. They are recognized over the reservation period regardless of whether the capacity is used.

title: Pipeline Tariff — Annual Escalation Clause
description: Applying the annual CPI-linked escalation to the pipeline tariff charged to shippers.
explanation: Long-term gas and pipeline agreements typically include annual escalation clauses linked to CPI or producer price indices. The tariff increase is applied prospectively from the contract anniversary date.

title: Force Majeure — Revenue Suspension
description: Suspending revenue recognition during a force majeure event (natural disaster, conflict) that halts production.
explanation: Force majeure events suspend contractual obligations. Revenue that cannot be earned due to force majeure is reversed if it was previously accrued. Insurance claims may partially offset the lost revenue.

title: Crude Blending Revenue
description: Earning a premium by blending heavy and light crude streams to produce a marketable blend grade.
explanation: Blending adds value by creating a product grade with broader market appeal. The blending margin (revenue less component costs) is the economic benefit. This is a common practice for companies managing multiple reservoir qualities.

title: Non-Operator — Share of JV Development Capex
description: Recording the non-operator's proportional share of development capex based on the JV billing statement.
explanation: When the non-operator receives the JV billing statement, the previously paid cash call advance is reclassified to the appropriate asset (PPE, E&E, or expense) based on the nature of each cost line in the statement.

title: JV Audit Adjustment — Prior Period Cost Correction
description: Recording a prior period adjustment following the annual JV audit that identified a misallocation.
explanation: Annual JV audits often identify prior period cost misallocations. If material, these are accounted for as prior period errors per IAS 8. If immaterial, they are recorded in the current period as an expense adjustment.

title: JV Partner Default — Non-Consent Penalty
description: Recording the penalty uplift when a JV partner elects not to participate in a well, triggering a non-consent penalty.
explanation: JOAs include non-consent provisions where a partner who elects not to fund a well forfeits a contractually defined penalty (typically 200-500% of their share). The consenting partners recover this uplift before the non-consenting partner receives any production.

title: JV Carried Interest — Funding on Behalf of Carry Partner
description: Recording the carrying of a partner's share of exploration costs in exchange for a promoted interest.
explanation: In a carry arrangement, one party funds another's share in exchange for a larger stake in production economics. The carrier capitalizes its own share to E&E and records a receivable for the carried amounts — recoverable from future production.

title: Technical Assistance Fee to Operator
description: Non-operator paying a technical services fee to the operator for managing the JV.
explanation: Operators typically charge a technical or management fee to the JV for their services. This is part of the non-operator's share of JV overhead. It is expensed unless it relates directly to a capitalized asset.

title: JV Cash Call Surplus — Returned to Partners
description: Recording the return of excess cash call funds after a budget underspend.
explanation: When actual JV expenditure is less than the cash call, the operator returns the surplus. The non-operator reduces their advance receivable. No P&L impact — it is a balance sheet adjustment only.

title: Farm-Out — Carry Obligation Fulfilled
description: Recording when the carry obligation (funding the partner's share) is satisfied and the receivable is recovered from production.
explanation: When production begins, the carrier deducts the carry amount from the carry partner's production entitlement. The carry receivable is reduced and the recovered amount flows to revenue — it is effectively a pre-payment of the carry partner's future production.

title: Refinery — Crude Oil Intake (Feedstock Purchase)
description: Recording the purchase of crude oil as feedstock for refinery processing.
explanation: The refinery purchases crude at market prices. Crude is held in storage as inventory until processed. FIFO or weighted average cost methods are typically used for crude inventory valuation.

title: Refined Product Inventory — Gasoline and Diesel
description: Transferring refined products from the refinery production process into finished goods inventory.
explanation: Refinery output transfers from WIP to finished goods at production cost. Yields are measured by volume and quality testing. The refining margin (product revenue less crude cost plus processing cost) is the key profitability metric.

title: Refinery Processing Cost — Operating Expense
description: Recording the monthly refinery operating costs: energy, catalysts, maintenance, and labour.
explanation: Refinery operating costs are production expenses — they go through WIP inventory and are ultimately part of the cost of finished products. Energy (catalytic cracking, distillation) is typically the largest single cost.

title: Refining Margin — Product Sale vs Crude Cost
description: Recording the sale of gasoline and diesel and recognizing the refining margin.
explanation: The refining margin (crack spread) is the difference between product revenue and feedstock cost. It varies with supply/demand for specific products and is the primary performance metric for downstream operations.

title: Pipeline Ship-or-Pay — Minimum Revenue Recognized
description: Recognizing minimum ship-or-pay revenue when a shipper fails to use their booked capacity.
explanation: Ship-or-pay contracts guarantee minimum revenue for pipeline operators. The payment is deferred if the shipper has a make-up right to use capacity later. If no make-up right exists, revenue is recognized immediately.

title: Storage Terminal Fee Income
description: Earning monthly storage fees from third parties storing oil products in the terminal.
explanation: Storage terminals earn fee-based income from third-party throughput and storage. This is low-risk, stable revenue compared to upstream production — making midstream assets increasingly valued by infrastructure investors.

title: LPG Bottling and Distribution Cost
description: Recording the cost of bottling LPG into cylinders and distributing to retail customers.
explanation: LPG distribution is a downstream retail business. Bottling, cylinder management, and last-mile delivery are the major costs. This is particularly important in Jordan and GCC where household LPG usage is significant.

title: Environmental Remediation Provision (IAS 37)
description: Recognizing a provision for contaminated soil remediation at an onshore production site.
explanation: When contamination exists and remediation is legally or constructively obligatory, a provision is recognized under IAS 37. The estimate must be the best estimate of the expenditure required. It is separate from the ARO (which covers planned end-of-life decommissioning).

title: Environmental Remediation — Actual Expenditure
description: Recording actual remediation spending against the previously established provision.
explanation: As remediation work is carried out, the provision is drawn down. If actual costs exceed the provision, additional expense is recognized. If below, a provision release reduces the expense.

title: Carbon Credit Purchased
description: Purchasing voluntary carbon credits to offset the company's Scope 1 and 2 emissions.
explanation: Carbon credits are intangible assets held for the purpose of surrendering against emissions obligations. They are carried at cost or NRV. As sustainability reporting becomes mandatory, carbon credit accounting is an increasingly important area.

title: Carbon Credit Surrendered (Obligation Met)
description: Surrendering carbon credits to meet a regulatory or voluntary emissions obligation.
explanation: When carbon credits are surrendered to a regulatory authority or carbon registry, the obligation is discharged and the intangible asset is derecognized. The provision set up at year-end for the emissions obligation is cleared.

title: HSE Training & Compliance Cost
description: Recording the cost of mandatory health, safety, and environment training programs.
explanation: HSE training is a mandatory regulatory requirement for oil and gas operations. It is classified as a direct operating expense. Safety performance is a key KPI for operators — poor HSE records increase insurance costs and regulatory scrutiny.

title: Community Investment / Social Obligation
description: Recording mandatory or voluntary social investment spending required under the concession agreement.
explanation: Many concession agreements in MENA and GCC require operators to invest in local communities — schools, clinics, roads. These are expensed as a cost of operating the concession. Social license obligations are increasingly material.

title: Oil Spill Cleanup Cost
description: Recording emergency costs to contain and clean up an oil spill from a pipeline rupture.
explanation: Oil spill costs are expensed immediately. Insurance recovery is recognized separately when virtually certain. Gross presentation (expense and recovery disclosed separately) provides transparency. Regulatory fines are not covered by insurance.

title: Expatriate Staff Payroll — Operations
description: Recording monthly gross salaries for expatriate drilling engineers, geologists, and production staff.
explanation: Expatriate staff in oil and gas operations are a major cost, particularly in exploration and early-stage production. Their salaries, including housing and allowances, are typically classified as LOE or capitalized if directly related to construction.

title: Local Staff Payroll and SSC (Jordan)
description: Recording monthly payroll and SSC for Jordanian national employees in oil operations.
explanation: Jordanian nationals employed in oil operations are subject to standard Jordanian labor law, including SSC obligations. Localization programs in GCC countries require increasing proportions of national staff.

title: EOSB Monthly Provision — O&G Staff (Jordan)
description: Accruing end-of-service benefits for Jordanian employees in oil and gas operations.
explanation: EOSB accrual is required under Jordanian labor law. For staff working on capitalized projects, the EOSB cost is capitalized proportionally to the project; for operational staff, it is an operating expense.

title: Expat Housing and Relocation Cost
description: Paying for expatriate staff accommodation and one-time relocation costs.
explanation: Expat housing in remote oil operations can be a significant cost. Permanent camp facilities for offshore workers are usually capitalized; temporary accommodation during a specific project is expensed.

title: Remote Operations — Camp / Accommodation Cost
description: Monthly cost of running accommodation camp facilities for workers at a remote onshore oil field.
explanation: Remote oil field operations require full accommodation, catering, and welfare facilities. These are a direct LOE cost. The cost per man-day is a key efficiency metric for field operations.

title: Helicopter and Offshore Logistics Cost
description: Monthly cost of helicopter crew changes and supply vessel logistics to an offshore platform.
explanation: Offshore operations depend entirely on helicopters and supply vessels. These are major recurring LOE costs. In Jordan's nascent offshore sector or for GCC operations, this is one of the largest operational cost items.

title: Reserve-Based Lending (RBL) — Facility Drawdown
description: Drawing down a reserve-based lending facility to fund development drilling.
explanation: Reserve-based lending is the primary debt financing instrument for E&P companies. The borrowing base is determined by the NPV of proved reserves and is redetermined semi-annually. Unlike corporate loans, RBL repayment is tied to production cash flows.

title: RBL — Semi-Annual Borrowing Base Redetermination
description: Reducing the RBL borrowing base after a downward reserve revision forces partial repayment.
explanation: If the borrowing base falls below the outstanding RBL balance at redetermination, the company must repay the shortfall within a defined cure period. This is a key liquidity risk for leveraged E&P companies.

title: Oil Price Collar — Put Option Premium Paid
description: Paying the upfront premium for a put option protecting against oil prices falling below a floor.
explanation: A price collar combines a put option (price floor protection) with a sold call (cap on upside). The put premium is paid upfront and recognized as a derivative asset. The collar provides budget certainty for RBL debt service.

title: Oil Price Collar — Fair Value Movement
description: Revaluing the oil price collar at month-end as oil prices move.
explanation: As oil prices fall, the put option gains value, protecting revenue. For an effective cash flow hedge, the gain is recorded in OCI and recycled to revenue when the hedged production is sold — offsetting the lower oil price received.

title: FX Loss on USD-Denominated Operating Costs
description: Recording FX losses when local currency weakens and USD-priced services cost more.
explanation: Oil and gas services (drilling, seismic, aviation) are predominantly priced in USD. When the local currency weakens between invoice and payment, the company records an FX loss. Hedging USD payables is an important treasury function.

title: Project Finance — Equity Bridge Loan
description: Drawing an equity bridge loan to fund the equity portion of a project before long-term financing closes.
explanation: Equity bridge loans are temporary financing used during project construction before long-term project finance is fully drawn. They are classified as current liabilities and repaid when the long-term financing is in place.

title: E&P Subsidiary Dividend to Parent Company
description: An oil-producing subsidiary declares and pays a dividend to its parent holding company.
explanation: Cash repatriation from oil-producing subsidiaries to parent companies is a key cash flow event. Withholding taxes, currency restrictions, and loan covenants may limit the timing and amount of dividends.

title: Petroleum Income Tax — Quarterly Installment Payment
description: Making a quarterly advance tax payment based on estimated annual petroleum income tax.
explanation: Petroleum income tax is often paid in quarterly installments based on estimated annual profits. The installments are prepaid assets offset against the annual tax provision. Under- or over-payments are settled at year-end.

title: Deferred Tax — ARO Liability
description: Recognizing a deferred tax asset on the ARO liability where the decommissioning expenditure is tax-deductible when incurred.
explanation: The ARO liability creates an accounting expense (accretion) but no current tax deduction — the deduction only comes when decommissioning occurs. This deductible temporary difference creates a DTA recognized over the asset's producing life.

title: Deferred Tax — Development Capex (Accelerated Tax Depreciation)
description: Recording the deferred tax liability where accelerated tax depreciation on development assets exceeds accounting DD&A.
explanation: Many petroleum fiscal regimes allow 100% first-year capital allowance on development spending. Where accounting DD&A is spread over reserves life, the faster tax deduction creates a taxable temporary difference and DTL.

title: Tax Loss Carryforward — Deferred Tax Asset
description: Recognizing a DTA for exploration-phase tax losses expected to be utilized against future production profits.
explanation: E&P companies generate large tax losses during exploration and development phases. When there is convincing evidence that future taxable profits will be sufficient to absorb these losses, a DTA is recognized. First oil events often trigger DTA recognition.

title: Withholding Tax on Service Contractor Payments
description: Withholding tax on payments to foreign drilling and oilfield service companies.
explanation: Payments to foreign oilfield service companies (Schlumberger, Halliburton, Baker Hughes) are typically subject to WHT. The applicable rate depends on the host country's tax law and any relevant double taxation treaty.

title: Customs Duty on Imported Drilling Equipment
description: Capitalizing customs duties and import taxes on a drilling rig imported for exploration.
explanation: Import duties on drilling equipment form part of the asset's cost under IAS 2/IAS 16. Some concession agreements include customs duty exemptions for petroleum operations — reducing the effective cost of imported equipment.

title: VAT Refund Claim on Exploration Costs
description: Filing a VAT refund claim for input tax paid on goods and services used in exploration.
explanation: Many countries exempt petroleum activities from VAT or allow full input tax recovery. The refund claim is filed periodically. In Jordan, the ISTD processes VAT refunds for qualifying petroleum operations.

title: Government Royalty in Kind (RIK)
description: Recording the government taking its royalty share in physical oil rather than cash.

entries.account: Crude Oil Inventory (Reduced by RIK Volume)

explanation: Some governments elect to take their royalty as physical oil lifted from the field. The RIK is measured at fair value on the lifting date and reduces the company's inventory. Revenue is not recognized on the government's share.
